topics.title: Physiology and Bio-Electric Concepts: Cell and Their Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEETVT615 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physiology and Bio-Electric Concepts: Cell and Their Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of a Cell

A **cell** is the fundamental structural, functional, and biological unit of all living organisms. It is the smallest entity capable of carrying out the processes characteristic of life: metabolism, growth, reproduction, response to stimuli, and homeostasis. From a **biomedical engineering perspective**, the cell is an electrochemical system — a lipid-bilayer membrane separating two ionic solutions of differing composition, across which bio-electric potentials are established and propagated.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the study of the cell is foundational to understanding how transducers interface with biological tissue, how ECG/EEG/EMG signals originate, and how therapeutic devices (pacemakers, defibrillators, neural stimulators) interact with excitable tissue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The cell is treated as the *basic bio-electric unit*. Mastery of membrane transport, resting potential, and action potential is mandatory for Module 1, Module 2 (Electrodes and Transducers), and Module 4 (Therapeutic Equipment).

### 1.2 Cell Theory — The Three Tenets

1. **All living organisms are composed of one or more cells.**
2. **The cell is the basic unit of structure and organization in organisms.**
3. **All cells arise from pre-existing cells** (*Omnis cellula e cellula* — Rudolf Virchow, 1855).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cell as a Bordered City"

Imagine a city surrounded by a **highly selective border wall** (the plasma membrane). Inside the wall:
- A **power plant** (mitochondria) generates energy (ATP).
- A **library / blueprint office** (nucleus) stores all genetic information.
- **Postal and customs offices** (endoplasmic reticulum & Golgi apparatus) package and dispatch materials.
- **Recycling trucks** (lysosomes) clean up waste.
- A **water-tight selective barrier** (lipid bilayer with embedded protein gates) only allows certain "citizens" (ions like K⁺, Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻) to cross, and only when proper documentation (channel proteins) is presented.

The **bio-electric potential** across this "border wall" is analogous to the **voltage difference** between the inside and outside of the city — maintained by constant pumping (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase) and selective opening of gates (voltage-gated ion channels).

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Used in Cell Bio-Electricity

| Symbol | Quantity | Value |
|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| $F$ | Faraday constant | **96,485 C·mol⁻¹** (≈ 96500 C/mol) |
| $T$ | Body temperature | **310 K** (37 °C) |
| $RT/F$ | Thermal voltage at 310 K | **26.73 mV** |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** |
| $q$ | Elementary charge | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examinable Constant:** At body temperature (310 K), the frequently used ratio $\dfrac{RT}{F} \approx 26.73 \, \text{mV}$. At room temperature (298 K), this becomes ≈ **25.69 mV**. Always state the temperature when using the Nernst equation in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Animal Cell — Structural Components

A typical **animal cell** (eukaryotic) consists of three broad regions: the **plasma membrane**, the **cytoplasm**, and the **nucleus**. Each component has a specific bio-electric or biomedical significance.

#### 2.1.1 The Plasma (Cell) Membrane

The plasma membrane is a **phospholipid bilayer** (~7–10 nm thick) embedded with proteins, cholesterol, and carbohydrate moieties. The most accepted model is the **Fluid Mosaic Model** (Singer & Nicolson, 1972).

**Composition by mass:**
- **Lipids (~50%)**: phospholipids (phosphatidylcholine, phosphatidylethanolamine, phosphatidylserine, sphingomyelin), cholesterol.
- **Proteins (~40%)**: integral (transmembrane) and peripheral.
- **Carbohydrates (~10%)**: glycolipids and glycoproteins forming the glycocalyx.

**Types of Membrane Proteins:**
1. **Channel proteins** — form aqueous pores (e.g., Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻ channels). Subtypes: *leak (always open)*, *voltage-gated*, *ligand-gated*, *mechanically-gated*.
2. **Carrier (transporter) proteins** — bind substrate and undergo conformational change (e.g., Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, glucose transporter GLUT).
3. **Receptor proteins** — bind signaling molecules (hormones, neurotransmitters).
4. **Enzymes** — catalyze reactions at the membrane surface.
5. **Anchoring proteins** — link membrane to cytoskeleton.

#### 2.1.2 Cytoplasm and Organelles

| Organelle | Function | Biomedical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Nucleus** | Stores DNA; controls protein synthesis | Site of genetic disorders; targeted in gene therapy |
| **Mitochondria** | ATP synthesis via oxidative phosphorylation | "Powerhouse of cell"; dysfunction in Parkinson's, cardiac ischemia |
| **Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER)** | Rough ER → protein synthesis; Smooth ER → lipid synthesis, Ca²⁺ storage | Ca²⁺ release → muscle contraction |
| **Golgi apparatus** | Modifies, packages, and ships proteins | Secretory pathway |
| **Lysosomes** | Intracellular digestion via hydrolytic enzymes | Apoptosis; lysosomal storage diseases |
| **Ribosomes** | Protein synthesis | Target of antibiotics |
| **Cytoskeleton** (microtubules, microfilaments, intermediate filaments) | Cell shape, intracellular transport, motility | Mechano-transduction |

#### 2.1.3 Nucleus

Bounded by a **double nuclear envelope** perforated by **nuclear pore complexes (~125 MDa)**. Contains:
- **Chromatin** (DNA + histones)
- **Nucleolus** — site of rRNA synthesis and ribosome assembly.

### 2.2 Membrane Transport Mechanisms

Movement of substances across the plasma membrane occurs by:

1. **Passive Transport (no ATP required)**
   - **Simple diffusion**: small non-polar molecules (O₂, CO₂, steroid hormones).
   - **Facilitated diffusion**: via carrier or channel proteins (glucose, ions).
   - **Osmosis**: net water movement across semi-permeable membrane.

2. **Active Transport (ATP required)**
   - **Primary active transport**: direct ATP hydrolysis (e.g., Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, Ca²⁺-ATPase).
   - **Secondary active transport**: uses electrochemical gradient of another ion (e.g., SGLT, Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger).

3. **Bulk Transport**
   - **Endocytosis** (phagocytosis, pinocytosis, receptor-mediated)
   - **Exocytosis**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (electrogenic) extrudes **3 Na⁺ out** for every **2 K⁺ in** per ATP hydrolyzed. This 3:2 stoichiometry is the *primary determinant* of the negative resting membrane potential. It contributes approximately **−4 to −10 mV** to the resting potential directly, and indirectly establishes the ionic gradients that drive the remaining ~−70 mV via K⁺ leak channels.

### 2.3 The Bio-Electric Cell — Ionic Basis of Membrane Potential

A cell at rest maintains a **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** of approximately **−70 mV to −90 mV** (inside negative relative to outside) for a typical neuron.

**Why is the inside negative?**

Three factors contribute:
1. **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** maintains high intracellular K⁺ and high extracellular Na⁺.
2. **K⁺ leak channels** make the membrane far more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺ at rest (P_K : P_Na ≈ 25 : 1).
3. **Impermeant anions** (proteins, phosphates, ATP) are trapped inside, contributing negative charge.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL CONSTRAINT NOTICE:** All absolute values use `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table rendering.

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Variables | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst Equation (single ion) | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | $E_{ion}$: equilibrium potential (V); $z$: valence; $[ion]$: molar concentration | Equilibrium potential of a single permeant ion |
| 2 | Nernst (log₁₀ form) | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | Same as above | Log-base-10 version used at 310 K: $E = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ mV |
| 3 | Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$ | $P_{ion}$: permeability; note Cl⁻ is reversed (in numerator denominator swapped) | RMP when multiple ions are permeant |
| 4 | Nernst-Planck Flux Equation | $J_i = -D_i \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} + \dfrac{z_i F}{RT} D_i C_i \dfrac{dV}{dx}$ | $J_i$: flux (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹); $D_i$: diffusion coefficient | Ion flux under concentration + electric field |
| 5 | Donnan Equilibrium | $[K^+]_i \cdot [Cl^-]_i = [K^+]_o \cdot [Cl^-]_o$ | For impermeant anion $[A^-]_i$ | Equilibrium with fixed intracellular anions |
| 6 | Osmotic Pressure (van't Hoff) | $\pi = CRT$ | $C$: osmolarity (osm·L⁻¹); $T$: K | Cell volume regulation |
| 7 | GHK Current Equation | $I_{ion} = P_{ion} \cdot \dfrac{z^2 F^2 V_m}{RT} \cdot \dfrac{[ion]_i - [ion]_o e^{-zFV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-zFV_m/RT}}$ | $I_{ion}$: ionic current | Single-channel conductance / net current |
| 8 | Chord Conductance Equation | $g_{ion} = \dfrac{I_{ion}}{V_m - E_{ion}}$ | $g$: conductance (S) | Equivalent circuit representation |

### 2.5 The Action Potential — Phases

A nerve or muscle action potential has 5 phases (memorize for KTU):

| Phase | Event | Dominant Current | Membrane Voltage Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resting** | K⁺ leak dominant; Na⁺/K⁺ pump maintains gradients | $I_{K,leak}$ | ≈ **−70 mV** (inside) |
| **Depolarization** (Phase 0) | Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open rapidly | $I_{Na}$ (inward) | Rises to ≈ **+30 to +40 mV** |
| **Repolarization** (Phase 1 & 3) | Na⁺ channels inactivate; voltage-gated K⁺ channels open | $I_K$ (outward) | Returns toward RMP |
| **Hyperpolarization** (Phase 4 / undershoot) | K⁺ channels close slowly | $I_K$ still active | Drops to ≈ **−80 mV** transiently |
| **Return to RMP** | Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase restores ionic gradients | Pump current | Re-establishes **−70 mV** |

### 2.6 Equivalent Circuit Model of the Cell Membrane (Hodgkin Model)

The membrane can be modeled as:
- A **capacitor** ($C_m$, ~1 μF/cm²) representing the lipid bilayer's charge storage.
- Variable **resistors** ($R_{Na}$, $R_K$, $R_{Cl}$) representing ion-selective channels, each in series with a **battery** ($E_{Na}$, $E_K$, $E_{Cl}$) representing the Nernst potential of that ion.

**Why this matters in biomedical engineering:** This model is the foundation of every **cable equation** derivation used in understanding ECG, EEG, nerve conduction studies, and the design of neuroprosthetic devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Nernst Equation

The **Nernst equation** describes the equilibrium potential across a membrane that is permeable to a single ion.

**Starting Setup:** Consider a membrane separating two solutions containing ion $X^{z}$ at concentrations $[X]_{in}$ (inside) and $[X]_{out}$ (outside). The membrane is permeable only to $X^{z}$.

**Step 1 — Electrochemical Potential**

The free energy change per mole for moving ion $X^{z}$ from inside to outside is:

$$\Delta G = \mu_{out} - \mu_{in}$$

$$\Delta G = RT \ln \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} + zF(V_{out} - V_{in})$$

where $\mu = \mu^0 + RT \ln [X] + zFV$ is the electrochemical potential.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium Condition**

At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ (no net flux of $X^{z}$ across the membrane).

$$RT \ln \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} + zF(V_{out} - V_{in}) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for the Membrane Potential**

Define $E_X = V_{in} - V_{out}$ (potential of inside relative to outside, the convention in physiology).

$$zF \cdot E_X = RT \ln \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

**Step 4 — Final Nernst Equation**

$$\boxed{E_X = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Base-10 Logarithm**

Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$E_X = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate at Body Temperature (T = 310 K)**

Substituting $R = 8.314$, $F = 96485$, $T = 310$:

$$\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = \dfrac{5925.27}{96485} \approx 0.0615 \, \text{V} = 61.5 \, \text{mV}$$

Therefore, the simplified form:

$$E_X \approx \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} \, \text{mV} \quad \text{(at 37 °C)}$$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Nernst Potential for K⁺

For a typical mammalian neuron:
- $[K^+]_{in} = 140 \, \text{mM}$, $[K^+]_{out} = 5 \, \text{mM}$, $z = +1$

**Step 1 — Write the equation**

$$E_K = \dfrac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10} \dfrac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}} \, \text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10} \dfrac{5}{140} \, \text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio**

$$\dfrac{5}{140} = 0.03571$$

**Step 4 — Take the log₁₀**

$$\log_{10}(0.03571) = -1.4472$$

**Step 5 — Multiply**

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.4472) \approx -89.0 \, \text{mV}$$

**Result:** $E_K \approx -89 \, \text{mV}$. This is close to the resting potential because K⁺ is the dominant permeant ion at rest.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Nernst Potential for Na⁺

For a typical neuron:
- $[Na^+]_{in} = 15 \, \text{mM}$, $[Na^+]_{out} = 145 \, \text{mM}$, $z = +1$

**Step 1**

$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \times \log_{10} \dfrac{145}{15}$$

**Step 2**

$$\dfrac{145}{15} = 9.6667$$

**Step 3**

$$\log_{10}(9.6667) = 0.9853$$

**Step 4**

$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \times 0.9853 \approx +60.6 \, \text{mV}$$

**Result:** $E_{Na} \approx +61 \, \text{mV}$. This is why during the action potential upstroke, $V_m$ approaches +60 mV.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

When **multiple ions** are simultaneously permeant, the simple Nernst equation fails. We must derive a more general expression.

**Starting Point — Flux from Nernst-Planck**

For a thin membrane, the steady-state flux of ion $i$ is:

$$J_i = P_i z_i F \dfrac{[i]_{in} - [i]_{out} e^{-z_i FV_m / RT}}{1 - e^{-z_i FV_m / RT}}$$

where $P_i$ is the permeability coefficient.

**Step 1 — Apply the Constraint of Zero Net Current at Steady State**

At the resting state, the net ionic current across the membrane is zero (capacitive current = 0 at steady state):

$$I_{Na} + I_K + I_{Cl} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the GHK Flux (Current) Expressions**

For monovalent ions ($z = \pm 1$):

$$P_{Na} \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{[Na^+]_{in} - [Na^+]_{out} e^{-FV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-FV_m/RT}} + P_K \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{[K^+]_{in} - [K^+]_{out} e^{-FV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-FV_m/RT}} - P_{Cl} \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{[Cl^-]_{out} - [Cl^-]_{in} e^{FV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{FV_m/RT}} = 0$$

(The Cl⁻ term differs in sign because of charge convention and directionality.)

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_m$ (Algebraic Simplification)**

After considerable algebra (factoring common denominators), the equation collapses to:

$$V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K [K^+]_{out} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K [K^+]_{in} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{out}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Trap (commonly loses marks!):** For anions (e.g., Cl⁻), the concentration terms are **swapped** (in the numerator on top, out on bottom) compared to cations, because anions carry negative charge. KTU examiners frequently test this.

**Step 4 — Final GHK Equation**

$$\boxed{V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K [K^+]_{out} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K [K^+]_{in} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{out}}}$$

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Resting Membrane Potential using GHK

**Given:** Squid giant axon at 6.3 °C (T = 279.5 K)
- $[K^+]_{in} = 400$, $[K^+]_{out} = 20$ mM
- $[Na^+]_{in} = 50$, $[Na^+]_{out} = 440$ mM
- $[Cl^-]_{in} = 40$, $[Cl^-]_{out} = 560$ mM
- Permeabilities: $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$

**Step 1 — Compute $\dfrac{RT}{F}$ at T = 279.5 K**

$$\dfrac{8.314 \times 279.5}{96485} = \dfrac{2323.4}{96485} \approx 0.02408 \, \text{V} = 24.08 \, \text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Numerator**

$$P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}$$
$$= 1 \cdot 20 + 0.04 \cdot 440 + 0.45 \cdot 40$$
$$= 20 + 17.6 + 18 = 55.6$$

**Step 3 — Compute Denominator**

$$P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}$$
$$= 1 \cdot 400 + 0.04 \cdot 50 + 0.45 \cdot 560$$
$$= 400 + 2 + 252 = 654$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Ratio**

$$\dfrac{55.6}{654} = 0.08502$$

**Step 5 — Take ln and Multiply**

$$\ln(0.08502) = -2.465$$

$$V_m = 24.08 \times (-2.465) \approx -59.4 \, \text{mV}$$

**Result:** $V_m \approx -60 \, \text{mV}$, consistent with the experimentally measured RMP of the squid giant axon (~ −60 to −70 mV). The discrepancy with the textbook value of ~ −70 mV arises because we ignored the electrogenic contribution of the Na⁺/K⁺ pump.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Situation | Equation to Use | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Single ion permeable, equilibrium | Nernst Equation | Only one electrochemical gradient matters |
| Multiple ions simultaneously permeable (resting cell) | Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Voltage Equation | Accounts for relative permeabilities |
| Ion flux under concentration + electric field | Nernst-Planck Equation | General 1-D flux; basis for Fick's law |
| Cell volume / water movement | van't Hoff Osmotic Pressure | Water follows osmotic gradients |
| Cell with fixed intracellular anions | Donnan Equilibrium | Trapped proteins set up Donnan distribution |
| Equivalent circuit modeling | Ohm's Law + Capacitor model | Hodgkin-Huxley formalism |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram — The Animal Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[ANIMAL CELL] --> B[Plasma Membrane]
    A --> C[Cytoplasm]
    A --> D[Nucleus]

    B --> B1[Phospholipid Bilayer]
    B --> B2[Integral Proteins]
    B --> B3[Peripheral Proteins]
    B --> B4[Glycocalyx]

    B2 --> B2a[Ion Channels]
    B2 --> B2b[Carrier Transporters]
    B2 --> B2c[Receptors]

    C --> C1[Mitochondria]
    C --> C2[Endoplasmic Reticulum]
    C --> C3[Golgi Apparatus]
    C --> C4[Lysosomes]
    C --> C5[Ribosomes]
    C --> C6[Cytoskeleton]

    D --> D1[Nuclear Envelope]
    D --> D2[Chromatin]
    D --> D3[Nucleolus]

    classDef cellRoot fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef membrane fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef cyto fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef nuc fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class A cellRoot
    class B,B1,B2,B3,B4,B2a,B2b,B2c membrane
    class C,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6 cyto
    class D,D1,D2,D3 nuc
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Bio-Electric Signal Generation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IONIC["Ionic Gradients"]
        K1[K high inside]
        K2[Na high outside]
        Ca1[Ca very low inside]
    end

    subgraph MEMBRANE["Plasma Membrane"]
        PUMP[Na K ATPase Pump]
        KCH[K Leak Channel]
        NAV[Voltage Gated Na Channel]
        KV[Voltage Gated K Channel]
    end

    subgraph STATE["Membrane States"]
        REST[Resting Potential -70 mV]
        DEPOL[Depolarization]
        REPOL[Repolarization]
        RMP[Return to RMP]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Biomedical Output"]
        AP[Action Potential]
        ECG[ECG Signal]
        EEG[EEG Signal]
        EMG[EMG Signal]
    end

    IONIC --> PUMP
    PUMP --> REST
    KCH --> REST
    STIM[Stimulus above threshold] --> NAV
    NAV --> DEPOL
    DEPOL --> KV
    KV --> REPOL
    REPOL --> RMP
    RMP --> AP
    AP --> ECG
    AP --> EEG
    AP --> EMG

    classDef ionicStyle fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef memStyle fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef stateStyle fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef outStyle fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    class K1,K2,Ca1 ionicStyle
    class PUMP,KCH,NAV,KV memStyle
    class REST,DEPOL,REPOL,RMP stateStyle
    class AP,ECG,EEG,EMG outStyle
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Hodgkin Membrane Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT["Extracellular Fluid"]
        Vout[Reference 0 mV]
    end

    subgraph MEM["Membrane Circuit"]
        CM[(Cm ~ 1 microF per cm2)]
    end

    subgraph INT["Intracellular Fluid"]
        VIN[Vin]
    end

    EXT --- CM
    CM --- INT

    CM --- NAK{Branch Na}
    CM --- KB{Branch K}
    CM --- CLB{Branch Cl}

    NAK --- ENa[Battery ENa approx +60 mV]
    NAK --- RNa[Variable Resistor RNa]
    ENa --- RNa

    KB --- EK[Battery EK approx -90 mV]
    KB --- RK[Variable Resistor RK]
    EK --- RK

    CLB --- ECl[Battery ECl approx -70 mV]
    CLB --- RCl[Variable Resistor RCl]
    ECl --- RCl

    classDef circuitStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class CM,NAK,KB,CLB,ENa,EK,ECl,RNa,RK,RCl circuitStyle
    class EXT,INT,Vout,VIN fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Process Flow — Ionic Basis of Action Potential

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Resting State -70 mV] --> S1{Stimulus Reaches Threshold approx -55 mV}
    S1 -- NO --> S0
    S1 -- YES --> S2[Voltage Gated Na Channels Open]
    S2 --> S3[Rapid Na Influx]
    S3 --> S4[Depolarization Phase 0]
    S4 --> S5[Membrane Potential approx +30 mV]
    S5 --> S6[Na Channels Inactivate]
    S6 --> S7[K Channels Open Slowly]
    S7 --> S8[K Efflux]
    S8 --> S9[Repolarization Phase 3]
    S9 --> S10[Hyperpolarization Phase 4 approx -80 mV]
    S10 --> S11[K Channels Close]
    S11 --> S12[Na K ATPase Restores Gradients]
    S12 --> S0

    classDef rest fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef depol fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef repol fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef hyper fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    class S0,S12 rest
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 depol
    class S6,S7,S8,S9 repol
    class S10,S11 hyper
```
