## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
**Define resting membrane potential. List the three principal factors that determine the resting membrane potential of a neuron.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The *resting membrane potential (RMP)* is the steady-state voltage difference across the plasma membrane of an excitable cell when it is not generating an action potential. Conventionally, it is expressed as the potential of the intracellular fluid relative to the extracellular fluid, and for a typical mammalian neuron it is approximately **−70 mV** (inside negative).
>
> **Three Principal Factors (2 marks — 1 mark each for naming + brief justification):**
> 1. **Ionic concentration gradients** established by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (high K⁺ inside, high Na⁺ outside).
> 2. **Differential membrane permeability** at rest: the membrane is far more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺ due to **K⁺ leak channels** ($P_K : P_{Na} \approx 25:1$).
> 3. **Impermeant intracellular anions** (proteins, organic phosphates, ATP) that are trapped inside the cell and contribute fixed negative charge.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
**What is the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump? Why is it called electrogenic, and what is its stoichiometry?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** (also called the *sodium-potassium pump*) is a P-type ATPase enzyme embedded in the plasma membrane that hydrolyzes one molecule of ATP to actively transport **3 Na⁺ ions out of the cell** and **2 K⁺ ions into the cell** against their respective electrochemical gradients.
>
> **Why electrogenic (1 mark):** It is called *electrogenic* because it transfers a **net positive charge of +1 out of the cell per cycle** (3 out − 2 in = 1 net positive charge moved outward). This creates an additional negative potential inside, contributing approximately **−4 to −10 mV** to the resting membrane potential beyond what is predicted by the GHK equation alone.
>
> **Stoichiometry (1 mark):** **3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in : 1 ATP hydrolyzed**, per pump cycle.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for the equilibrium potential of a single ionic species across a semipermeable membrane. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Nernst equation, calculate the equilibrium potentials for **K⁺** and **Na⁺** at body temperature (37 °C), given the following typical intracellular and extracellular concentrations for a mammalian neuron:

| Ion | Intracellular (mM) | Extracellular (mM) |
|---|---|---|
| K⁺ | 140 | 5 |
| Na⁺ | 15 | 145 |

Comment on which ion's equilibrium potential is closer to the resting membrane potential and explain why. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation of the Nernst Equation (7 marks)**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The membrane is permeable to **only one ionic species** at a time.
2. The system has reached **thermodynamic equilibrium** (no net flux).
3. The solutions are **ideal** and **dilute** (activity ≈ concentration).
4. Temperature is **uniform** and constant.

**Derivation Steps (6 marks — distribute as 1.5 per major step):**

**Step 1 — Electrochemical Potential:** The free energy (chemical potential) of one mole of ion $X^{z}$ at concentration $[X]$ and electrical potential $V$ is:

$$\mu = \mu^0 + RT \ln[X] + zFV$$

For ion moving from **inside** (concentration $[X]_{in}$, potential $V_{in}$) to **outside** (concentration $[X]_{out}$, potential $V_{out}$):

$$\Delta G = \mu_{out} - \mu_{in} = RT \ln\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} + zF(V_{out} - V_{in})$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium condition:** At equilibrium, the **net driving force is zero**, hence $\Delta G = 0$:

$$RT \ln\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} + zF(V_{out} - V_{in}) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the membrane potential:** Define $E_X = V_{in} - V_{out}$. Rearranging:

$$zFE_X = RT \ln\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

$$E_X = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to log₁₀ form:**

$$E_X = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

At **T = 310 K** (body temperature), $\dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \approx 61.5 \, \text{mV}$, giving:

$$E_X \approx \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} \, \text{mV}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks)**

**For K⁺ (3 marks):**

$$E_K = \dfrac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\dfrac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}} = 61.5 \log_{10}\dfrac{5}{140}$$

$$E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.03571) = 61.5 \times (-1.4472)$$

$$\boxed{E_K \approx -89.0 \, \text{mV}}$$

**For Na⁺ (3 marks):**

$$E_{Na} = \dfrac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\dfrac{[Na^+]_{out}}{[Na^+]_{in}} = 61.5 \log_{10}\dfrac{145}{15}$$

$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(9.6667) = 61.5 \times 0.9853$$

$$\boxed{E_{Na} \approx +60.6 \, \text{mV}}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** $E_K \approx -89$ mV is **closer** to the resting membrane potential (≈ −70 mV) than $E_{Na} \approx +61$ mV. This is because, at rest, the membrane is **predominantly permeable to K⁺** (via K⁺ leak channels), so the resting potential is driven *toward* $E_K$ rather than $E_{Na}$. The exact resting potential lies between $E_K$ and $E_{Na}$, weighted by their relative permeabilities — as given quantitatively by the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **Fluid Mosaic Model** of the plasma membrane. List the major types of membrane proteins and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation** for the resting membrane potential. Explain why the equation reduces to the Nernst equation for a single ion when only one permeant species exists. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Fluid Mosaic Model of Plasma Membrane (7 marks)**

**Description (3 marks):**
The **Fluid Mosaic Model**, proposed by **S. Jonathan Singer and Garth L. Nicolson in 1972**, describes the plasma membrane as a **two-dimensional fluid** in which a **phospholipid bilayer** serves as a matrix in which diverse **proteins** are embedded. The phospholipids are amphipathic: hydrophilic heads face the aqueous environments (cytoplasm and extracellular fluid), while hydrophobic tails point inward. The bilayer is **laterally fluid**, allowing proteins and lipids to diffuse in the plane of the membrane. The membrane is approximately **7–10 nm thick**.

**Major Components:**
- **Phospholipids** (≈ 50%): phosphatidylcholine, phosphatidylethanolamine, phosphatidylserine, sphingomyelin — form the bilayer.
- **Cholesterol** (inserted between phospholipids): modulates fluidity.
- **Proteins** (≈ 40%): integral and peripheral.
- **Carbohydrates** (≈ 10%): form glycolipids and glycoproteins (glycocalyx) — involved in cell recognition.

**Types of Membrane Proteins and Functions (4 marks — allocate ~1 mark each):**

| Type | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Channel Proteins** | Form aqueous pores for ion passage; may be leak, voltage-gated, or ligand-gated | Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻ channels |
| **Carrier (Transporter) Proteins** | Bind substrate, undergo conformational change to transport across bilayer | Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, GLUT, Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger |
| **Receptor Proteins** | Bind signaling molecules (hormones, neurotransmitters) and trigger intracellular response | β-adrenergic receptor, nicotinic ACh receptor |
| **Enzymes** | Catalyze reactions at membrane surface | Adenylate cyclase, ATPases |
| **Anchoring / Cell Adhesion Proteins** | Link membrane to cytoskeleton or to neighboring cells | Integrins, cadherins |

---

**(b) Derivation of the GHK Voltage Equation (7 marks)**

**Starting assumption (1 mark):** At steady state, the **net transmembrane ionic current is zero** (no net charge accumulation by ionic flux alone; capacitive current also zero at DC):

$$I_{Na} + I_K + I_{Cl} = 0$$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Use the **Nernst-Planck equation** for steady-state flux of each ion across a thin membrane. For monovalent ion $i$:

$$J_i = P_i \cdot z_i F \cdot \dfrac{[i]_{in} - [i]_{out} \cdot e^{-z_i F V_m / RT}}{1 - e^{-z_i F V_m / RT}}$$

where $P_i$ is the permeability coefficient and $V_m = V_{in} - V_{out}$ is the membrane potential.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Multiply each flux by $z_iF$ to get current density, and write each ionic current. For cations ($z = +1$): $I_i = z_iF \cdot J_i$. For Cl⁻ ($z = -1$): $I_{Cl} = -F J_{Cl}$ (note the sign change because the flux equation already includes $z$ — careful bookkeeping required).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Set $I_{Na} + I_K + I_{Cl} = 0$ and solve algebraically. Factoring out the common denominator $(1 - e^{-FV_m/RT})$ for cations and the analogous denominator for the anion, after cancellation:

$$V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K [K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K [K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Note the convention — for **anions** (Cl⁻), the in/out concentrations appear **reversed** in numerator vs. denominator because they carry negative charge.

**Reduction to Nernst Equation (1 mark):** If the membrane is permeable to **only one ion**, say K⁺, then $P_{Na} = P_{Cl} = 0$. Substituting:

$$V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K [K^+]_{out}}{P_K [K^+]_{in}} = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}} = E_K$$

which is precisely the **Nernst equation** for K⁺.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention confusion (most common, costs 1–2 marks):** The membrane potential is *inside relative to outside* ($V_m = V_{in} - V_{out}$), but the **Nernst equation** for cations uses $[ion]_{out}/[ion]_{in}$ in the log ratio — students sometimes flip this. Memorize: *"Nernst = out over in."*
>
> 2. **Anion swap in GHK (costs 1–2 marks):** In the GHK equation, **Cl⁻ concentrations are written reversed** (numerator has Cl⁻ᵢₙ, denominator has Cl⁻ₒᵤₜ). Students copy the formula incorrectly by putting Cl⁻ₒᵤₜ in the numerator.
>
> 3. **Temperature omission (costs 1 mark):** When using the Nernst equation with the simplified 61.5 mV constant, students forget to state **"at body temperature (310 K)"**. Always declare T.
>
> 4. **Confusing $E_{ion}$ with $V_m$ (costs conceptual marks):** $E_{ion}$ is the equilibrium potential for *one* ion. The *actual* membrane potential $V_m$ equals $E_{ion}$ only if that ion is the **sole** permeant species.
>
> 5. **No units in numerical answers:** Always write the unit (mV) with numerical answers. A correct numerical value without units loses a half-mark.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the electrogenic pump:** When discussing RMP, many students explain the GHK equation but forget the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase contribution** of −4 to −10 mV. Examiners explicitly look for this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **cell** is the basic structural and functional unit of life, bounded by a **phospholipid bilayer plasma membrane**.
- ✅ The **Fluid Mosaic Model** (Singer & Nicolson, 1972) describes the plasma membrane; key components are **lipids, proteins, and carbohydrates**.
- ✅ **Membrane proteins** include channel, carrier, receptor, enzyme, and anchoring proteins.
- ✅ **Transport mechanisms** are: passive (simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis) and active (primary, secondary, bulk).
- ✅ The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** has a stoichiometry of **3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in : 1 ATP**, is **electrogenic**, and is the primary driver of ionic gradients.
- ✅ **Nernst equation:** $E_X = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$; at 37 °C, $E_X \approx \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$ mV.
- ✅ **Typical equilibrium potentials at 37 °C:** $E_K \approx -89$ mV; $E_{Na} \approx +61$ mV; $E_{Ca} \approx +123$ mV; $E_{Cl} \approx -65$ mV.
- ✅ **GHK voltage equation** gives the steady-state $V_m$ when multiple ions are permeant; uses **permeability ratios**, not conductance.
- ✅ **RMP ≈ −70 mV** (neuron) lies between $E_K$ and $E_{Na}$, dominated by K⁺ permeability at rest.
- ✅ **Action potential phases**: Resting → Depolarization (Na⁺ in) → Repolarization (K⁺ out) → Hyperpolarization → Return via Na⁺/K⁺ pump.
- ✅ **Threshold ≈ −55 mV** (typically). Stimuli below threshold → graded local potential; above threshold → all-or-none action potential.
- ✅ **Equivalent circuit**: membrane = capacitor ($C_m$) in parallel with variable resistors + batteries (one per ion).
- ✅ **Key constants**: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $F = 96485$ C/mol; at 310 K, $RT/F \approx 26.73$ mV; $2.303 RT/F \approx 61.5$ mV.
- ✅ **Sign convention traps**: Nernst uses *out/in*; GHK uses *out/in* for cations but *in/out* for anions.
- ✅ **Biomedical relevance**: RMP and AP underpin ECG, EEG, EMG, nerve conduction studies, defibrillator design, and pacemaker function.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transport of Ions Through the Cell Membrane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEETVT615 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Transport of Ions Through the Cell Membrane # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Transport of ions through the cell membrane** refers to the regulated movement of charged ionic species (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻, etc.) across the **phospholipid bilayer** that encloses every living cell. This transport is governed by electrochemical gradients (concentration gradient + electrical gradient) and is mediated by specialized transmembrane proteins functioning as **ion channels**, **carriers**, or **pumps**. The cell membrane is approximately **7–10 nm thick** and is composed of a **lipid bilayer** (phospholipids + cholesterol) interspersed with integral proteins. The lipid core is **hydrophobic**, which makes it virtually impermeable to charged ions — therefore *all ionic transport is protein-mediated*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: This topic directly maps to **CO1** (Understand the fundamental physiological processes at cellular level) of OEETVT615. Students must master the distinction between *passive* (down the gradient, no ATP) and *active* (against the gradient, requires ATP) transport, and must be able to derive the **Nernst Equation** and apply the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation**. > [!NOTE] > **Core Terminology Box**: > - **Electrochemical Gradient** = sum of chemical (concentration) driving force and electrical driving force on an ion. > - **Permeability (P)** = ease with which an ion crosses the membrane (units: cm/s). > - **Membrane Potential (Vm)** = voltage difference between intracellular and extracellular fluid (typically **−70 mV** at rest for a neuron). > - **Equilibrium Potential (Eion)** = membrane voltage at which the electrical force exactly balances the chemical force on a given ion (no net flux). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer at a Club" Imagine the cell membrane as the **front door of an exclusive nightclub**, and the ions as the guests trying to enter. - The **lipid bilayer** is the *wall* — guests cannot simply walk through. - **Ion channels** are like **regular doors** — they open briefly when "buzzed in" (stimulated), letting specific guests (Na⁺, K⁺, etc.) through quickly down a "hill" (gradient). These are passive, downhill movements. - **Carrier proteins** are like **turnstiles** — they bind a guest, twist, and release them on the other side. Slower, but selective. - **The Sodium-Potassium Pump (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase)** is the **VIP bouncer working overtime** — he physically grabs 3 Na⁺ guests from inside and kicks them out, while pulling 2 K⁺ guests inside. He needs an energy drink (ATP) to do this against the natural flow. This regulated traffic creates a **voltage difference** between the inside and outside of the cell — the **membrane potential** — which is the fundamental basis of nerve signaling, muscle contraction, and every bioelectric event a biomedical engineer must understand. ## 1.3 Why This Matters in Biomedical Engineering - **ECG / EEG / EMG**: All bioelectric signals arise from ionic currents (predominantly Na⁺ and K⁺) crossing millions of cell membranes in synchrony. - **Defibrillators / Pacemakers**: Inject or sense ionic currents to reset cardiac rhythm. - **Drug Delivery & Iontophoresis**: Engineering transport of charged drug molecules across skin/cell membranes using applied electric fields. - **Dialysis Machines**: Exploit concentration gradients and selective membrane permeability to filter ions from blood. - **Biosensors & Patch-Clamp Amplifiers**: Measure single-channel ion currents (pA range). - **Neural Prosthetics & Brain-Computer Interfaces**: Modulate ionic transport optically (optogenetics) or electrically. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Membrane Transport Mechanisms ### A. Passive Transport (Down the electrochemical gradient, ΔG < 0) 1. **Simple Diffusion** - Direct passage of small non-polar molecules (O₂, CO₂) through the lipid bilayer. - No protein involvement; follows **Fick's Law**. 2. **Facilitated Diffusion** - Mediated by **carrier proteins** or **channel proteins**. - Still passive (no ATP), but saturable and specific. - Example: GLUT transporters for glucose. 3. **Diffusion through Ion Channels** - Selective for specific ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻). - Gated by voltage, ligand binding, or mechanical stretch. - Very high throughput (~10⁶ ions/sec per channel). ### B. Active Transport (Against the electrochemical gradient, ΔG > 0) 1. **Primary Active Transport** - Directly powered by **ATP hydrolysis**. - Example: **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** (pumps 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per ATP). 2. **Secondary Active Transport** - Uses energy stored in an ion gradient (created by primary active transport). - **Symport** (both ions same direction, e.g., Na⁺-glucose cotransporter SGLT1). - **Antiport** (opposite directions, e.g., Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger NCX). ## 2.2 The Resting Membrane Potential — Nernst and Goldman ### Ionic Basis of Resting Potential Inside the cell: **High K⁺ (~140 mM), Low Na⁺ (~10 mM), High Cl⁻ (~4 mM), High A⁻ (anionic proteins, ~140 mM)**. Outside the cell: **High Na⁺ (~145 mM), Low K⁺ (~4 mM), High Cl⁻ (~120 mM)**. This asymmetric distribution is maintained by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump**. > [!IMPORTANT] > At rest, the membrane is **~25× more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺**. K⁺ diffuses outward down its concentration gradient, leaving the inside negative — establishing the resting potential near **−70 mV**. ### The Nernst Equation For a single ion at equilibrium across a membrane permeable only to that ion: $$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{outside}}{[ion]_{inside}}$$ Using **log₁₀** (more common in physiology): $$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{outside}}{[ion]_{inside}}$$ At body temperature **T = 310 K (37 °C)** and substituting constants: $$E_{ion} = \frac{61 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{outside}}{[ion]_{inside}}$$ ### The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation When the membrane is permeable to multiple ions, the **resting membrane potential** is the weighted sum of the individual Nernst potentials, weighted by their relative permeabilities: $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$ At **37 °C**, the numerical form becomes: $$V_m = 61 \text{ mV} \cdot \log_{10} \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$ ## 2.3 The Nernst-Planck Equation (Flux Through Channels) The ionic current density across a membrane due to both diffusion and electromigration: $$J_{ion} = -D_{ion} \left( \frac{dC_{ion}}{dx} \right) - \frac{z F D_{ion}}{RT} C_{ion} \left( \frac{dV}{dx} \right)$$ where: - **J** = ionic flux (mol/m²·s) - **D** = diffusion coefficient (m²/s) - **C** = ion concentration (mol/m³) - **z** = ionic valence - **V** = electric potential (V) - **x** = position across membrane (m) ## 2.4 Donnan Equilibrium (Gibbs-Donnan Effect) When a membrane is impermeable to a large intracellular anion (A⁻, e.g., proteins), the diffusible ions distribute asymmetrically to maintain **electroneutrality**. The Donnan ratio is: $$r = \frac{[K^+]_i}{[K^+]_o} = \frac{[Cl^-]_o}{[Cl^-]_i} = \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{[A^-]_i}{[C]_o}\right)^2} + \frac{[A^-]_i}{[C]_o}$$ This explains the **unequal distribution of diffusible ions** between intracellular and extracellular compartments even at equilibrium. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Key Variables | Typical Units / Values | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Nernst Equation** (decimal log) | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}$ | z = ion charge | **E** in mV | | 2 | **Nernst Equation** (natural log) | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}$ | R = 8.314 J/mol·K, F = 96,485 C/mol | T in Kelvin | | 3 | **GHK Voltage Equation** | $V_m = 61 \log_{10} \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ | $P_X$ = permeability of ion X | Vm in mV | | 4 | **Nernst-Planck Flux** | $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx} - \dfrac{zFD}{RT} C \dfrac{dV}{dx}$ | D = diffusion coeff. | J in mol/m²·s | | 5 | **Fick's Law (Diffusion)** | $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | dC/dx = conc. gradient | J in mol/m²·s | | 6 | **Goldman Current Equation** | $I_i = P_i z_i^2 F^2 V_m \dfrac{[I]_i - [I]_o e^{-z_iFV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-z_iFV_m/RT}}$ | I = current density | I in A/m² | | 7 | **Donnan Ratio** | $r = \dfrac{[K^+]_i}{[K^+]_o} = \dfrac{[Cl^-]_o}{[Cl^-]_i}$ | [ ] = concentrations | dimensionless | | 8 | **Na⁺/K⁺ Pump Ratio** | 3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in per 1 ATP | Net charge: **+1 outward** | electrogenic | | 9 | **Resting Vm (typical neuron)** | ≈ **−70 mV** | determined mainly by K⁺ | mV | | 10 | **Thermal Voltage (kT/e)** | $V_T = \dfrac{RT}{F}$ ≈ **25.7 mV** (27 °C), **26.7 mV** (37 °C) | thermal voltage | mV | ## 2.6 Engineering Utility - **Cardiac Pacing & Defibrillation**: Threshold currents depend on membrane permeability changes; calculations use GHK. - **Electroporation**: Applying a high external field (V/cm) overcomes the lipid barrier — used in gene transfection and tumor ablation. - **Biosensor Design**: ISFETs and ion-selective electrodes exploit Nernst's logarithmic dependence. - **Pharmacokinetics**: Henderson-Hasselbalch and ion-trapping use Nernst principles. - **Neuroprosthetics**: Cochlear implants stimulate spiral ganglion neurons by depolarizing their membranes → requires understanding of channel kinetics. - **Hemodialysis**: Ion clearance across synthetic membranes uses the same diffusion + electromigration physics as Nernst-Planck. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation **Starting Premise:** Consider an ion $X^{z+}$ at concentration $[X]_i$ inside the cell and $[X]_o$ outside, separated by a membrane permeable only to that ion. At equilibrium, the **electrochemical potential** on both sides must be equal. ### Step 1: Write the Electrochemical Potential on Each Side The electrochemical potential of an ion at location with concentration [C] and electric potential V is: $$\tilde{\mu} = \mu^0 + RT \ln[C] + zFV$$ Inside the cell: $\tilde{\mu}_i = \mu^0 + RT \ln[X]_i + zF V_i$ Outside the cell: $\tilde{\mu}_o = \mu^0 + RT \ln[X]_o + zF V_o$ ### Step 2: Apply the Equilibrium Condition At equilibrium, there is no net flux, so $\tilde{\mu}_i = \tilde{\mu}_o$: $$\mu^0 + RT \ln[X]_i + zF V_i = \mu^0 + RT \ln[X]_o + zF V_o$$ The standard chemical potential $\mu^0$ cancels on both sides. ### Step 3: Rearrange the Equation $$RT \ln[X]_i - RT \ln[X]_o = zF(V_o - V_i)$$ $$RT \ln \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o} = -zF(V_m)$$ where $V_m = V_i - V_o$ (the membrane potential, defined as inside minus outside). ### Step 4: Solve for the Membrane Potential $$V_m = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o}$$ Applying the logarithm identity $-\ln(a/b) = \ln(b/a)$: $$V_m = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$$ ### Step 5: Convert Natural Logarithm to Base-10 Logarithm Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$: $$V_m = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$$ ### Step 6: Substitute Numerical Constants at Body Temperature (T = 310 K) $$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = \frac{5935.7}{96485} \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{E_{ion} = \frac{61 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}}$$ ### Step 7: Numerical Example — Potassium Equilibrium Potential Given: $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM, z = +1: $$E_{K^+} = \frac{61}{+1} \log_{10} \frac{4}{140}$$ $$E_{K^+} = 61 \times \log_{10}(0.02857)$$ $$E_{K^+} = 61 \times (-1.544)$$ $$\boxed{E_{K^+} \approx -94 \text{ mV}}$$ This is very close to the resting membrane potential, confirming K⁺ is the principal determinant. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation ### Step 1: Define Constant Electric Field Assumption Assume the electric field across the membrane is **constant** (linear potential profile). Integrate the Nernst-Planck equation across the membrane thickness $\delta$: $$\frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{V_m}{\delta}$$ ### Step 2: Integrate the Flux for a Single Ion Starting from Nernst-Planck: $$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx} - \frac{zFD}{RT} C \frac{dV}{dx}$$ Multiply both sides by $\exp\left(\frac{zFV}{RT}\right)$ and recognize that this turns it into an exact differential. The integrated flux is: $$J = \frac{z^2 F^2 V_m P}{RT} \cdot \frac{[C]_i - [C]_o \exp(-zFV_m/RT)}{1 - \exp(-zFV_m/RT)}$$ where $P = D/\delta$ is the **permeability coefficient** (cm/s). ### Step 3: Apply the Zero-Net-Current Condition at Rest At rest (no net membrane current), the sum of all individual ionic currents must be zero: $$I_{Na} + I_K + I_{Cl} = 0$$ $$P_{Na} z_{Na}^2 F^2 V_m \frac{[Na]_i - [Na]_o e^{-z_{Na}FV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-z_{Na}FV_m/RT}} + P_K z_K^2 F^2 V_m \frac{[K]_i - [K]_o e^{-z_K FV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-z_K FV_m/RT}} + P_{Cl} z_{Cl}^2 F^2 V_m \frac{[Cl]_o - [Cl]_i e^{-z_{Cl}FV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-z_{Cl}FV_m/RT}} = 0$$ ### Step 4: Simplify Using the Identity Using the identity $\dfrac{a}{1 - e^{-x}} = \dfrac{ae^{+x}}{e^{+x} - 1}$ and recognizing that $z_{Na} = +1$, $z_K = +1$, $z_{Cl} = -1$, the algebra collapses to: $$\sum_i P_i z_i^2 \left( [I]_i - [I]_o e^{-z_i FV_m/RT} \right) = 0$$ ### Step 5: Rearrange and Take the Logarithm Rearranging and solving for $V_m$: $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$ ### Step 6: At 37 °C, Substitute RT/F Numerically $$\frac{RT}{F} = 26.7 \text{ mV}$$ Multiply by 2.303 to convert to log base 10: $2.303 \times 26.7 = 61.5$ mV. $$\boxed{V_m = 61 \text{ mV} \cdot \log_{10} \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}}$$ ### Step 7: Numerical Example — Typical Neuron at Rest Given: - $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM - $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM, $[Na^+]_i = 10$ mM - $[Cl^-]_o = 120$ mM, $[Cl^-]_i = 4$ mM - Permeability ratios: $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$ Compute numerator: $$1(4) + 0.04(145) + 0.45(4) = 4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6$$ Compute denominator: $$1(140) + 0.04(10) + 0.45(120) = 140 + 0.4 + 54 = 194.4$$ Therefore: $$V_m = 61 \log_{10} \frac{11.6}{194.4} = 61 \times \log_{10}(0.05967)$$ $$V_m = 61 \times (-1.224)$$ $$\boxed{V_m \approx -74.7 \text{ mV}}$$ This matches the experimentally observed resting potential of a typical neuron (~−70 mV). ✓ ## 3.3 Derivation of the Donnan Equilibrium Ratio **Starting Premise:** A membrane separates two compartments. One compartment (intracellular) contains an impermeable anion $A^-$ at concentration $C_i$. The other (extracellular) contains only freely permeable ions like K⁺ and Cl⁻ at initial concentration $C_o$. ### Step 1: Electroneutrality in Each Compartment Extracellular: $[K^+]_o = [Cl^-]_o$ Intracellular: $[K^+]_i = [Cl^-]_i + [A^-]_i$ ### Step 2: Equilibrium Condition for Diffusible Ions The product of diffusible cation and anion concentrations must be equal on both sides (from Nernst equation at $V = 0$): $$[K^+]_i \cdot [Cl^-]_i = [K^+]_o \cdot [Cl^-]_o$$ ### Step 3: Donnan Ratio Definition Define $r = [K^+]_i / [K^+]_o$. Then $[K^+]_i = r C_o$ and $[Cl^-]_o = [K^+]_o = C_o$. From the product rule: $[Cl^-]_i = C_o / r$. From intracellular electroneutrality: $$r C_o = \frac{C_o}{r} + [A^-]_i$$ ### Step 4: Solve for r (the Donnan Factor) $$r C_o - \frac{C_o}{r} = [A^-]_i$$ $$r - \frac{1}{r} = \frac{[A^-]_i}{C_o}$$ $$r^2 - 1 = \frac{[A^-]_i}{C_o} \cdot r$$ $$r^2 - \frac{[A^-]_i}{C_o} r - 1 = 0$$ Using the quadratic formula (taking the positive root): $$\boxed{r = \frac{[A^-]_i}{2C_o} + \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{[A^-]_i}{2C_o}\right)^2}}$$ ## 3.4 Worked-Out Analytical Matrix — Multiple Ion Concentrations | Ion | Outside (mM) | Inside (mM) | z | $E_{ion}$ (mV) | Physiological role | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | K⁺ | 4 | 140 | +1 | **−94** | Main determinant of resting Vm | | Na⁺ | 145 | 10 | +1 | **+70** | Drives depolarization phase | | Ca²⁺ | 1.2 | 0.0001 | +2 | **+136** | Triggers neurotransmitter release | | Cl⁻ | 120 | 4 | −1 | **−89** | Stabilizes resting potential | | HCO₃⁻ | 27 | 12 | −1 | **−21** | pH buffering | (Note: $E_{Ca^{2+}}$ cannot be calculated with $1/10^5$ mM inside as 0; using $10^{-4}$ mM gives $\approx +136$ mV.) # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Major Transport Pathways Across the Cell Membrane ```mermaid flowchart TD A["CELL MEMBRANE
Lipid Bilayer 7 to 10 nm"] --> B["PASSIVE TRANSPORT
Down Gradient No ATP"] A --> C["ACTIVE TRANSPORT
Against Gradient ATP Required"] B --> B1["Simple Diffusion
O2 CO2 H2O"] B --> B2["Facilitated Diffusion
GLUT Carrier"] B --> B3["Ion Channels
Voltage Ligand Mech"] B3 --> B3a["Voltage Gated
Nav Kv Cav"] B3 --> B3b["Ligand Gated
nAChR GABA"] B3 --> B3c["Leak Channels
Always Open"] C --> C1["PRIMARY ACTIVE
Direct ATP Hydrolysis"] C --> C2["SECONDARY ACTIVE
Ion Gradient Driven"] C1 --> C1a["Na plus K plus ATPase
3 Na out 2 K in per ATP"] C1 --> C1b["Ca2 plus ATPase SERCA
Pumps Ca into ER"] C2 --> C2a["SYMPORT
Same Direction
SGLT1 Na Glucose"] C2 --> C2b["ANTIPORT
Opposite Direction
NCX Na Ca Exchanger"] B3a --> D["ELECTROCHEMICAL GRADIENT
Sets up Membrane Potential"] C1a --> D D --> E["Vm approx minus 70 mV at Rest
plus 30 mV at Peak AP"] style A fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style B fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B3 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#4b0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow: From Ionic Gradient to Action Potential ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["STEP 1
RESTING STATE
Vm equals minus 70 mV
K plus high inside"] --> S2["STEP 2
STIMULUS
Threshold minus 55 mV
Voltage gated Na opens"] S2 --> S3["STEP 3
DEPOLARIZATION
Na plus rushes IN
Vm rises to plus 30 mV"] S3 --> S4["STEP 4
REPOLARIZATION
Na channels INACTIVATE
K plus channels OPEN"] S4 --> S5["STEP 5
HYPERPOLARIZATION
Vm dips below minus 70 mV"] S5 --> S6["STEP 6
RETURN TO REST
Na K ATPASE restores
ionic gradients"] style S1 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style S2 fill:#ffffe0,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style S3 fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff style S4 fill:#98fb98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style S5 fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#4b0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style S6 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#228b22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase Pump ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph EC["EXTRACELLULAR SPACE"] NAOUT["3 Na plus ions
High Na outside"] KOUT["Low K outside 4 mM"] end subgraph MB["MEMBRANE PUMP Na K ATPase"] P1["E1 STATE
Open to Inside
High Affinity for Na plus"] P2["ATP BINDS
Phosphorylation"] P3["E2 STATE
Open to Outside
High Affinity for K plus"] P4["DEPHOSPHORYLATION
K plus released inside"] end subgraph IC["INTRACELLULAR SPACE"] NAIN["Low Na inside 10 mM"] KIN["2 K plus ions
High K inside 140 mM"] end NAIN --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> NAOUT KOUT --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> KIN P4 --> P1 style EC fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style MB fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style IC fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Conceptual Map: Equations and Their Applicability ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1["When is the membrane permeable to only ONE ion"] --> A1["Use NERNST EQUATION
Computes Eion"] Q2["When is the membrane permeable to MULTIPLE ions"] --> A2["Use GOLDMAN GHK EQUATION
Computes Vm"] Q3["When do you need ionic FLUX through channels"] --> A3["Use NERNST PLANCK EQUATION
Computes J mol per m2 per s"] Q4["When are charged proteins trapped on one side"] --> A4["Use DONNAN EQUILIBRIUM
Computes ion distribution ratio"] A1 --> R1["Single ion electrogenic transport
Example Cardiac pacemaker If current"] A2 --> R2["Resting membrane potential
Action potential reversal"] A3 --> R3["Channel current calculations
Patch clamp data analysis"] A4 --> R4["Cell volume regulation
Intracellular ion distribution"] style Q1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Q2 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Q3 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Q4 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style A1 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style A2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style A3 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style A4 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 1] **Define resting membrane potential. Explain its ionic basis in a typical neuron.** **Model Answer:** The **resting membrane potential (RMP)** is the steady-state voltage difference across the plasma membrane of a living cell when it is not generating an electrical signal, conventionally defined as inside relative to outside. For a typical neuron: **RMP ≈ −70 mV**. **Ionic basis:** - The intracellular fluid is rich in **K⁺ (140 mM)** and large anionic proteins (A⁻). - The extracellular fluid is rich in **Na⁺ (145 mM)** and **Cl⁻ (120 mM)**. - The membrane is **~25× more permeable to K⁺ than Na⁺** at rest. - K⁺ diffuses outward down its concentration gradient, leaving behind uncompensated negative charges (A⁻) → inside becomes negative. - The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** continuously maintains this gradient (3 Na⁺ out / 2 K⁺ in per ATP), making the pump directly responsible for ~5–10 mV of the RMP (electrogenic contribution). - The Nernst potential for K⁺ is −94 mV; the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz weighted sum gives the actual RMP of ~−70 mV. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 1] **Distinguish between active transport and passive transport. Give one example for each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Passive Transport | Active Transport | |---|---|---| | Driving force | Electrochemical gradient (downhill) | Directly/indirectly ATP (uphill) | | Energy requirement | None | ATP required | | Direction of net flux | High → Low concentration | Low → High concentration | | Carrier proteins | Channels or carriers; not pumps | Pumps (ATPases) or co-transporters | | Example | K⁺ leak channels (K⁺ efflux at rest) | Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump | | Saturability | Yes (for carriers) | Yes | **Additional example:** Secondary active transport of glucose via SGLT1 (Na⁺-glucose symporter in intestinal epithelium) is **active** because it uses the Na⁺ gradient established by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase**. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with KTU Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 1] **(a)** Derive the Nernst equation for the equilibrium potential of an ion across a cell membrane. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cardiac muscle cell has the following ionic concentrations: | Ion | Outside (mM) | Inside (mM) | |---|---|---| | K⁺ | 4 | 150 | | Na⁺ | 140 | 10 | | Cl⁻ | 100 | 5 | The relative permeabilities at rest are: $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.05 : 0.3$. Calculate the resting membrane potential using the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation** at 37 °C. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a): Derivation of Nernst Equation **Assumptions:** 1. The membrane is permeable to **only one ionic species** at a time. 2. The system has reached **electrochemical equilibrium** (ΔG = 0). 3. Temperature is constant (isothermal, T = 310 K for body). 4. The membrane is **uniform** and electrically neutral except for the surface charges. **Derivation:** The electrochemical potential of ion $X^{z+}$ in compartment j is: $$\tilde{\mu}_j = \mu^0 + RT \ln[X]_j + zF V_j$$ At equilibrium, $\tilde{\mu}_{inside} = \tilde{\mu}_{outside}$: $$\mu^0 + RT \ln[X]_i + zF V_i = \mu^0 + RT \ln[X]_o + zF V_o$$ Cancel $\mu^0$ and rearrange: $$zF(V_i - V_o) = RT \ln \frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$$ $$\boxed{E_{ion} = V_m = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i} = \frac{61 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b): GHK Calculation **Step 1:** Substitute into the GHK equation: $$V_m = 61 \text{ mV} \cdot \log_{10} \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the numerator: $$1(4) + 0.05(140) + 0.3(5) = 4 + 7 + 1.5 = 12.5$$ **Step 3:** Compute the denominator: $$1(150) + 0.05(10) + 0.3(100) = 150 + 0.5 + 30 = 180.5$$ **Step 4:** Compute the ratio: $$\frac{12.5}{180.5} = 0.06925$$ **Step 5:** Compute the logarithm: $$\log_{10}(0.06925) = -1.1594$$ **Step 6:** Compute Vm: $$V_m = 61 \times (-1.1594) = -70.7 \text{ mV}$$ $$\boxed{V_m \approx -70.7 \text{ mV}}$$ This matches the typical resting membrane potential of a cardiac ventricular cell. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 1] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** mechanism. Why is it called an **electrogenic pump**? **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is the **Donnan equilibrium**? A cell contains an impermeable anion $A^-$ at an intracellular concentration of 140 mM. The extracellular fluid contains only KCl at 4 mM. Calculate the equilibrium distribution of K⁺ and Cl⁻ across the membrane using the Donnan equations. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a): Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase Pump **Structure and Mechanism:** The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase is a P-type ATPase composed of: - A catalytic **α-subunit** (~1000 amino acids, 10 transmembrane helices) — contains Na⁺ and K⁺ binding sites plus the ATPase activity. - A regulatory **β-subunit** (essential for folding and trafficking). - A **γ-subunit** (FXYD protein) that modulates activity. **Working Cycle (See Block Diagram in Section 4.3):** 1. **E1 State (open to inside):** The pump binds 3 intracellular Na⁺ ions at high-affinity sites. 2. **ATP binding + Phosphorylation:** A terminal aspartate residue is phosphorylated by ATP; this drives a major conformational change. 3. **E2 State (open to outside):** The Na⁺ binding affinity drops; 3 Na⁺ are released to the extracellular side. 4. **K⁺ binding:** 2 extracellular K⁺ ions bind at newly exposed high-affinity sites. 5. **Dephosphorylation:** The aspartyl-phosphate is hydrolyzed → another conformational change. 6. **K⁺ release inside:** K⁺ affinity drops, 2 K⁺ released into the cytoplasm. The pump returns to E1 state. **Why Electrogenic:** - Per ATP, **3 positive charges exit** and only **2 positive charges enter**. - Net transfer = **+1 charge outward per cycle**. - This creates a net outward current → contributes directly to making the inside negative. - Contributes approximately **−5 to −10 mV** to the resting membrane potential. **Inhibitors:** Cardiac glycosides like **Ouabain** and **Digoxin** inhibit the pump → used to treat congestive heart failure by increasing intracellular Na⁺ → reducing the Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger activity → raising intracellular Ca²⁺ → stronger contraction. #### Model Solution — Part (b): Donnan Equilibrium **Concept:** **Donnan equilibrium** (Gibbs-Donnan effect) is the steady-state distribution of charged species across a membrane that is **impermeable to at least one charged species** (typically a large intracellular anion like a protein). The system settles such that: 1. **Electroneutrality** holds in both compartments. 2. The **electrochemical potential** of each permeant ion is equal on both sides. **Problem Statement:** - $[A^-]_i = 140$ mM (impermeable anion inside the cell). - Initial extracellular [KCl] = 4 mM (so $[K^+]_o^{initial} = [Cl^-]_o^{initial} = 4$ mM). **Step 1:** Apply electroneutrality in the extracellular compartment: $$[K^+]_o = [Cl^-]_o = C_o$$ **Step 2:** Apply electroneutrality inside: $$[K^+]_i = [Cl^-]_i + [A^-]_i = [Cl^-]_i + 140$$ **Step 3:** Apply the Donnan equilibrium condition for diffusible ions: $$[K^+]_i \cdot [Cl^-]_i = [K^+]_o \cdot [Cl^-]_o = C_o^2$$ **Step 4:** Solve simultaneously. Let $x = [Cl^-]_i$ inside. Then $[K^+]_i = x + 140$. $$(x + 140) \cdot x = C_o^2$$ **Step 5:** Assume most K⁺ enters the cell (anion is trapped). Let $C_o \approx 4$ mM. Check assumption: $16 = (x+140)x$. **Step 6:** Solve the quadratic $x^2 + 140x - 16 = 0$: $$x = \frac{-140 + \sqrt{140^2 + 4 \cdot 16}}{2} = \frac{-140 + \sqrt{19664}}{2}$$ $$x = \frac{-140 + 140.23}{2} \approx 0.114 \text{ mM}$$ **Step 7:** Calculate the concentrations: $$[Cl^-]_i \approx 0.114 \text{ mM}$$ $$[K^+]_i = 140 + 0.114 \approx 140.11 \text{ mM}$$ **Step 8:** Calculate the Donnan ratio: $$r = \frac{[K^+]_i}{[K^+]_o} = \frac{140.11}{4} \approx 35.03$$ $$r = \frac{[Cl^-]_o}{[Cl^-]_i} = \frac{4}{0.114} \approx 35.09 \;\; (\text{small rounding differences})$$ $$\boxed{[K^+]_i \approx 140.1 \text{ mM}, \quad [Cl^-]_i \approx 0.11 \text{ mM}, \quad \text{Donnan ratio} \approx 35}$$ **Step 9:** Check the Nernst potentials: $$E_{K^+} = 61 \log_{10} \frac{4}{140.1} = 61 \times (-1.544) \approx -94.2 \text{ mV}$$ $$E_{Cl^-} = -61 \log_{10} \frac{4}{0.11} = -61 \times (1.561) \approx -95.2 \text{ mV}$$ Both ions have **nearly equal Nernst potentials**, confirming that the Donnan equilibrium has been correctly reached. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Sign convention errors**: Forgetting that **z = −1 for Cl⁻** in the Nernst equation. Always include z in the denominator. KTU examiners specifically check for sign errors in $E_{Cl^-}$. > > 2. **Ratio inversion**: Students commonly write $\log_{10}([ion]_i / [ion]_o)$ instead of $[ion]_o / [ion]_i$. The Nernst potential requires **outside/inside** ratio. > > 3. **Confusing Nernst with GHK**: Nernst is for a *single* permeant ion. If the question gives permeability values, you **must** use the GHK equation. Examiners will deduct 4 marks if you use the wrong equation. > > 4. **Unit inconsistency**: Permeability ratios in GHK are *dimensionless ratios*, not absolute permeabilities. Never write the absolute permeability in the formula directly. > > 5. **Forgetting the Donnan condition**: When asked to compute ionic distribution with trapped anions, students forget that $[K^+]_i \neq [Cl^-]_i$ inside; the difference equals $[A^-]_i$. > > 6. **Miscounting the Na⁺/K⁺ stoichiometry**: The ratio is **3 out : 2 in**, NOT 2:3 or 1:1. Ouabain blocks this pump — common viva question. --- ## TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Important Things to Remember (Print-Friendly Summary)** - **Cell Membrane**: 7–10 nm lipid bilayer + embedded proteins; hydrophobic core blocks ions. - **Passive transport** (down gradient, no ATP): simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion, ion channels (leak, voltage-gated, ligand-gated, mechanically-gated). - **Active transport** (against gradient, ATP needed): primary (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, Ca²⁺-ATPase) and secondary (symport, antiport). - **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase**: 3 Na⁺ out / 2 K⁺ in per ATP → electrogenic → contributes to RMP. Inhibited by **ouabain** and **digoxin**. - **Resting Membrane Potential** ≈ **−70 mV**, determined primarily by K⁺ permeability. - **Nernst Equation** (single ion): $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}$. - **GHK Equation** (multiple ions): weighted sum of Nernst potentials by permeabilities. - **Nernst-Planck Equation**: gives ionic flux when both concentration gradient and electric field act on an ion. - **Donnan Equilibrium**: When a non-permeant anion is trapped, diffusible ions distribute unequally; the Donnan ratio $r = [K^+]_i/[K^+]_o = [Cl^-]_o/[Cl^-]_i$. - **Action Potential phases**: Rest → Depolarization (Na⁺ in) → Repolarization (K⁺ out) → Hyperpolarization → Rest. - **Threshold**: ≈ −55 mV for typical neurons. - **Peak of AP**: ≈ +30 to +40 mV (approaches $E_{Na}$). - **Standard concentrations**: K⁺(in 140, out 4); Na⁺(in 10, out 145); Cl⁻(in 4, out 120). - **Constants**: R = 8.314 J/mol·K, F = 96,485 C/mol, 2.303 RT/F at 37 °C = 61.5 mV. - **Engineering applications**: ECG/EEG/EMG, defibrillators, pacemakers, dialysis, biosensors, neural prosthetics, iontophoretic drug delivery. - **Medical relevance**: Hyperkalemia → cardiac arrhythmia; Digoxin therapy → +ve inotropy via Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase inhibition; Lidocaine blocks voltage-gated Na⁺ channels.

topics.title: Resting and Action Potentials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEETVT615 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resting and Action Potentials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** is the steady-state, trans-membrane voltage difference (typically **−70 mV** in a mammalian neuron) that exists across the plasma membrane of an excitable cell when it is *not* generating an electrical signal. By convention, the potential is reported as the intracellular potential *relative to* the extracellular fluid (which is grounded at 0 mV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The RMP is *not zero*. It is actively maintained by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (an electrogenic pump that expels 3 Na⁺ ions out and imports 2 K⁺ ions in per ATP hydrolyzed) and the differential permeability of the lipid bilayer to K⁺ versus Na⁺.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Charged Battery"
Think of a neuron at rest as a **tiny biological battery**:
- The **inside of the cell** acts as the **negative terminal**.
- The **outside** acts as the **positive terminal**.
- **K⁺ ions** are like eager passengers wanting to leave a crowded bus (high inside → low outside), pushing positive charge out.
- The **Na⁺/K⁺ pump** is the bouncer who continuously restores the imbalance by throwing out 3 Na⁺ for every 2 K⁺ let in, creating a net negative charge inside.

This stored electrochemical energy is what powers every nerve impulse, muscle contraction, and thought you have ever had.

---

## 1.2 Action Potential (AP)

### Formal Academic Definition
An **Action Potential** is a rapid, transient, all-or-nothing depolarization and repolarization of the membrane potential, propagating along an excitable cell's membrane. It is initiated when a stimulus drives the membrane potential past a critical **threshold (≈ −55 mV)**, causing voltage-gated Na⁺ channels to open en masse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Properties (Must Memorize for KTU ESE):**
> 1. **All-or-None Law:** Sub-threshold stimuli produce *no* AP; supra-threshold stimuli always produce a *full* AP.
> 2. **Self-Propagating:** Once initiated, the AP regenerates itself along the axon without loss of amplitude.
> 3. **Refractory Period:** A brief unexcitable phase (≈ 1–2 ms) limits the maximum firing frequency.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Domino Chain Reaction"
Imagine a row of dominos standing on edge:
- The **resting potential** = stable upright dominos (energy stored).
- A **threshold stimulus** = a finger that pushes the first domino hard enough.
- The **action potential** = the toppling cascade — once started, it cannot be half-done or stopped; it must complete.
- The **refractory period** = the time the dominos need to be stood up again before a new wave can pass.
- The **Na⁺/K⁺ pump** is the person who resets the entire line for the next round.

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Values (Memorize for Numericals)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Resting Potential ($V_m$) | **−70 mV** |
| Threshold Potential | **−55 mV** |
| Peak Depolarization (Na⁺ equilibrium) | **+30 to +40 mV** |
| After-Hyperpolarization | **−80 mV** |
| Action Potential Duration | **1–2 ms** (neuron) |
| Intracellular [K⁺] | **140 mM** |
| Extracellular [K⁺] | **4–5 mM** |
| Intracellular [Na⁺] | **10–15 mM** |
| Extracellular [Na⁺] | **145 mM** |
| Body Temperature | **310 K (37 °C)** |
| Faraday Constant ($F$) | **96,485 C/mol** |
| Gas Constant ($R$) | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Ionic Basis of the Resting Potential — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Ion Concentration Gradients (established by Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase):**
   - [K⁺] is **high inside** (~140 mM) and **low outside** (~5 mM).
   - [Na⁺] is **high outside** (~145 mM) and **low inside** (~15 mM).
   - [Cl⁻] is **high outside** and **low inside**.

2. **Differential Permeability at Rest:**
   - The membrane is **~25–100× more permeable to K⁺** than to Na⁺ (due to abundant *leak* K⁺ channels).
   - Therefore, K⁺ tends to diffuse *outward* down its chemical gradient.

3. **Charge Separation:**
   - As K⁺ leaves, the inside becomes **negatively charged** (Cl⁻ and organic anions cannot follow).
   - This electrical gradient opposes further K⁺ efflux.

4. **Dynamic Equilibrium (Nernst/Donnan balance):**
   - The system reaches a steady state when the **chemical driving force** (concentration gradient) is exactly balanced by the **electrical driving force** (membrane potential).
   - The potential at which this occurs for a single ion is the **Nernst Potential** ($E_{ion}$).

5. **Steady-State Result:**
   - For a real cell with multiple permeant ions, the resulting RMP is given by the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation**.

---

## 2.2 Ionic Basis of the Action Potential — Phase-by-Phase Logic

| Phase | Membrane Event | Dominant Ion | Channel Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resting** | −70 mV stable | K⁺ leak | Leak K⁺ open; Na⁺ closed |
| **Depolarization (to threshold)** | Stimulus-driven | Na⁺ | Voltage-gated Na⁺ activation gates open |
| **Rapid Depolarization** | −55 mV → +30 mV | Na⁺ influx | Na⁺ activation open; inactivation still open |
| **Repolarization** | +30 mV → −70 mV | K⁺ efflux | Na⁺ inactivation gates close; K⁺ delayed rectifiers open |
| **Hyperpolarization (undershoot)** | −70 mV → −80 mV | K⁺ efflux | K⁺ channels slow to close |
| **Return to Rest** | Restored to −70 mV | Pump | Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase restores gradients |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The *threshold* is determined by the balance of Na⁺ and K⁺ conductances, NOT by a fixed channel. Once Na⁺ conductance ($g_{Na}$) exceeds K⁺ conductance ($g_K$) by a critical ratio, the regenerative feedback loop ensures full AP.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nernst Equation** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | $E_{ion}$ in volts; $z$ = ion charge; $F$ = 96485 C/mol | Equilibrium potential of a *single* permeant ion |
| 2 | **Nernst (37 °C, log₁₀)** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ mV | Same as above | Fast numerical computation |
| 3 | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage** | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | $P$ = permeability (m/s) | RMP with *multiple* permeant ions |
| 4 | **Hodgkin-Huxley Membrane Current** | $I = C_m\dfrac{dV}{dt} + \bar{g}_K n^4 (V-V_K) + \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V-V_{Na}) + g_L (V-V_L)$ | $g$ = conductance (S); $m,h,n$ = gating variables (0→1) | Quantitative AP model |
| 5 | **Gating Variable Rate Eq.** | $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x (1-x) - \beta_x x$ | $\alpha, \beta$ = rate constants (1/s) | Channel kinetics |
| 6 | **Nernst-Planck Flux** | $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx} + \dfrac{zFDC}{RT} \dfrac{dV}{dx}$ | $J$ = mol/(m²·s); $D$ = m²/s | Diffusive + electric flux |
| 7 | **Time Constant (Membrane)** | $\tau = R_m C_m$ | $R_m$ = Ω·cm²; $C_m$ = F/cm² | How fast voltage changes |
| 8 | **Conduction Velocity (Unmyelinated)** | $v \propto \sqrt{r \cdot g_{Na}}$ | $r$ = axon radius | Saltatory vs continuous |
| 9 | **Conduction Velocity (Myelinated)** | $v \propto r$ | $r$ = axon radius | Saltatory conduction |
| 10 | **Strength-Duration (Weiss) Law** | $I_{thr} = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau}}$ | $I_{rh}$ = rheobase | Threshold excitability |
| 11 | **Nernst for K⁺ (typical)** | $E_K \approx -94$ mV | — | Permeable mostly to K⁺ |
| 12 | **Nernst for Na⁺ (typical)** | $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV | — | Drives depolarization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Hodgkin-Huxley framework is the foundation of modern **neuroprosthetics**, **deep brain stimulators (DBS)**, **ECG/EEG signal modeling**, and **cardiac pacemaker design**. Engineers use the GHK equation to model cardiac cell RMP for arrhythmia prediction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation from Thermodynamics

**Starting Premise:** At electrochemical equilibrium, the **electrochemical potential** of an ion must be equal on both sides of the membrane.

The electrochemical potential $\tilde{\mu}$ of an ion in a solution of concentration $C$ and electric potential $V$ is:

$$\tilde{\mu} = \mu^0 + RT \ln(C) + zFV$$

**Step 1 — Apply equilibrium condition** ($\tilde{\mu}_{in} = \tilde{\mu}_{out}$):

$$\mu^0 + RT \ln([ion]_{in}) + zF V_{in} = \mu^0 + RT \ln([ion]_{out}) + zF V_{out}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel $\mu^0$ from both sides:**

$$RT \ln([ion]_{in}) + zF V_{in} = RT \ln([ion]_{out}) + zF V_{out}$$

**Step 3 — Collect $V$ terms on the left, $RT$ terms on the right:**

$$zF (V_{in} - V_{out}) = RT \ln([ion]_{out}) - RT \ln([ion]_{in})$$

**Step 4 — Factor $RT$ on the right using log identity $\ln(a) - \ln(b) = \ln(a/b)$:**

$$zF (V_{in} - V_{out}) = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $(V_{in} - V_{out})$, which by definition is the Nernst potential $E_{ion}$:**

$$E_{ion} = V_{in} - V_{out} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Numerical form at body temperature (T = 310 K):**
Substitute $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 310$ K, $F = 96485$ C/mol, and convert $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ by dividing by 2.303:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{z \times 96485 \times 2.303} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right) \times 1000 \text{ mV}$$

$$E_{ion} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right) \text{ mV}$$

> **This is the form KTU expects in numerical problems.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Nernst Potential for K⁺

**Given:**
- $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM, $z = +1$, $T = 37$ °C.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the 61.5 form:**

$$E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{140}\right) \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{4}{140} = 0.02857$$

**Step 3 — Take log₁₀:**

$$\log_{10}(0.02857) = -1.544$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 61.5:**

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.544) = -94.96 \text{ mV}$$

**Answer:** $E_K \approx -95$ mV, which closely matches the literature value. This means at −95 mV, the K⁺ efflux (chemical) is exactly balanced by K⁺ influx (electrical).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical: Nernst Potential for Na⁺

**Given:** $[Na^+]_{in} = 15$ mM, $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ mM, $z = +1$.

**Step 1 — Substitute:**

$$E_{Na} = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{145}{15}\right) \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute ratio:**

$$\frac{145}{15} = 9.667$$

**Step 3 — Take log₁₀:**

$$\log_{10}(9.667) = 0.9854$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 61.5:**

$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \times 0.9854 = +60.60 \text{ mV}$$

**Answer:** $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV. This is why during an AP, the membrane potential *shoots toward* but does not quite reach +60 mV (Na⁺ channels begin to inactivate).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Resting Potential via GHK

**Given:**
- $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$ (typical squid axon)
- $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM
- $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM, $[Na^+]_i = 15$ mM
- $[Cl^-]_o = 120$ mM, $[Cl^-]_i = 4$ mM

**Step 1 — Apply GHK at 37 °C (61.5 form):**

$$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator:**

$$N = (1)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)$$
$$N = 4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$D = (1)(140) + (0.04)(15) + (0.45)(120)$$
$$D = 140 + 0.6 + 54 = 194.6$$

**Step 4 — Take ratio and log:**

$$\frac{N}{D} = \frac{11.6}{194.6} = 0.0596$$

$$\log_{10}(0.0596) = -1.2248$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 61.5:**

$$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.2248) = -75.3 \text{ mV}$$

**Answer:** $V_m \approx -75$ mV, which is in excellent agreement with measured RMPs of squid giant axon. The small discrepancy from −70 mV is due to assumptions in permeability ratios.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: AP Phase vs. Channel State

| Phase | Time (ms) | $V_m$ (mV) | $g_{Na}$ | $g_K$ | Net Current |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resting | 0 | −70 | Low | Moderate | 0 (steady state) |
| Approach to threshold | 0.1 | −60 | Rising | Falling | Inward (depolarizing) |
| Threshold crossed | 0.2 | −55 | High | Moderate | Strong inward |
| Peak depolarization | 0.5 | +30 | **Inactivating** | **Rising** | 0 (transition) |
| Repolarization start | 0.7 | 0 | Closed (inactivated) | High | Outward (hyperpolarizing) |
| After-hyperpolarization | 1.5 | −80 | Closed (de-inactivating) | High (lingering) | Outward |
| Return to rest | 3.0 | −70 | Restored | Restored | Pump restoring gradients |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Action Potential Generation Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stimulus Applied"] --> B["Membrane depolarizes toward threshold"]
    B --> C{"Reaches threshold -55 mV?"}
    C -->|No| D["Subthreshold response: local graded potential decays"]
    C -->|Yes| E["Voltage gated Na channels activation gates open"]
    E --> F["Na ions rush IN: rapid depolarization toward plus 30 mV"]
    F --> G["Na channels inactivation gates close at peak"]
    G --> H["Voltage gated K channels open: K ions flow OUT"]
    H --> I["Repolarization back toward -70 mV"]
    I --> J["K channels remain open: hyperpolarization to -80 mV"]
    J --> K["Na K ATPase pump restores ion gradients"]
    K --> L["Membrane returns to resting -70 mV"]
    L --> A

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#C9E4CA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Ionic Pump and Leak Channels at Rest

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OUTSIDE["Extracellular Space"]
        NAOUT["Na plus 145 mM"]
        KOUT["K plus 5 mM"]
    end

    subgraph MEMBRANE["Plasma Membrane"]
        NAKPUMP["Na K ATPase Pump: 3 Na OUT per 2 K IN per ATP"]
        KLEAK["K Leak Channel: K plus flows OUT down gradient"]
        NAVG["Voltage Gated Na Channel: CLOSED at rest"]
        KVG["Voltage Gated K Channel: CLOSED at rest"]
    end

    subgraph INSIDE["Intracellular Space"]
        NAIN["Na plus 15 mM"]
        KIN["K plus 140 mM"]
        ANION["Organic Anions minus: large impermeant negative charge"]
    end

    NAKPUMP -->|pumps Na out| OUTSIDE
    OUTSIDE -->|pumps K in| NAKPUMP
    KLEAK -->|leakage| OUTSIDE
    ANION -.->|trapped inside| INSIDE
    OUTSIDE -->|resting Vm minus 70 mV| INSIDE

    style NAKPUMP fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style KLEAK fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style NAVG fill:#95A5A6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style KVG fill:#95A5A6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ANION fill:#F7DC6F,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Refractory Period Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Action Potential Fired"] --> B["Absolute Refractory Period 0 to 1 ms"]
    B --> C["No stimulus can elicit AP: Na channels inactivated"]
    C --> D["Relative Refractory Period 1 to 3 ms"]
    D --> E{"Stimulus strength?"}
    E -->|Stronger than normal| F["AP possible: K channels still partially open"]
    E -->|Normal stimulus| G["No AP: membrane still hyperpolarized"]
    F --> H["Full recovery: all channels back to resting state"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Cell ready for next action potential"]

    style B fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture: Hodgkin-Huxley Computational Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Stimulus Current I stim t"] --> B["Membrane Capacitance Cm dV/dt"]
    B --> C["Membrane Potential V m t"]
    C --> D["Na Conductance: g Na m cubed h times V minus V Na"]
    C --> E["K Conductance: g K n to the fourth times V minus V K"]
    C --> F["Leak Conductance: g L times V minus V L"]
    D --> G["Total Ionic Current I ion"]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["Feedback to Membrane Equation: I stim minus I ion"]
    H --> B

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**"Define Resting Membrane Potential. Mention the role of the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump in maintaining it."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Resting Membrane Potential is the steady-state voltage difference across the plasma membrane of an excitable cell in the absence of any external stimulus. It is typically **−70 mV** (inside negative relative to outside).
- **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase role (2 marks):** The pump hydrolyses **1 ATP** to expel **3 Na⁺** ions out of the cell and import **2 K⁺** ions into the cell. This (a) maintains the **concentration gradients** essential for RMP, and (b) contributes a direct **electrogenic effect** of **−3 to −5 mV** to the resting potential (net loss of one positive charge per cycle).

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**"List the four phases of an Action Potential. State the threshold value."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Resting / Depolarization-to-threshold** — stimulus brings $V_m$ to **−55 mV** (1 mark).
2. **Rapid Depolarization** — voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open, $V_m$ rises to **+30 mV** (1 mark).
3. **Repolarization** — Na⁺ inactivates, voltage-gated K⁺ channels open, $V_m$ returns toward **−70 mV** (½ mark).
4. **Hyperpolarization (Undershoot)** — K⁺ channels remain open, $V_m$ dips to **−80 mV** before pump restores resting state (½ mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** Students often write "−50 mV" or "−65 mV" as threshold. The *accepted* KTU textbook value is **−55 mV**. Writing the wrong threshold is a 1-mark penalty trap.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**"With a neat diagram, explain the ionic basis of the Resting Membrane Potential. Derive the Nernst equation and the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz voltage equation. Compute $E_K$ and $E_{Na}$ at 37 °C for the following data: $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM; $[Na^+]_i = 15$ mM, $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM."**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Ionic Basis of RMP (7 marks)**

1. **Concentration Gradients (1 mark):** The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump establishes high [K⁺] inside (~140 mM) and low [K⁺] outside (~5 mM); reverse for Na⁺ (15 mM inside, 145 mM outside).

2. **Differential Permeability (1 mark):** At rest, the membrane is **~25 times more permeable to K⁺** than to Na⁺ due to abundant *leak* K⁺ channels. Therefore K⁺ diffuses outward, leaving the interior electrically negative.

3. **Donnan-type Equilibrium (1 mark):** Organic anions (proteins, phosphates) are impermeant and trapped inside, contributing fixed negative charge. K⁺ efflux continues until electrical attraction balances the chemical gradient.

4. **Na⁺/K⁺ Pump (2 marks):** Continuously expels 3 Na⁺ out and brings 2 K⁺ in per ATP, producing:
   - Maintenance of gradients.
   - Net electrogenic contribution of about −4 mV.

5. **Steady-state result (2 marks):** The RMP (≈ −70 mV) lies **closer to $E_K$ (−95 mV) than to $E_{Na}$ (+60 mV)** because of higher K⁺ permeability. This is quantitatively described by the GHK equation.

**Part (b) — Derivation of Nernst + Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Nernst Derivation (3 marks):** *(Same as Section 3.1 of these notes — show the 5-step derivation from electrochemical potential equality.)*

**Numerical Computation (4 marks):**

- **For $E_K$ (2 marks):**
  $$E_K = 61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{140}\right) = 61.5 \times (-1.544) \approx -95 \text{ mV}$$

- **For $E_{Na}$ (2 marks):**
  $$E_{Na} = 61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{145}{15}\right) = 61.5 \times (0.9854) \approx +60.6 \text{ mV}$$

- **Conclusion:** The RMP of −70 mV lies between $E_K$ and $E_{Na}$, confirming K⁺ dominance at rest.

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**"Describe the phases of an Action Potential with suitable voltage-time graph. Explain the role of voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels, the all-or-none law, and the absolute and relative refractory periods."**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Phases of Action Potential (7 marks)**

1. **Resting State (½ mark):** $V_m = -70$ mV; voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels closed.

2. **Depolarization to Threshold (1 mark):** Stimulus raises $V_m$ from −70 mV to threshold (−55 mV). Subthreshold = local graded potential; supra-threshold = AP triggered.

3. **Rapid Depolarization (1 mark):** Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels' **activation (m) gates** open → Na⁺ influx → $V_m$ rises rapidly to **+30 mV** (approaches $E_{Na} = +60$ mV but does not reach it).

4. **Repolarization (1 mark):** Na⁺ channels' **inactivation (h) gates** close at peak; **delayed-rectifier K⁺ channels** open → K⁺ efflux → $V_m$ falls.

5. **Hyperpolarization (½ mark):** K⁺ channels close slowly; $V_m$ briefly undershoots to **−80 mV** (closer to $E_K$).

6. **Return to Rest (½ mark):** Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase restores ionic gradients; membrane returns to −70 mV.

7. **Voltage-Time Graph (2 marks):** Draw a labeled diagram showing all five phases on the x-axis (time in ms) and y-axis ($V_m$ in mV), with threshold, peak, and hyperpolarization marked.

**Part (b) — Channel Roles, All-or-None, and Refractory Periods (7 marks)**

1. **Voltage-Gated Na⁺ Channels (2 marks):** Have **two gates**:
   - **m (activation) gate** — opens rapidly upon depolarization.
   - **h (inactivation) gate** — closes slowly, ~0.5 ms after m opens.
   - These give rise to the Hodgkin-Huxley $m^3h$ kinetics.

2. **Voltage-Gated K⁺ Channels (1 mark):** Have a single **n (activation) gate** that opens *slowly* in response to depolarization. They do *not* inactivate quickly — causing the hyperpolarization overshoot.

3. **All-or-None Law (2 marks):**
   - **Definition:** An AP either fires completely or not at all.
   - **Mechanism:** Once threshold is reached, the regenerative Na⁺-positive-feedback loop guarantees a full-sized AP. Stimulus intensity affects *frequency*, not *amplitude* of APs.

4. **Refractory Periods (2 marks):**
   - **Absolute Refractory Period (~0.5–1 ms):** Na⁺ channels are inactivated; no stimulus, no matter how strong, can elicit another AP. This enforces **unidirectional propagation**.
   - **Relative Refractory Period (~1–3 ms):** Na⁺ channels have recovered, but K⁺ channels are still open and $V_m$ is hyperpolarized; only a **supra-normal stimulus** can trigger an AP. This limits the **maximum firing frequency**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (7-mark subparts):**
> 1. **Don't confuse "threshold" with "all-or-none"!** Many students write "all-or-none means threshold" — this loses 1 mark. All-or-none is a *property of the propagated AP*, threshold is a *voltage level*.
> 2. **Forgetting the absolute/relative distinction in refractory periods** — examiners specifically test this. State BOTH the channel state AND the stimulus requirement.
> 3. **Skipping the voltage-time graph** in Question B costs a full 2 marks. Even a hand-drawn rough sketch with axis labels and 4 phase markers earns 1.5 marks.
> 4. **Sign errors in Nernst equation numericals** — putting 140/4 instead of 4/140 is the most common error (gives +95 mV instead of −95 mV for K⁺).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These:**

- ⭐ **RMP value:** **−70 mV** (inside negative).
- ⭐ **Threshold:** **−55 mV**; **Peak AP:** **+30 mV**; **Undershoot:** **−80 mV**.
- ⭐ **Na⁺/K⁺ Pump stoichiometry:** **3 Na⁺ OUT : 2 K⁺ IN : 1 ATP hydrolyzed**.
- ⭐ **Nernst Equation (37 °C):** $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ **mV**.
- ⭐ **GHK Voltage Equation:** Includes $P_K$, $P_{Na}$, $P_{Cl}$ permeabilities — at rest, $P_K \gg P_{Na}$, so $V_m \approx E_K$.
- ⭐ **AP Phases (in order):** Rest → Depolarization → Peak → Repolarization → Hyperpolarization → Rest.
- ⭐ **Na⁺ channel gates:** m (activation, fast) + h (inactivation, slow).
- ⭐ **K⁺ channel gate:** n (activation, slow).
- ⭐ **All-or-None:** Initiated at threshold; full AP or none.
- ⭐ **Absolute Refractory Period:** Na⁺ inactivated → no AP possible.
- ⭐ **Relative Refractory Period:** Stronger-than-normal stimulus needed.
- ⭐ **E_K ≈ −95 mV; E_{Na} ≈ +60 mV; E_{Cl} ≈ −70 mV** (memorize all three).
- ⭐ **Hodgkin-Huxley form:** $I = C_m \dfrac{dV}{dt} + g_K n^4 (V-V_K) + g_{Na} m^3 h (V-V_{Na}) + g_L (V-V_L)$.
- ⭐ **Threshold criterion:** $g_{Na}$ must exceed $g_K$ by a critical ratio.
- ⭐ **Engineering Applications:** ECG/EEG modeling, neuroprosthetics, cardiac pacemakers, deep brain stimulators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio-Electric Potentials and Electrode Theory: Electrode Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEETVT615 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bio-Electric Potentials and Electrode Theory: Electrode Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrode Potential** (also called **Half-Cell Potential** or **Electrode-Electrolyte Interface Potential**) is defined as the electrical potential difference established at the boundary between a metal (electronic conductor) and an electrolyte (ionic conductor) when they are placed in contact. This potential arises due to the transfer of charge carriers across the phase boundary, producing a charge separation that forms an **electrical double layer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Electrode potential is the foundational concept for understanding all bioelectric signal acquisition (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG). Every measurement of biopotential ultimately depends on how two electrodes interact with body electrolytes.

**Mathematical Symbolism:**
- $E$ → Electrode (half-cell) potential measured in Volts (V) or millivolts (mV)
- $E^0$ → Standard electrode potential (measured at standard conditions: 25 °C, 1 M concentration, 1 atm pressure)
- $R$ → Universal Gas Constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $F$ → Faraday's Constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $T$ → Absolute Temperature in Kelvin
- $n$ → Number of electrons transferred per ion
- $[\text{Ox}]$, $[\text{Red}]$ → Molar activities of oxidized and reduced species

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Two-City Trade Border"**

Imagine two neighboring cities, City-Metal (the electrode) and City-Electrolyte (the body fluid). Metal atoms in the electrode are like restless citizens who want to escape into the liquid to become dissolved ions. Meanwhile, ions in the electrolyte want to grab electrons from the metal and plate themselves onto it.

At the **border crossing (the interface)**, this tug-of-war creates a queue — electrons piling up on one side and positive ions on the other. This "queue" is the **electrical double layer**, and the resulting tension at the border is exactly the **electrode potential**.

The stronger the metal's tendency to dissolve (e.g., zinc, magnesium), the more negative its electrode potential. The weaker this tendency (e.g., gold, platinum), the more positive the potential.

**Biomedical context:** When an ECG electrode (Ag/AgCl) touches the skin, the body's sweat acts as the electrolyte. The ion exchange creates a stable, predictable half-cell voltage — this becomes the reference baseline against which cardiac signals are measured.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants at a Glance

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Faraday's Constant | $F$ | **96,485** | C/mol |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Standard Temperature | $T$ | **298.15 (≈ 25)** | K / °C |
| Thermal Voltage ($RT/F$) at 25 °C | $V_T$ | **0.02569** | V |
| Logarithmic factor at 25 °C | $2.303 \, RT/F$ | **0.0591** | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At body temperature ($T = 310\,K$, i.e., 37 °C), the thermal voltage becomes $V_T \approx 26.71\,$mV, and the logarithmic conversion factor becomes $\approx 0.0615\,$V. **KTU examiners frequently test this temperature-dependent substitution!**
