- **ECG signal:** 0.5–5 mV, 0.05–150 Hz; uses 10 electrodes for 12 leads; Einthoven's law: $V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$.
- **ECG front-end essentials:** INA (CMRR > 100 dB, $Z_{in} >$ 10 M$\Omega$), Right Leg Driver, 50 Hz notch, 1 mV calibration.
- **EEG:** Uses 10-20 system; five bands: delta (0.5–4), theta (4–8), alpha (8–13), beta (13–30), gamma (>30 Hz).
- **EEG amplifier:** Gain ~ 20,000; bandwidth 0.1–100 Hz; noise < 0.5 $\mu$V.
- **Evoked Potentials:** 1–10 $\mu$V signals extracted from EEG by **signal averaging**.
- **SNR improvement:** $SNR_{out} = \sqrt{N}\cdot SNR_{in}$ (amplitude), or $= N\cdot SNR_{in}$ (power).
- **Types of EPs:** VEP (visual), BAER (auditory brainstem), SSEP (somatosensory), MEP (motor).
- **Skin-electrode model:** Polarization impedance $Z = \frac{R_s}{1+j\omega R_s C_d}$.
- **Wilson Central Terminal:** $V_{WCT} = (V_{RA}+V_{LA}+V_{LL})/3$ — reference for chest leads.
- **Filter cutoffs:** HPF @ 0.05 Hz (ECG), 0.1 Hz (EEG); LPF @ 150 Hz (ECG), 100 Hz (EEG); Notch @ 50 Hz (India).
- **Clinical gems:** Alpha blocking (Berger effect), Delta in awake adult = pathology, BAER for newborn hearing screening, VEP for MS diagnosis.
- **Safety:** Patient isolation via **optocouplers / transformers**, defibrillator protection, current-limiting resistors to keep leakage < 10 $\mu$A.
- **Modern trend:** Same block diagram is now realized in **ADS129x series ICs** for portable/wearable ECG and EEG devices — same principles, smaller footprint.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brain Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEETVT615 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Brain Waves # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Brain Waves > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > **Brain Waves (Electroencephalographic Signals / EEG):** Brain waves are rhythmic, oscillatory electrical potential fluctuations generated by the **synchronized synaptic activity of billions of cortical neurons**, primarily pyramidal cells in the cerebral cortex, which are recordable from the scalp surface using surface electrodes. The diagnostic technique used to capture these signals is called **Electroencephalography (EEG)**, and the resulting time-varying voltage trace is the **electroencephalogram**. The signal is typically characterized by three fundamental parameters: - **Frequency ($f$):** measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, ranging from sub-Hz (DC drifts) up to ~**100 Hz** for clinical EEG. - **Amplitude ($A$):** measured in **microvolts (µV)**, typically **10 µV to 300 µV** at scalp electrodes. - **Morphology / Waveform shape:** spatial and temporal distribution pattern. > [!NOTE] **Historical Note for KTU Examinations** > The EEG was first recorded in humans by **Hans Berger** (a German psychiatrist) in **1924**. Berger is universally recognized as the *father of the EEG*. He named the rhythmic 8–13 Hz signal the **"alpha wave"** and the faster 13–30 Hz signal the **"beta wave"**. Examiners frequently award 1–2 marks for this historical fact. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Orchestra of the Cortex" Imagine the human brain as a **massive orchestra with 86 billion neurons (the musicians)**. No single violinist can be heard above the audience. But when a large section (e.g., the string section = **pyramidal neurons of the cortex**) plays in synchronized rhythm, the combined sound is loud enough to be heard even from the balcony (= **the scalp surface**). - **Brain wave = the audible hum of the orchestra section** (only the synchronized *sum* of millions of post-synaptic potentials reaches the surface). - **Frequency of brain wave = tempo** of that section (fast = excited/Beta, slow = sleepy/Delta). - **Amplitude of brain wave = loudness** (more neurons in sync = louder signal). If musicians play out of sync (desynchronized), the hum is faint — that is exactly why **Beta waves (active thinking) have lower amplitude** than **Delta waves (deep sleep)**: in deep sleep, cortical neurons are *more synchronized* than during active wakefulness. ## 1.3 Origin of the EEG Signal The EEG signal is generated by: 1. **Excitatory and Inhibitory Post-Synaptic Potentials (EPSPs & IPSPs)** of **cortical pyramidal neurons** (layer IV and V of the cerebral cortex). 2. **Dipole formation:** When a pyramidal cell is excited, current flows from the apical dendrite (extracellularly) to the soma, creating a measurable dipole oriented perpendicular to the cortical surface. 3. **Volume conduction** through skull and scalp to the surface electrodes. > [!IMPORTANT] **Key Point** > Action potentials (spikes) are *too brief and asynchronous* to summate at the scalp. Hence **only graded post-synaptic potentials** — which last tens of milliseconds — produce the slow oscillations we call brain waves. ## 1.4 The 10–20 International Electrode Placement System To record EEG, electrodes are placed on the scalp in a standardized pattern called the **International 10–20 System** (Jasper, 1958). The "10" and "20" refer to the fact that inter-electrode distances are either 10% or 20% of the total front-to-back or right-to-left distance of the skull. | Region | Electrode Label | Function | |---|---|---| | Frontal pole | Fp1, Fp2 | Frontal lobe activity | | Frontal | F3, F4, Fz | Executive function | | Central | C3, C4, Cz | Motor / sensory cortex | | Parietal | P3, P4, Pz | Sensory integration | | Occipital | O1, O2, Oz | Visual processing (alpha rhythm) | | Temporal | T3, T4, T5, T6 | Auditory / memory | | Reference | A1, A2 | Ear-lobe reference | | Ground | Fpz | Earth / ground electrode | A standard clinical EEG uses **21 electrodes** (with newer systems 32, 64, 128, or 256 channels). Each electrode pair is called a **derivation** or **montage**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Normal Brain Waves Brain waves are categorized into **five canonical frequency bands** based on their frequency, amplitude, and the brain state in which they predominate. Memorization of these bands is a **favourite 3-mark and 5-mark KTU question**. ### 2.1.1 Delta Waves (δ) - **Frequency range:** 0.5 – 4 Hz - **Amplitude:** 20 – 200 µV (highest of all bands) - **State:** Deep, dreamless **slow-wave sleep (SWS)** / N3 stage of NREM sleep; also seen in infants and during deep anesthesia. - **Origin:** Thalamocortical neurons, subcortical structures. - **Clinical significance:** Pathological delta in awake adults = **focal brain lesion**, **tumour**, **encephalopathy**, or **deep anaesthesia**. ### 2.1.2 Theta Waves (θ) - **Frequency range:** 4 – 8 Hz - **Amplitude:** 5 – 20 µV (often >20 µV in children) - **State:** Light sleep (N1, N2 NREM), **drowsiness**, deep meditation, creative / emotional states. - **Origin:** Hippocampus (memory formation), frontal-midline. - **Clinical significance:** Exaggerated theta seen in **focal subcortical lesions**, **mild head injury**, and certain forms of **epilepsy**. ### 2.1.3 Alpha Waves (α) - **Frequency range:** 8 – 13 Hz - **Amplitude:** 20 – 60 µV - **State:** **Relaxed wakefulness with eyes closed**; attenuated by eye-opening (**alpha blocking** or **Berger effect**). - **Origin:** Occipital lobe (visual cortex, Brodmann area 17–19), thalamus. - **Clinical significance:** Posterior dominant alpha rhythm is the hallmark of a healthy awake adult. **Alpha coma** (generalized alpha in coma) indicates poor prognosis (e.g., anoxic brain injury, pontine lesions). ### 2.1.4 Beta Waves (β) - **Frequency range:** 13 – 30 Hz - **Amplitude:** 2 – 20 µV (low amplitude) - **State:** **Active mental activity**, focused attention, problem-solving, anxiety, REM sleep. - **Origin:** Frontal and central regions. - **Sub-classes:** **Beta 1 (13–15 Hz)**, **Beta 2 (15–20 Hz)**, **Beta 3 (>20 Hz)**. - **Clinical significance:** **Frontal intermittent rhythmic delta activity (FIRDA)** and excess beta are seen in **sedative-hypnotic drug use (e.g., benzodiazepines)**. ### 2.1.5 Gamma Waves (γ) - **Frequency range:** > 30 Hz (typically 30 – 100 Hz) - **Amplitude:** < 2 µV (very low; requires high-resolution amplifiers) - **State:** Higher cognitive processing, **conscious perception**, cross-modal sensory binding, attention, memory retrieval. - **Origin:** Widespread cortical networks. - **Clinical significance:** Reduced gamma is implicated in **Alzheimer's disease, schizophrenia, and autism spectrum disorder** (research-grade marker). > [!NOTE] **Sensory Rhythm (SMR) — Bonus Point for KTU** > A subset of beta activity at **12–15 Hz over the sensorimotor cortex** is termed the **sensorimotor rhythm (SMR / mu rhythm)**. It is desynchronized by movement or the *intention to move* and forms the basis of **BCI (Brain-Computer Interface)** motor-imagery paradigms. ## 2.2 Mechanism of Wave Generation — The Thalamocortical Loop The rhythmicity of EEG waves is **not intrinsic to the cortex alone**. The **thalamus acts as a pacemaker**: 1. Thalamic relay neurons (e.g., thalamic reticular nucleus) deliver **GABAergic inhibitory bursts** to cortical pyramidal cells. 2. This inhibition produces an **IPSP** in cortical cells → rebound excitation → next burst. 3. The cycle of **inhibition–rebound** at frequencies determined by the **after-hyperpolarization kinetics** of thalamic neurons produces the rhythmic EEG. > [!IMPORTANT] During **deep sleep**, the thalamus acts as a slow pacemaker → **Delta (0.5–4 Hz)**. > During **relaxed wakefulness**, the thalamus is depolarized → **Alpha (8–13 Hz)**. > During **active cognition**, the thalamus is gated by ascending reticular activating system → **Beta / Gamma**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Typical Value | |---|---|---| | Frequency band limits | $f_{\text{low}} \le f \le f_{\text{high}}$ | Hz | | Period of wave | $T = 1 / f$ | seconds (s) | | Average power of EEG band | $P = \dfrac{1}{T}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt$ | µV² | | RMS amplitude | $V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt}$ | µV | | Skin–electrode impedance (target) | $Z \le 5\ \text{k}\Omega$ | kilo-ohms | | Sampling rate (clinical) | $f_s \ge 200\ \text{Hz}$ (often 256 or 512 Hz) | Hz | | Low-cut (high-pass) filter | $f_c \le 0.5\ \text{Hz}$ | Hz | | High-cut (low-pass) filter | $f_c \ge 70\ \text{Hz}$ (clinical) | Hz | | Notch filter (line noise) | $f_n = 50\ \text{Hz}$ (India/Europe) / $60\ \text{Hz}$ (US) | Hz | | Spectral density (band) | $\text{PSD}(f) = \vert \mathcal{F}\{v(t)\}\vert^{2}$ | µV²/Hz | | Nyquist criterion | $f_s \ge 2\,f_{\text{max}}$ | Hz | | Bandwidth (for a band) | $\text{BW} = f_{\text{high}} - f_{\text{low}}$ | Hz | | Delta / Theta ratio (DTR) | $\text{DTR} = \dfrac{\sum \delta\text{-power}}{\sum \theta\text{-power}}$ | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] **CRITICAL — KTU Examiner's Trap** > Do not write `|x|` in any table row. In your answer sheet you may write the absolute value of $x$ as $\vert x \vert$ or use the magnitude operator. Examiners explicitly mark down the use of bare vertical bars in formulae. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of EEG / Brain Waves | Engineering Domain | Specific Application | |---|---| | **Clinical neurology** | Epilepsy diagnosis, sleep-disorder study, depth of anaesthesia (BIS), coma assessment | | **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** | Motor-imagery driven wheelchairs, spellers, robotic arms | | **Neuroergonomics** | Cognitive-load monitoring in pilots / drivers via frontal-theta power | | **Psychiatry** | Objective biomarkers for depression, ADHD, schizophrenia | | **Rehabilitation** | Neurofeedback therapy for ADHD, PTSD, stroke recovery | | **Forensics & lie-detection** | P300-based Guilty Knowledge Test (research) | | **Research** | Event-Related Potentials (ERP) — P300, N400, MMN | | **Consumer wearables** | Single-channel dry-electrode EEG headbands (Muse, Emotiv) for meditation tracking | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Period of Each Brain Wave Band The fundamental relationship between **frequency $f$** and **period $T$** of any periodic waveform is: $$ T = \frac{1}{f} $$ For a **band-limited** signal, the **central frequency** $f_c$ and the **central period** $T_c$ are given by the geometric mean of the band limits. The derivation proceeds as follows. $$ \begin{aligned} f_c &= \sqrt{f_{\text{low}} \cdot f_{\text{high}}} \quad &\text{(1) Definition of central frequency} \\ T_c &= \frac{1}{f_c} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{f_{\text{low}} \cdot f_{\text{high}}}} \quad &\text{(2) Inversion of frequency} \\ T_c &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{f_{\text{low}} \cdot f_{\text{high}}}} = \sqrt{T_{\text{low}} \cdot T_{\text{high}}} \quad &\text{(3) Using } T_i = 1/f_i \\ \therefore\quad T_{\min} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{high}}}, \quad T_{\max} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{low}}} \quad &\text{(4) Boundary values} \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical evaluation for the Delta band (0.5 – 4 Hz):** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{delta, min}} &= \frac{1}{4\ \text{Hz}} = 0.250\ \text{s} = 250\ \text{ms} \\ T_{\text{delta, max}} &= \frac{1}{0.5\ \text{Hz}} = 2.000\ \text{s} = 2000\ \text{ms} \\ f_{c,\,\delta} &= \sqrt{0.5 \times 4} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414\ \text{Hz} \\ T_{c,\,\delta} &= \frac{1}{1.414} \approx 0.707\ \text{s} = 707\ \text{ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical evaluation for the Alpha band (8 – 13 Hz):** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{alpha, min}} &= \frac{1}{13\ \text{Hz}} \approx 76.92\ \text{ms} \\ T_{\text{alpha, max}} &= \frac{1}{8\ \text{Hz}} = 125.00\ \text{ms} \\ f_{c,\,\alpha} &= \sqrt{8 \times 13} = \sqrt{104} \approx 10.20\ \text{Hz} \\ T_{c,\,\alpha} &= \frac{1}{10.20} \approx 98.04\ \text{ms} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] **Interpretation** — A Delta wave is so slow that you can almost count individual cycles with a stopwatch (one cycle ≈ half a second to 2 seconds), whereas an Alpha wave is ~10× faster, with 8–13 cycles per second. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Sampling Rate (Nyquist) for EEG To **digitize** an analog EEG signal without **aliasing**, the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem** requires: $$ f_s \ge 2\,f_{\text{max}} $$ For a clinical EEG with $f_{\text{max}} = 70$ Hz: $$ \begin{aligned} f_s^{\text{min}} &= 2 \times 70\ \text{Hz} \\ f_s^{\text{min}} &= 140\ \text{Hz} \end{aligned} $$ In practice, KTU examiners expect the answer **$f_s = 256$ Hz or 512 Hz** because the clinical standard is to **oversample** (4–7×) to: - Allow anti-aliasing filter roll-off, - Permit accurate peak-detection of spike/sharp-wave transients, - Enable sub-Hz resolution in spectral analysis. $$ f_s^{\text{clinical}} = 256\ \text{Hz} \quad \text{or} \quad 512\ \text{Hz} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RMS Amplitude and Band-Power EEG is a random (stochastic) signal. Its "size" is best described by the **RMS amplitude** and the **band-power** from the Power Spectral Density (PSD). **Step 1:** Consider an EEG segment $v(t)$ of duration $T$. Its **mean-square value** is: $$ \overline{v^{2}(t)} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt $$ **Step 2:** The **RMS amplitude** is the square-root of the mean-square value: $$ V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt} $$ **Step 3:** Apply **Parseval's theorem** in the frequency domain (using the Fourier Transform $\mathcal{F}\{v(t)\} = V(f)$): $$ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} v^{2}(t)\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert V(f)\vert^{2}\,df $$ **Step 4:** Therefore the **power in the band** $[f_1, f_2]$ is: $$ P_{\text{band}} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_{vv}(f)\,df \quad \text{where} \quad S_{vv}(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\vert V(f)\vert^{2}}{T} $$ **Step 5:** The relative band-power (used clinically) is: $$ P_{\text{rel}} = \frac{\int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_{vv}(f)\,df}{\int_{0}^{f_{\text{Nyquist}}} S_{vv}(f)\,df} \times 100\% $$ > [!TIP] **Engineering Insight** > In BCI pipelines, the **band-power in mu (8–12 Hz) and beta (13–30 Hz)** of the sensorimotor cortex is computed every 250 ms using a **Welch periodogram** (FFT averaged over overlapping windows) and fed as a feature vector to a Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) classifier for motor-imagery decoding. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison of Brain Waves | Parameter | Delta (δ) | Theta (θ) | Alpha (α) | Beta (β) | Gamma (γ) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Frequency (Hz)** | 0.5 – 4 | 4 – 8 | 8 – 13 | 13 – 30 | > 30 | | **Amplitude (µV)** | 20 – 200 | 5 – 20 (>20 in children) | 20 – 60 | 2 – 20 | < 2 | | **Synchronization** | High | Moderate | High | Low | Low | | **Brain State** | Deep sleep (N3) | Drowsy / light sleep / meditation | Relaxed, eyes closed | Active thinking / focused | Higher cognition / perception | | **Dominant Region** | Frontal (children), generalized | Temporal / hippocampal | Occipital | Frontal / Central | Widespread cortical | | **Pacemaker** | Thalamus (burst mode) | Hippocampus | Thalamus (tonic mode) | Cortical | Cortical networks | | **Eye-state correlation** | Eyes closed (deep sleep) | Eyes closed | **Eyes closed** (alpha blocks on opening) | Eyes open, active | Eyes open, cognitively engaged | | **Amplitude rationale** | High — many neurons in phase | Moderate | Moderate–High | Low | Low | | **Clinical abnormality** | Pathological in awake adult = lesion | Focal subcortical lesion | Alpha coma | Drug effects, anxiety | Cognitive disorders | | **Discovered by** | Walter & Dovey (1944) | Walter (1953) | Hans Berger (1924) | Hans Berger (1929) | Başar (1980s) | ## 3.5 Step-by-Step Worked Example (Board Exam Style) > **Problem (KTU model):** An EEG signal is observed with a dominant rhythmic activity at 10 Hz with peak-to-peak amplitude 60 µV. Identify the wave, calculate the period, and state the brain state in which it is most likely to be observed. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify the wave:** A frequency of **10 Hz** lies in the range 8–13 Hz → the wave is the **Alpha (α) wave**. **Step 2 — Period calculation:** $$ T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{10\ \text{Hz}} = 0.1\ \text{s} = 100\ \text{ms} $$ **Step 3 — Convert peak-to-peak amplitude to RMS (assuming sinusoid):** $$ V_{\text{rms}} = \frac{V_{\text{pp}}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{60\ \mu\text{V}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{60}{2.828}\ \mu\text{V} \approx 21.21\ \mu\text{V} $$ **Step 4 — Brain state:** Alpha waves dominate during **relaxed wakefulness with eyes closed**, especially over the **occipital region**. The signal is **attenuated (alpha blocking / Berger effect)** when the subject opens their eyes or performs mental arithmetic. **Step 5 — Engineering application:** This signal would be picked up by an **O1–A1 derivation** in a 10–20 montage and amplified by an **EEG amplifier** with gain ≈ 10 000, CMRR ≥ 80 dB, and band-pass 0.5–70 Hz. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Brain-Wave Acquisition Block Diagram (EEG Front-End) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Brain Cortex
Pyramidal Neurons] --> A2[Volume Conductor
Scalp and Skull] A2 --> B1[Surface Electrodes
Ag or AgCl] B1 --> B2[Electrode Gel
Reduces Impedance] B2 --> C1[Input Protection
and Buffers] C1 --> C2[Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain 100 to 1000] C2 --> D1[Band Pass Filter
0.5 to 70 Hz] D1 --> D2[Notch Filter
50 Hz Line Noise] D2 --> E1[Second Stage Amplifier
Gain 10 to 100] E1 --> F1[ADC
16 bit at 256 Hz] F1 --> G1[Digital Signal Processing
FFT, Welch PSD] G1 --> H1[Display or BCI Classifier] H1 --> H2[Clinical Report or Device] ``` **Reading the diagram:** The flow is strictly left-to-right. Each block has a single alphanumeric identifier (no `end` or `style` reserved words) and a plain uppercase label inside double quotes (no markdown formatting). ## 4.2 Thalamocortical Pacemaker Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeT[Thalamus
Thalamic Reticular Nucleus] nodeC[Cortex
Pyramidal Cell Layer 4 and 5] nodeE[EPSP Excitation] nodeI[IPSP Inhibition] nodeR[Rebound Burst] nodeW[EEG Waveform] nodeT -->|GABAergic burst| nodeI nodeI -->|Hyperpolarization| nodeC nodeC -->|Rebound excitation| nodeR nodeR -->|Next burst cycle| nodeT nodeC -->|Summated postsynaptic potential| nodeW nodeE --> nodeC ``` **Interpretation:** The thalamus fires an inhibitory burst → cortex hyperpolarizes → rebounds into excitation → triggers next thalamic burst. The cycle frequency is set by the **after-hyperpolarization time constant** of thalamic neurons, producing **Delta** (slow) or **Alpha** (fast) depending on membrane state. ## 4.3 Brain-Wave Hierarchy by Frequency and State ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeDS[Deep Sleep N3
Delta 0.5 to 4 Hz] nodeLS[Light Sleep N1 N2
Theta 4 to 8 Hz] nodeRW[Relaxed Awake Eyes Closed
Alpha 8 to 13 Hz] nodeAW[Active Thinking Focused
Beta 13 to 30 Hz] nodeHC[Higher Cognition
Gamma 30 to 100 Hz] nodeDS --> nodeLS nodeLS --> nodeRW nodeRW --> nodeAW nodeAW --> nodeHC ``` > [!NOTE] The diagram shows progressive **increase in frequency** as the brain becomes more activated. Amplitude, in contrast, **decreases** because cortical neurons desynchronize during active processing. ## 4.4 Frequency-Band Visualization (Block Map) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeF0[DC Drift
0 to 0.5 Hz] nodeF1[Delta Band
0.5 to 4 Hz] nodeF2[Theta Band
4 to 8 Hz] nodeF3[Alpha Band
8 to 13 Hz] nodeF4[Mu Rhythm SMR
12 to 15 Hz] nodeF5[Beta Band
13 to 30 Hz] nodeF6[Gamma Band
30 to 100 Hz] nodeF7[High Frequency Noise
Greater than 100 Hz] nodeF0 --> nodeF1 nodeF1 --> nodeF2 nodeF2 --> nodeF3 nodeF3 --> nodeF4 nodeF4 --> nodeF5 nodeF5 --> nodeF6 nodeF6 --> nodeF7 ``` **Use:** This is the diagram you must redraw from memory in any 7-mark KTU question. Note that **Mu rhythm (12–15 Hz)** overlaps the boundary of alpha and beta — this overlap is a favourite examiner trick. ## 4.5 10–20 Electrode Montage (Schematic Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeFp1[Fp1 Frontal Pole Left] nodeFp2[Fp2 Frontal Pole Right] nodeF3[F3 Frontal Left] nodeF4[F4 Frontal Right] nodeC3[C3 Central Left] nodeC4[C4 Central Right] nodeP3[P3 Parietal Left] nodeP4[P4 Parietal Right] nodeO1[O1 Occipital Left] nodeO2[O2 Occipital Right] nodeT3[T3 Temporal Left] nodeT4[T4 Temporal Right] nodeA1[A1 Reference Left Ear] nodeA2[A2 Reference Right Ear] nodeFz[Fz Midline Frontal] nodeCz[Cz Midline Central] nodePz[Pz Midline Parietal] nodeF3 --- nodeF4 nodeC3 --- nodeC4 nodeP3 --- nodeP4 nodeO1 --- nodeO2 nodeT3 --- nodeT4 nodeFp1 --- nodeF3 nodeFp2 --- nodeF4 nodeF3 --- nodeC3 nodeF4 --- nodeC4 nodeC3 --- nodeP3 nodeC4 --- nodeP4 nodeP3 --- nodeO1 nodeP4 --- nodeO2 nodeFz --- nodeCz nodeCz --- nodePz nodeA1 --- nodeT3 nodeA2 --- nodeT4 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]** > **Q1.** Define EEG. List the different types of normal brain waves and their frequency ranges. **(3 marks)** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] **Definition (1 mark)** > **Electroencephalography (EEG)** is the non-invasive technique of recording the **summed electrical activity of cortical pyramidal neurons** from the scalp surface using surface electrodes. The time-varying voltage trace is the **electroencephalogram**. **Frequency bands table (2 marks):** | Wave | Frequency (Hz) | Predominant State | |---|---|---| | Delta (δ) | 0.5 – 4 | Deep sleep | | Theta (θ) | 4 – 8 | Drowsiness, light sleep | | Alpha (α) | 8 – 13 | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed | | Beta (β) | 13 – 30 | Active thinking, alertness | | Gamma (γ) | > 30 | Higher cognitive processing | --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]** > **Q2.** Explain the **Alpha Blocking Response** (Berger Effect). Why is it clinically important? **(3 marks)** **Model Answer:** > When a subject who is awake, relaxed, and has eyes closed is suddenly **asked to open the eyes** (or perform mental arithmetic), the **posterior dominant alpha rhythm (8–13 Hz) over the occipital region is replaced by a low-voltage, desynchronized beta activity**. This phenomenon, first described by Hans Berger in 1929, is called **alpha blocking** or the **Berger effect**. **Mechanism (1 mark):** Visual input activates the lateral geniculate nucleus → thalamocortical loop is desynchronized → cortical pyramidal cells fire asynchronously → amplitude of rhythmic alpha drops. **Clinical importance (1 mark):** The **presence of a normal alpha rhythm and its blocking on eye opening** confirms: - Wakefulness (versus sleep / coma), - Integrity of the visual pathway and cortex, - Proper functioning of the ascending reticular activating system. Absence of alpha blocking suggests an encephalopathy, diffuse cerebral dysfunction, or non-cooperation of the patient. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE) > **[KTU University Exam – Model ESE Question, Dec 2024 Scheme, 14 marks]** ### **Question A — (a)** Classify the normal brain waves. Explain the origin, frequency range, amplitude, and clinical significance of each. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer Outline:** **(i) Introduction (1 mark):** The EEG, first recorded by Hans Berger in 1924, is a non-invasive measure of cortical electrical activity. The International Federation of Clinical Neurophysiology classifies waves into five bands. **(ii) Classification table (3 marks):** | Wave | Frequency (Hz) | Amplitude (µV) | State / Origin | Clinical | |---|---|---|---|---| | Delta (δ) | 0.5 – 4 | 20 – 200 | Deep sleep (N3); thalamus burst mode | Pathological in awake adult → focal lesion | | Theta (θ) | 4 – 8 | 5 – 20 | Drowsiness, light sleep; hippocampus | Mild TBI, focal subcortical lesion | | Alpha (α) | 8 – 13 | 20 – 60 | Relaxed wakefulness eyes closed; occipital cortex, thalamus | Alpha coma, poor prognosis | | Beta (β) | 13 – 30 | 2 – 20 | Active thinking, alertness; frontal/central | Drug effects (benzodiazepines) | | Gamma (γ) | > 30 | < 2 | Higher cognition; widespread cortex | Reduced in Alzheimer's, schizophrenia | **(iii) Origin detail (2 marks):** The thalamus acts as a pacemaker. The thalamic reticular nucleus delivers GABAergic inhibition → cortical pyramidal cells hyperpolarize → rebound excitation → next burst. Frequency depends on the **after-hyperpolarization time constant**: slow in deep sleep (delta), tonic in relaxed wakefulness (alpha). **(iv) Clinical conclusion (1 mark):** Wave-band analysis is a **biomarker for sleep stages, epilepsy, encephalopathy, and depth of anaesthesia (BIS uses a weighted sum of beta/alpha powers)**. ### **Question A — (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the EEG acquisition system. List the important specifications of a clinical EEG amplifier. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Block diagram (3 marks):** ``` Scalp Electrode → Input Protection → Instrumentation Amplifier (IA) → High-Pass Filter (≥ 0.5 Hz) → Low-Pass Filter (≤ 70 Hz) → Notch Filter (50 Hz) → Second-Stage Amplifier → ADC (≥ 16 bit, ≥ 256 Hz) → DSP → Display/PC ``` **Specification table (3 marks):** | Specification | Typical Clinical Value | Reason | |---|---|---| | Gain | 10 000 – 100 000 | To amplify µV signals to 0–5 V ADC range | | CMRR | ≥ 80 dB (preferably 100 dB) | To reject common-mode 50 Hz interference | | Input impedance | ≥ 10 MΩ (preferably 100 MΩ) | To avoid loading the high-impedance electrode | | Bandwidth | 0.5 – 70 Hz | Captures all clinical bands; rejects DC drift and HF noise | | Noise | < 0.5 µV RMS | Smaller than smallest EEG feature | | Notch filter | 50 Hz (or 60 Hz) | To reject mains line noise | **Why CMRR matters (1 mark):** A 1 V mains interference must be rejected by ~100 dB to leave the 50 µV EEG signal uncontaminated. Hence a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier topology (e.g., INA128, AD620) is mandatory. --- ### **Question B (Alternative to Question A) — (a)** Describe the **10–20 International System of Electrode Placement** for EEG. Why is a standard placement essential? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Description (4 marks):** The 10–20 system, proposed by **Herbert Jasper (1958)**, places 21 electrodes (or more) on the scalp at distances that are either 10% or 20% of the total skull dimensions. Reference points are: - **Nasion (Nz):** front-most depression at the bridge of the nose. - **Inion (Iz):** bony bump at the back of the skull. - **Pre-auricular points (A1, A2):** in front of the ear tragus. Electrodes are labelled with a **letter** (anatomical region) and a **number/letter** (side): - **F** = Frontal, **C** = Central, **P** = Parietal, **O** = Occipital, **T** = Temporal, **Fp** = Frontal pole. - **Even numbers** = right hemisphere, **Odd numbers** = left hemisphere, **z** = midline. **(ii) Why standardization matters (3 marks):** 1. **Reproducibility:** Different laboratories obtain comparable EEG records. 2. **Localizability:** A waveform abnormality at C3 can be confidently attributed to the left central sulcus (hand-motor area). 3. **Montage consistency:** Bipolar and referential montages require fixed electrode positions. 4. **Longitudinal comparison:** Same patient tracked over years (e.g., epilepsy monitoring). ### **Question B — (b)** Explain the **mechanism of generation of brain waves** and the role of the **thalamus as a pacemaker**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Cellular origin (2 marks):** Pyramidal cells of cortical layers IV–V (especially layer V) are aligned perpendicular to the cortical surface. When excited, current flows from apical dendrite to soma, generating a dipole recordable at the scalp. **EPSPs and IPSPs** (not action potentials) are long enough to summate and produce the slow EEG oscillations. **(ii) Thalamic pacemaker model (3 marks):** - The **thalamic reticular nucleus (TRN)** sends **GABAergic inhibitory bursts** to thalamocortical relay neurons. - The relay cells hyperpolarize, then **rebound-burst** to fire the next volley to the cortex. - This **inhibition–rebound loop** produces rhythmic excitation of cortical pyramidal cells → rhythmic EEG. **(iii) Frequency modulation (2 marks):** - During **deep sleep (Delta)**: thalamic T-type Ca²⁺ channels activate in burst mode → slow oscillation 0.5–4 Hz. - During **relaxed wakefulness (Alpha)**: thalamic cells are tonically depolarized → 8–13 Hz. - During **alertness (Beta/Gamma)**: cholinergic input from ascending reticular activating system desynchronizes the thalamocortical loop → fast, low-amplitude waves. **Conclusion:** Thus brain waves are **not generated by the cortex in isolation**, but by a **thalamocortical resonance** whose frequency is set by thalamic membrane kinetics and modulated by brainstem cholinergic input. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students most commonly lose marks on Brain-Wave questions:** > > 1. **Confusing the 10–20 system percentages with electrode impedances.** "10–20" refers to *skull distances*, NOT impedance. Many students lose 1 mark by saying "electrodes placed at 10–20 kΩ". > 2. **Mixing up frequency limits.** Beta is 13–30 Hz (NOT 13–25 Hz, NOT 12–30 Hz). Examiners are strict on numerical limits. > 3. **Forgetting the name "Berger effect"** when describing alpha blocking. Naming conventions earn easy marks. > 4. **Writing "EEG measures action potentials"** — wrong. EEG measures **graded post-synaptic potentials**. This is worth a full 1 mark by itself. > 5. **Confusing Mu rhythm (12–15 Hz, sensorimotor) with Alpha rhythm (8–13 Hz, occipital).** They overlap in frequency but differ in topography and reactivity. > 6. **Not drawing a block diagram** in 7-mark questions. A clear block diagram is worth 2–3 marks by itself. > 7. **Using uncalibrated units** — write "Hz", "µV", "kΩ" precisely. Do NOT write "cycles per sec" or "millivolts". > 8. **Skipping the Nyquist argument** when justifying sampling rate of 256 Hz. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Night Reading for KTU Exams)** > > ☐ **EEG = non-invasive** record of **summed post-synaptic potentials** of cortical pyramidal neurons. > > ☐ **Discovered by Hans Berger (1924, alpha wave; 1929, beta wave).** Always name him. > > ☐ **Five canonical bands:** Delta (0.5–4 Hz), Theta (4–8 Hz), Alpha (8–13 Hz), Beta (13–30 Hz), Gamma (>30 Hz). > > ☐ **Delta** = deep sleep, highest amplitude, slow. **Theta** = light sleep, drowsiness. > > ☐ **Alpha** = relaxed wakefulness eyes closed, posterior dominant, blocks on eye opening (**Berger effect / alpha blocking**). > > ☐ **Beta** = active thinking, low amplitude, frontal-central. **Gamma** = higher cognition, requires research-grade amplifiers. > > ☐ **Mu rhythm (12–15 Hz)** is sensorimotor beta, the basis of **motor-imagery BCI**. > > ☐ **Pyramidal neurons in cortical layers IV–V** are the source — their dipoles are perpendicular to the cortex and summate at the scalp. > > ☐ **Thalamus = pacemaker.** Burst mode → delta; tonic mode → alpha; desynchronized by ARAS → beta/gamma. > > ☐ **10–20 electrode system:** 21 electrodes, F (frontal), C (central), P (parietal), O (occipital), T (temporal), Fp (frontal pole); odd = left, even = right, z = midline. > > ☐ **Key amplifier specs:** Gain 10⁴–10⁵, CMRR ≥ 80 dB, input impedance ≥ 10 MΩ, bandwidth 0.5–70 Hz, notch at 50 Hz, noise < 0.5 µV RMS. > > ☐ **Sampling rate ≥ 2 × f_max = 2 × 70 = 140 Hz** (clinical standard 256 or 512 Hz). > > ☐ **Clinical uses:** Epilepsy, sleep staging, depth of anaesthesia (BIS), coma assessment, BCI, neurofeedback. > > ☐ **Key formulae to memorize:** $T = 1/f$, $V_{\text{rms}} = V_{\text{pp}}/(2\sqrt{2})$, $f_s \ge 2 f_{\text{max}}$, $P_{\text{band}} = \int S_{vv}(f) df$. > > ☐ **Always draw a block diagram** of the EEG acquisition chain in 7-mark questions — it is non-negotiable. > > ☐ **Do not confuse bands:** Beta is 13–30 Hz (not 13–25 Hz); Delta is 0.5–4 Hz (not 0.5–3 Hz). **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Brain Waves**

topics.title: Analysis of EEG, EMG - Recording Setup, ERG, and EOG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEETVT615 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Analysis of EEG, EMG - Recording Setup, ERG, and EOG # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Electroencephalogram (EEG) — Brain Wave Recording > [!IMPORTANT] > **EEG (Electroencephalogram):** A non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique that records the summed electrical activity of populations of cortical neurons (specifically the postsynaptic potentials of pyramidal cells in the cerebral cortex) using scalp electrodes, expressed as time-varying voltage waveforms. - **Standard Amplitude Range:** **10 – 100 µV** (microvolts) - **Standard Frequency Range:** **0.5 – 100 Hz** (clinical band: 0.5 – 30 Hz) - **Origin:** Extracellular currents generated by synchronous **EPSPs and IPSPs** in cortical pyramidal neurons (Layer V). - **Measurement Standard:** International **10–20 Electrode Placement System** (Jasper, 1958). > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — Intuition Builder:** > Think of the brain as a massive stadium full of people (neurons) clapping in rhythm. Individually, one person’s clap is silent. But when **millions of pyramidal neurons** synchronize their postsynaptic firing (think "Mexican wave" of excitation), the resulting summed field potential becomes strong enough to be detected through the skull and scalp. The EEG is essentially listening to that crowd-wave through the stadium walls. Electrodes are your microphones, and the amplifier boosts the whisper into a roar you can analyze. --- ## 1.2 Electromyogram (EMG) — Muscle Electrical Activity > [!IMPORTANT] > **EMG (Electromyogram):** A diagnostic technique that records the electrical potential generated by muscle fibers during voluntary or stimulated contraction, reflecting the depolarization of the sarcolemma triggered by the neuromuscular junction. - **Surface EMG Amplitude:** **50 µV – 5 mV** - **Intramuscular EMG Amplitude:** **100 µV – 10 mV** - **Frequency Bandwidth:** **20 Hz – 5 kHz** - **Two Acquisition Modalities:** - **Surface EMG (sEMG)** — non-invasive, summed activity of many motor units. - **Needle/Intramuscular EMG** — invasive, single motor unit action potentials (MUAPs). > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** > Imagine a stadium where every spectator has a personal microphone but only when they cheer. The **motor unit** is like a section of fans led by one cheerleader (motor neuron). When the section fires, you record one **MUAP**. Surface EMG is listening to the whole stadium; needle EMG is sticking a microphone into one section. Kinesiology labs and prosthetic control use sEMG like using a stadium-wide mic to detect crowd mood. --- ## 1.3 Electroretinogram (ERG) — Retina's Electrical Response to Light > [!IMPORTANT] > **ERG (Electroretinogram):** A diagnostic ophthalmic test that measures the electrical responses of various retinal cell types (photoreceptors, bipolar cells, retinal ganglion cells) to a light stimulus, captured via electrodes placed on the cornea and skin near the eye. - **Amplitude:** **100 – 500 µV** (light-adapted); up to **1 mV** (dark-adapted) - **Stimulus:** Standardized flash (ISCEV protocol) or pattern reversal. - **Key Waves:** **a-wave, b-wave, c-wave, d-wave** (each originating from a different retinal layer). > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** > Treat the retina as a **photographic sensor** with a built-in electrical readout. When photons hit, the sensor (photoreceptors) doesn't just record — it emits a tiny voltage pulse that propagates through the retinal circuitry. The ERG is the oscilloscope reading of that pulse train. Different components of the waveform tell you which layer of the retina responded — like checking if your camera's sensor, processor, or memory is faulty. --- ## 1.4 Electrooculogram (EOG) — Eye Movement Recording > [!IMPORTANT] > **EOG (Electrooculogram):** A technique for measuring the **corneo-retinal standing potential (DC potential of ≈ 0.4 – 1.0 mV)** and its variation as the eye rotates within the orbit, allowing precise measurement of eye position and saccadic/horizontal/vertical movement. - **Standing Potential Magnitude:** **0.4 – 1.0 mV** (cornea positive relative to retina). - **Signal Bandwidth:** **DC – 100 Hz** (DC for absolute position, AC for movement). - **Sensitivity:** Approximately **20 µV per degree** of eye rotation. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** > The eye acts like a tiny **electric dipole battery**: the cornea is the positive terminal and the retina is the negative terminal, generating a steady 0.5 mV potential. As the eye rotates, this battery tilts, and skin electrodes near the eyes detect the changing voltage field. EOG is like watching a flashlight's beam pattern shift as you rotate the flashlight — the electrodes sense the rotation angle. This is why EOG is widely used in **sleep studies** to detect REM (Rapid Eye Movement) cycles, even with the eyes closed. --- ## 1.5 Summary Snapshot Table | Modality | Source | Amplitude | Bandwidth | Primary Use | |----------|--------|-----------|-----------|-------------| | **EEG** | Cortical pyramidal neurons | 10–100 µV | 0.5–100 Hz | Epilepsy, sleep, cognition | | **EMG (surface)** | Motor units (summed) | 50 µV–5 mV | 20 Hz–5 kHz | Kinesiology, prosthetics | | **EMG (needle)** | Single motor units | 100 µV–10 mV | 20 Hz–10 kHz | Neuromuscular disorders | | **ERG** | Retinal layers | 100 µV–1 mV | 0.1–300 Hz | Retinal disease diagnosis | | **EOG** | Corneo-retinal dipole | 100 µV–1 mV | DC–100 Hz | Eye movement, sleep staging | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 EEG — Detailed Analysis ### 2.1.1 Genesis of the EEG Signal The EEG signal originates **not from action potentials** (which are too brief and asynchronous to sum externally) but from the **graded postsynaptic potentials** of the apical dendrites of cortical pyramidal cells. When a population of these neurons receives synchronous synaptic input: - **Excitatory PSPs (EPSPs):** cause extracellular negative shifts (surface positive). - **Inhibitory PSPs (IPSPs):** cause surface negative shifts. - The dipole layers produced align perpendicular to the cortical surface, allowing summation in volume. ### 2.1.2 EEG Frequency Bands (Clinical Relevance) | Band | Frequency Range | Amplitude | Associated State | |------|----------------|-----------|------------------| | **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz | 20 – 200 µV | Deep (slow-wave) sleep, infants | | **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 Hz | < 30 µV | Drowsiness, meditation, childhood | | **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 Hz | 20 – 60 µV | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed | | **Mu (μ)** | 8 – 13 Hz | < 50 µV | Motor cortex idling (Rolandic) | | **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 Hz | < 20 µV | Active thinking, alert state | | **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 Hz | Very low | Cognitive binding, perception | ### 2.1.3 10–20 Electrode Placement System - 21 electrodes placed at **10% or 20%** of measured skull distances. - Reference points: **Nasion (Nz)** and **Inion (Iz)**. - Odd numbers = **left hemisphere**; Even numbers = **right hemisphere**; Z = midline. - Common derivations: **Fp1, Fp2, F3, F4, C3, C4, P3, P4, O1, O2, F7, F8, T3, T4, T5, T6**, plus **A1, A2** (earlobes) and **Cz, Fz, Pz** (midline). ### 2.1.4 EEG Recording Setup — Block Description 1. **Electrodes** (Ag/AgCl disc, scalp) → convert ionic skin currents to electron flow. 2. **Input Stage** (high-impedance differential amplifier, gain ≈ 1,000–10,000). 3. **Filters**: HPF (~0.5 Hz) → LPF (~30–100 Hz) → Notch (50 Hz line interference). 4. **ADC** (16-bit minimum, ≥ 256 Hz sampling per channel for clinical EEG). 5. **Display/Storage** → polygraph or computer-based digital system. ### 2.1.5 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) A critical specification for biopotential amplifiers: $$ \text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right) \;\;\text{[dB]} $$ where $A_d$ = differential gain, $A_c$ = common-mode gain. **EEG amplifiers require CMRR ≥ 80 dB** (typically 100+ dB). ### 2.1.6 Berger Effect (Alpha Blocking) When a relaxed subject with eyes closed (alpha-dominant) opens the eyes, **alpha rhythm is suppressed** (desynchronization). Discovered by **Hans Berger (1929)** — the founder of human EEG. --- ## 2.2 EMG — Detailed Analysis ### 2.2.1 Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP) - **Motor Unit = 1 motor neuron + all muscle fibers it innervates.** - MUAP duration: **2 – 15 ms**; amplitude: **100 µV – 3 mV** (surface). - Recruitment: As force demand rises, more motor units activate (Henneman's Size Principle — smaller units first). ### 2.2.2 EMG Frequency Content - Median frequency: **70 – 130 Hz** for voluntary contraction. - Mean power frequency shifts downward with **muscle fatigue** due to conduction velocity slowing. ### 2.2.3 Surface EMG vs Needle EMG | Feature | Surface (sEMG) | Needle (iEMG) | |---------|----------------|---------------| | Invasiveness | None | Invasive | | Pickup area | Large (many MUs) | Localized (few MUs) | | Bandwidth | 20–500 Hz | 20 Hz–10 kHz | | Application | Kinesiology, rehab, prosthetics | Clinical neurophysiology | | Electrode | Ag/AgCl disc/bipolar | Concentric/monopolar needle | ### 2.2.4 EMG Signal Conditioning - **Amplification:** gain up to **1,000×** (input ≈ 0.1–5 mV). - **Bandpass:** 20 Hz – 5 kHz. - **Notch filter:** 50/60 Hz line removal. - **Rectification:** full-wave → envelope detection. - **RMS or Mean Absolute Value (MAV)** computation for amplitude quantification. $$ \text{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} x_n^2} $$ $$ \text{MAV} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} \vert x_n \vert $$ ### 2.2.5 Nerve Conduction Velocity (NCV) Clinically, EMG systems often measure NCV to localize nerve lesions: $$ v_{NC} = \frac{\Delta L}{\Delta t} \;\;\text{[m/s]} $$ where $\Delta L$ = distance between stimulation sites, $\Delta t$ = latency difference. - Normal motor NCV: **50 – 70 m/s** (upper limb). - Demyelination reduces NCV; axonal loss reduces amplitude. --- ## 2.3 ERG — Detailed Analysis ### 2.3.1 Components of the ERG Waveform | Wave | Origin | Polarity | Latency | Sensitivity | |------|--------|----------|---------|-------------| | **a-wave** | Photoreceptor hyperpolarization | Negative | < 15 ms | Rods & cones | | **b-wave** | Bipolar / Müller cells (K⁺ flux) | Positive | 30–50 ms | Inner retinal activity | | **c-wave** | RPE (retinal pigment epithelium) | Positive (slow) | 1–2 s | RPE health | | **d-wave** (OFF response) | OFF-bipolar cells | Positive | 50–100 ms | Cone OFF pathway | | **Photopic negative response (PhNR)** | Retinal ganglion cells | Negative | 75 ms | Ganglion cell loss | ### 2.3.2 ISCEV Standard Stimuli - **Dark-adapted (scotopic):** 0.01, 3.0, 10.0 cd·s/m². - **Light-adapted (photopic):** 3.0 cd·s/m² on 30 cd/m² background. - **Flicker ERG:** 30 Hz for cone system isolation. ### 2.3.3 Pattern ERG (PERG) - Uses reversing checkerboard instead of flash. - Reflects **ganglion cell function**; useful for **glaucoma**. --- ## 2.4 EOG — Detailed Analysis ### 2.4.1 Corneo-Retinal Standing Potential The retina is maintained at a **negative potential of ~ 0.4–1.0 mV** relative to the cornea. The eye thus acts as a rotating dipole. ### 2.4.2 EOG Signal Modeling With skin electrodes placed lateral to the outer canthi: $$ V_{EOG} = k \cdot \theta $$ where $k$ ≈ 20 µV/° (sensitivity) and $\theta$ = eye rotation angle in degrees. ### 2.4.3 Recording Configuration - **Horizontal EOG:** 2 electrodes at left & right outer canthi. - **Vertical EOG:** 2 electrodes above & below one eye. - **Reference:** forehead or earlobe. ### 2.4.4 Clinical & Research Applications - **Sleep staging (polysomnography):** detection of REM. - **Ophthalmology:** diagnosis of **Best disease** (LP:DT ratio assessment). - **Human–computer interfaces:** gaze tracking. - **Vestibular studies:** nystagmus quantification. ### 2.4.5 Light Peak to Dark Trough (LP:DT) Ratio A key EOG metric for RPE assessment: $$ \text{LP:DT ratio} = \frac{\text{Light Peak amplitude}}{\text{Dark Trough amplitude}} $$ - **Normal:** ≥ **1.85** - **Best disease:** < 1.50 (severely reduced) --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---------|-------------|---------------| | $A_{EEG} \in [10, 100]$ | EEG amplitude range | µV | | $A_{EMG,s} \in [50\,\mu V, 5\,mV]$ | Surface EMG amplitude | µV to mV | | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_c)$ | Common-mode rejection ratio | dB; ≥ 80 dB required | | $v_{NC} = \Delta L / \Delta t$ | Nerve conduction velocity | m/s; normal 50–70 m/s | | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{(1/N)\sum x_n^2}$ | EMG amplitude index | mV | | $\text{MAV} = (1/N)\sum \vert x_n \vert$ | Mean absolute value | mV | | $V_{EOG} = k\,\theta$, $k \approx 20\,\mu V/^\circ$ | EOG sensitivity | µV/° | | $V_{CR} \in [0.4, 1.0]$ | Corneo-retinal standing potential | mV | | $\text{LP:DT} = V_{LP} / V_{DT}$ | EOG clinical ratio | Normal ≥ 1.85 | | $f_{\delta} \in [0.5, 4]$ | EEG delta band | Hz | | $f_{\alpha} \in [8, 13]$ | EEG alpha band | Hz | | $f_{\beta} \in [13, 30]$ | EEG beta band | Hz | | $f_{\gamma} > 30$ | EEG gamma band | Hz | | $f_{EMG} \in [20, 5000]$ | EMG bandwidth | Hz | | $f_{s,EEG} \geq 256$ | EEG sampling rate | Hz (clinical) | | $f_{s,EMG} \geq 1000$ | EMG sampling rate | Hz (clinical) | | $A_{ERG} \in [100, 1000]$ | ERG amplitude range | µV | | $G_{EEG} \approx 10^4$ | EEG amplifier gain | unitless | | $Z_{in} \geq 10\,\text{M}\Omega$ | Required input impedance | Ω | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Reality Check:** > These four modalities (EEG, EMG, ERG, EOG) are foundational to the **$60+ billion** global neurodiagnostic and biosignal industry. Understanding their differences — especially the **frequency band** and **amplitude** — is essential for designing appropriate front-end analog conditioning circuits, choosing the right ADC resolution, and applying the right filtering strategy. KTU examiners reward students who can **justify** their design choices with these parameters. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Sensitivity of the EOG Dipole Model The eye, modeled as a dipole of fixed magnitude $V_0$ (corneo-retinal potential) rotating with the gaze, produces a measurable voltage between two lateral skin electrodes separated by a distance $d$ ≈ 3 cm. **Step 1 — Define the geometry.** Let the eye center be at the origin. Two electrodes are placed at horizontal positions $\pm d/2$ along the x-axis. The eye's dipole vector $\vec{p}$ makes an angle $\theta$ with the midline (positive $\theta$ = rightward gaze). **Step 2 — Write the dipole potential at each electrode.** $$ V_1 = \frac{k_p \cos(\pi/2 - \theta)}{r_1} = \frac{k_p \sin\theta}{r_1} $$ $$ V_2 = \frac{k_p \cos(\pi/2 + \theta)}{r_2} = -\frac{k_p \sin\theta}{r_2} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the differential signal $V_{EOG} = V_1 - V_2$.** $$ V_{EOG} = k_p \sin\theta \left(\frac{1}{r_1} + \frac{1}{r_2}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Apply the small-angle and symmetric placement approximation.** For $|\theta| < 30°$ and $r_1 \approx r_2 = r$: $$ V_{EOG} \approx \frac{2k_p \sin\theta}{r} \approx \frac{2k_p \theta}{r} $$ **Step 5 — Define the empirical sensitivity.** $$ S_{EOG} = \frac{dV_{EOG}}{d\theta} = \frac{2k_p}{r} \approx 20 \;\;\mu V/^\circ $$ > This is the **calibration factor** used in clinical EOG and gaze-tracking systems. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Required Amplifier Gain for EEG **Step 1 — Identify the signal and ADC range.** EEG amplitude range: $A_{EEG} = 10$ – $100\,\mu V$. Typical ADC full-scale input: $V_{FS} = \pm 5\,V$. ADC bit resolution: $n = 16$ bits. **Step 2 — Compute the least significant bit (LSB) of the ADC.** $$ V_{LSB} = \frac{2V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{65536} \approx 152.6 \;\;\mu V $$ **Step 3 — Determine the gain needed so that the smallest EEG signal occupies a reasonable fraction of full scale.** To make a $10\,\mu V$ signal map to at least 1 mV (≈ 6.5 LSB), gain must satisfy: $$ G \geq \frac{V_{min,output}}{V_{signal,min}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 100 $$ To fully utilize the ADC range for the largest EEG signal: $$ G_{max} = \frac{V_{FS}}{A_{EEG,max}} = \frac{5}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 50{,}000 $$ **Step 4 — Conclusion.** A **two-stage amplifier chain** is required: a pre-amplifier with gain 100–1000 (high CMRR), followed by a second stage with gain 50–100 to bring total gain to ≈ 5,000–10,000. $$ G_{total} = G_1 \times G_2 \approx 10^3 \times 10^1 = 10^4 $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Nyquist Sampling for EMG **Step 1 — Identify the highest frequency component.** EMG upper bandwidth: $f_{max} = 5{,}000\;Hz$. **Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist criterion.** $$ f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 5000 = 10{,}000 \;\;Hz $$ **Step 3 — Practical recommendation.** In practice, oversampling by 2×–4× is used to ease analog anti-aliasing filter design: $$ f_{s,clinical} = 20{,}000 \text{ to } 50{,}000\;Hz $$ > A sampling rate of **20 kHz** is the typical KTU exam / clinical standard for EMG acquisition. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Clinical Use-Case Mapping | Clinical Question | Best Modality | Why? | Typical Finding | |-------------------|---------------|------|-----------------| | Is the patient having a seizure? | **EEG** | Detects cortical hypersynchrony | Spike-and-wave discharges | | Is the facial nerve damaged? | **EMG + NCV** | Detects denervation/CMAP loss | Reduced CMAP amplitude, slowed NCV | | Is retinitis pigmentosa present? | **ERG** | Tests photoreceptor function | Reduced a-wave | | Does the patient have glaucoma? | **PERG** | Ganglion cell function | Reduced N95 wave | | Is the patient in REM sleep? | **EOG** | Detects rapid eye movements | High-frequency eye deflections | | Best vitelliform macular dystrophy? | **EOG (LP/DT)** | Tests RPE function | LP:DT < 1.5 | | Myasthenia gravis? | **EMG (repetitive stim)** | NMJ transmission | Decremental CMAP response | | Brain death confirmation? | **EEG** | Cortical activity | Electrocerebral silence (flatline) | --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style > **Problem:** A clinical EEG amplifier uses a differential gain $A_d = 10{,}000$ and a common-mode gain $A_c = 0.1$. Calculate the CMRR in dB. Is this sufficient for EEG use? **Step 1 — Substitute into the CMRR formula.** $$ \text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{10000}{0.1}\right) $$ $$ = 20 \log_{10}(100000) = 20 \times 5 = 100 \;\;dB $$ **Step 2 — Compare with the EEG requirement.** - Required minimum: **80 dB**. - Calculated: **100 dB**. **Step 3 — Conclusion.** **Yes, the amplifier is well within specification** for clinical EEG use, providing excellent rejection of 50 Hz mains interference and other common-mode noise. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Biopotential Recording Chain This block diagram applies identically to **EEG, EMG, ERG, and EOG** systems. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Biological Source
Neurons / Muscle / Retina / Eye] --> B[Electrodes
Ag-AgCl] B --> C[Input Protection
and Buffering] C --> D[Instrumentation
Amplifier] D --> E[Bandpass Filter
HPF and LPF] E --> F[Notch Filter
50 or 60 Hz] F --> G[Main Amplifier
Gain Stage 2] G --> H[ADC
16-bit minimum] H --> I[Digital Signal
Processing] I --> J[Display
or Storage] I --> K[Artifact
Rejection] ``` --- ## 4.2 EEG Acquisition System — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Head[Scalp and Cortex] H1[Cortical Pyramidal Neurons] --> H2[Volume Conducted Field] end H2 --> H3[Ag-AgCl Scalp Electrodes 10-20 System] H3 --> H4[Electrode Gel Electrolyte Interface] H4 --> H5[Patient Cable with Driven Right Leg] H5 --> H6[Instrumentation Amplifier CMRR 100 dB] H6 --> H7[HPF 0.5 Hz] H7 --> H8[LPF 30 to 70 Hz] H8 --> H9[Notch 50 Hz] H9 --> H10[Main Amp Gain 10K] H10 --> H11[16-bit ADC at 256 Hz] H11 --> H12[Digital Filter and Storage] H12 --> H13[EEG Trace Display] ``` --- ## 4.3 EMG Acquisition System — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB M1[Motor Cortex Command] --> M2[Spinal Motor Neuron] M2 --> M3[Axonal Action Potential] M3 --> M4[Neuromuscular Junction] M4 --> M5[Muscle Fiber Depolarization] M5 --> M6[Surface or Needle Electrode] M6 --> M7[Differential Amplifier Gain 1000] M7 --> M8[Bandpass 20 Hz to 5 kHz] M8 --> M9[Notch Filter 50 Hz] M9 --> M10[Rectification Full Wave] M10 --> M11[Envelope RMS or MAV] M11 --> M12[Display or Prosthetic Control] ``` --- ## 4.4 ERG Acquisition System — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Light Stimulator Ganzfeld] --> P2[Patient Retina] P2 --> P3[Photoreceptor Response a-wave] P2 --> P4[Bipolar and Muller Cell Response b-wave] P2 --> P5[RPE Response c-wave] P3 --> P6[Corneal Electrode] P4 --> P6 P5 --> P6 P6 --> P7[Differential Amplifier] P7 --> P8[Bandpass 0.1 to 300 Hz] P8 --> P9[ADC 1 kHz] P9 --> P10[ERG Waveform Analysis] ``` --- ## 4.5 EOG Dipole Model — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR E1[Cornea + Terminal] --> E3[Standing Potential 0.4 to 1.0 mV] E2[Retina - Terminal] --> E3 E3 --> E4[Eye Rotation Theta] E4 --> E5[Horizontal Electrode Pair] E4 --> E6[Vertical Electrode Pair] E5 --> E7[Differential Amplifier Gain 1000] E6 --> E7 E7 --> E8[LPF 30 Hz] E8 --> E9[Signal Output 20 uV per degree] E9 --> E10[Gaze Tracker or Sleep Stage Classifier] ``` --- ## 4.6 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Modality ```mermaid flowchart TB Q1{What is the clinical
question?} Q1 -->|Brain activity| Q2[EEG] Q1 -->|Muscle function| Q3[EMG] Q1 -->|Retinal response| Q4[ERG] Q1 -->|Eye movement| Q5[EOG] Q2 --> Q6[Pickup: Scalp electrodes, 10-20 system] Q3 --> Q7[Pickup: Surface or needle electrode] Q4 --> Q8[Pickup: Corneal contact lens electrode] Q5 --> Q9[Pickup: Periorbital skin electrodes] Q6 --> R[Apply: 0.5-100 Hz BPF, gain 10K] Q7 --> R2[Apply: 20-5000 Hz BPF, gain 1K] Q8 --> R3[Apply: 0.1-300 Hz BPF, light flash] Q9 --> R4[Apply: DC-30 Hz, calibrate 20 uV/deg] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** ### Q1. Define EEG. List any four standard EEG frequency bands with their frequency ranges. **Model Answer:** EEG (Electroencephalogram) is a non-invasive electrophysiological recording of the summed electrical activity of cortical pyramidal neurons, obtained via scalp electrodes. Four standard EEG frequency bands: 1. **Delta (δ):** 0.5 – 4 Hz — deep sleep. 2. **Theta (θ):** 4 – 8 Hz — drowsiness, light sleep. 3. **Alpha (α):** 8 – 13 Hz — relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed. 4. **Beta (β):** 13 – 30 Hz — active thinking, alertness. --- ### Q2. What is the corneo-retinal standing potential? Why is it important in EOG recording? **Model Answer:** The corneo-retinal standing potential is a steady DC voltage of **0.4 – 1.0 mV** that exists across the eye, with the cornea positive relative to the retina. It forms a stable electric dipole that rotates with the eyeball. **Importance:** It is the *source signal* for EOG. As the eye rotates within the orbit, the dipole orientation changes, producing measurable voltage variations at periorbital skin electrodes. This enables: - Quantitative measurement of eye position (sensitivity ≈ 20 µV/°). - Detection of REM during sleep (even with eyes closed). - Diagnosis of RPE disorders via the **LP:DT ratio**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – December 2023, Adapted]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the block diagram of an EEG recording system. Discuss the role of each block in detail. **Model Answer:** A clinical EEG recording system consists of the following cascaded blocks: 1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl disc electrodes, 10–20 system placement):** Convert ionic currents from the scalp into electron flow. The electrolyte gel reduces skin–electrode impedance to < 5 kΩ. 2. **Input Protection & Buffering:** Protects the subject from defibrillator-type surges and isolates the high-impedance electrode signal from the amplifier. 3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA):** - Differential topology providing **CMRR ≥ 80 dB** (typically 100+ dB for EEG). - High input impedance **> 10 MΩ** to prevent signal loading. - Initial gain of **×100 to ×1000**. 4. **Bandpass Filter (HPF + LPF):** - HPF at **0.5 Hz** removes DC electrode offset and slow drift. - LPF at **30–70 Hz** removes high-frequency EMG contamination and RF interference. 5. **Notch Filter (50 Hz in India / 60 Hz in USA):** Suppresses power-line interference. 6. **Main Amplifier (Gain stage 2):** Provides the remaining gain to bring total system gain to **≈ 10,000** (so a 50 µV EEG signal becomes 0.5 V for ADC). 7. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** - Resolution: **16-bit minimum**. - Sampling rate: **≥ 256 Hz per channel** (Nyquist for 100 Hz max). 8. **Digital Signal Processing & Display:** - Artifact rejection (eye blink, muscle). - FFT, topographic mapping, spike detection. - Display on monitor / storage in EDF format. 9. **Driven Right Leg (DRL) Circuit:** Actively drives the body to reduce common-mode voltage, improving CMRR in practice. **Significance:** Each block ensures the EEG signal (10–100 µV) is amplified, filtered, digitized, and presented for clinical interpretation of epilepsy, sleep stages, and encephalopathies. --- #### (b) [7 Marks] With suitable diagrams, describe the recording technique for an Electromyogram (EMG). Compare surface and needle EMG. **Model Answer:** **EMG Recording Setup:** 1. **Subject Preparation:** Skin cleaned with alcohol to lower impedance (< 10 kΩ). 2. **Electrode Placement:** - *Active electrode* over the muscle belly. - *Reference electrode* over tendon/bony landmark. - *Ground electrode* over electrically neutral site. 3. **Amplification:** Differential amplifier with gain 1,000–5,000, bandwidth 20 Hz – 5 kHz. 4. **Filtering:** HPF (20 Hz) removes motion artifact; LPF (5 kHz) prevents aliasing; notch at 50 Hz. 5. **Display:** Oscilloscope or computer with rectification and RMS envelope. 6. **Storage:** Sampled at ≥ 20 kHz, 16-bit resolution. **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Surface EMG (sEMG) | Needle EMG (iEMG) | |-----------|-------------------|-------------------| | Invasiveness | Non-invasive | Invasive (skin puncture) | | Pickup area | Large; many MUs | Localized; 1–5 MUs | | Amplitude | 50 µV – 5 mV | 100 µV – 10 mV | | Bandwidth | 20 Hz – 500 Hz | 20 Hz – 10 kHz | | Information | Gross muscle activation | Single MU morphology | | Pain | None | Mild discomfort | | Application | Kinesiology, prosthetics, sports | Clinical diagnosis of neuropathy/myopathy | **Clinical Note:** Needle EMG is the gold standard for diagnosing neuromuscular disorders such as **ALS, myasthenia gravis, peripheral neuropathy**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* #### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the origin, components, and clinical significance of the Electroretinogram (ERG). **Model Answer:** **Origin of ERG:** The ERG is a mass electrical response of the retina to a light stimulus. It is generated by the **summed ionic currents** flowing through retinal cells (photoreceptors, bipolar cells, Müller cells, RPE) when photons are absorbed by rhodopsin and photopsins. **Recording Technique:** - **Active electrode:** Contact lens electrode (Burian-Allen or ERG-jet) on the cornea. - **Reference electrode:** Temporal canthus skin electrode. - **Ground:** Forehead. - **Stimulus:** Ganzfeld full-field flash; standardized by **ISCEV protocol**. **Components of the ERG Waveform:** | Wave | Polarity | Origin | Latency | Clinical Use | |------|----------|--------|---------|--------------| | **a-wave** | Negative | Photoreceptor (rod/cone) hyperpolarization | < 15 ms | Rod/cone disease | | **b-wave** | Positive | Bipolar & Müller cell K⁺ flux | 30–50 ms | Inner retinal disease | | **c-wave** | Positive (slow) | RPE response to K⁺ | 1–2 s | RPE disorders | | **d-wave** | Positive | OFF-bipolar cells (cone system) | 50 ms | Cone ON/OFF pathway | | **PhNR** | Negative | Retinal ganglion cells | 75 ms | Glaucoma | **Clinical Significance:** - **Retinitis pigmentosa:** absent or reduced a-wave. - **Leber congenital amaurosis:** extinguished ERG. - **Glaucoma:** reduced PhNR. - **Diabetic retinopathy:** altered oscillatory potentials. - **Vitamin A deficiency:** reduced scotopic response. --- #### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the Electrooculogram (EOG) recording method. Discuss its applications in sleep studies and clinical ophthalmology. **Model Answer:** **Principle:** The eye acts as a **rotating electric dipole** with the cornea positive (+0.4 – 1.0 mV) and the retina negative. When the eye rotates, the dipole vector changes orientation, and skin electrodes placed around the orbit detect the resulting voltage change. **Recording Method:** 1. **Electrode Placement:** - Horizontal EOG: Two electrodes at outer canthi (left & right). - Vertical EOG: Two electrodes above and below one eye. - Reference: earlobe or forehead. 2. **Amplification:** Differential amplifier, gain ≈ 1000, DC-coupled (or very low HPF ≈ 0.1 Hz). 3. **Calibration:** Patient fixates on targets at known angles (±10°, ±20°) to establish the **sensitivity** (typically **20 µV/°**). 4. **Recording:** Patient performs saccades between targets while voltage is recorded. **Applications in Sleep Studies (Polysomnography):** - EOG is one of the **four standard polysomnographic channels** (EEG, EOG, EMG chin, ECG). - Detects **Rapid Eye Movement (REM)** — bursts of high-frequency, conjugate eye movements. - Critical for **REM sleep staging** per Rechtschaffen & Kales criteria. - EOG works **even with eyes closed** because the corneo-retinal dipole persists. **Applications in Clinical Ophthalmology:** - **Best disease (vitelliform macular dystrophy):** severely reduced **LP:DT ratio (< 1.5)**; this is the diagnostic gold standard. - **RPE disorders** (e.g., pattern dystrophies): abnormal light-peak response. - **Diagnosis of nystagmus** and quantification of eye movement disorders. - **Gaze tracking** in human–computer interfaces for paralyzed patients (e.g., Stephen Hawking's communication systems used infrared, but EOG-based versions exist). **LP:DT Ratio Calculation:** $$ \text{LP:DT} = \frac{V_{LightPeak}}{V_{DarkTrough}} \;\;\;\; \text{Normal} \geq 1.85 $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Loss Pitfalls in EEG/EMG/ERG/EOG Questions:** > > 1. **Wrong Frequency Bands:** Mixing up the EEG band ranges (delta vs theta, alpha vs mu) costs full marks. Memorize: **Delta < 4, Theta 4–8, Alpha 8–13, Beta 13–30, Gamma > 30**. > 2. **Wrong Amplitude Range:** Writing mV for EEG (it is **µV**, not mV) is a classic blunder. The correct sequence is **EEG (µV) < ERG (µV) < EOG (mV) < EMG (mV)**. > 3. **Confusing Electrodes:** 10–20 system is for **EEG only**, not EMG/EOG. Corneal contact lens electrode is for **ERG only**. > 4. **Forgetting the Notch Filter:** Examiners specifically award marks for mentioning the **50 Hz notch** to remove line interference. > 5. **Missing CMRR Specification:** Always state that biopotential amplifiers require **CMRR ≥ 80 dB** (or higher) — this is a frequent 2-mark item. > 6. **EOG Sensitivity Value:** Writing just "sensitivity" without the value **20 µV/°** loses 1 mark. > 7. **ERG Wave Names:** Examiners expect **a-wave, b-wave, c-wave, d-wave** in correct order with correct origin (photoreceptor / bipolar / RPE / OFF-bipolar). > 8. **MUAP vs M-wave:** Surface EMG summation of many MUs is sometimes called **M-wave** (compound muscle action potential) — avoid confusing with single MUAP. > 9. **Bipolar vs Monopolar Derivations in EEG:** Bipolar = difference between two scalp electrodes; Monopolar/Reference = one scalp electrode vs reference (e.g., linked ears A1+A2). Examiners may ask. > 10. **Forgetting Artifact Sources:** Always mention **EOG, EMG, ECG, motion, sweat, 50 Hz** as common EEG artifacts. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### High-Density Revision Checklist - [ ] **EEG** measures **summed postsynaptic potentials** of cortical pyramidal neurons, NOT action potentials. - [ ] **EEG amplitude = 10–100 µV**; bandwidth = **0.5–100 Hz**; placement = **10–20 system**. - [ ] EEG bands: **δ (0.5–4)**, **θ (4–8)**, **α (8–13)**, **β (13–30)**, **γ (>30) Hz**. - [ ] **Alpha blocking (Berger effect):** eyes open suppresses alpha rhythm. - [ ] **EMG** measures **MUAPs** (motor unit action potentials). - [ ] Surface EMG bandwidth: **20 Hz – 5 kHz**; amplitude: **50 µV – 5 mV**. - [ ] **Nerve conduction velocity (NCV):** $v = \Delta L / \Delta t$, normal 50–70 m/s. - [ ] EMG signal quantification: **RMS** or **MAV**; **median frequency** decreases with fatigue. - [ ] **ERG** measures retinal response to light; waves: **a (photoreceptor), b (bipolar/Müller), c (RPE), d (OFF-bipolar), PhNR (ganglion cells)**. - [ ] ERG uses **corneal contact lens electrode**; standardized by **ISCEV** protocol. - [ ] **EOG** measures **corneo-retinal standing potential (0.4–1.0 mV)**; sensitivity **20 µV/°**. - [ ] EOG detects **REM sleep** and is used to diagnose **Best disease** (LP:DT < 1.5). - [ ] All biopotential amplifiers require: **CMRR ≥ 80 dB**, **Zin > 10 MΩ**, **notch filter at 50 Hz**, **bandpass filtering**. - [ ] EEG sampling rate: **≥ 256 Hz**; EMG sampling rate: **≥ 20 kHz**. - [ ] EEG gain: **≈ 10,000×**; EMG gain: **≈ 1,000–5,000×**. - [ ] Common artifacts in EEG: **eye blink (EOG), muscle (EMG), cardiac (ECG), sweat, electrode motion, 50 Hz mains**. - [ ] **Henneman's size principle:** smaller motor units are recruited first during voluntary contraction. - [ ] **Isolated ground (DRL) circuit** actively reduces common-mode interference in EEG. - [ ] **Photopic ERG** tests cones; **scotopic ERG** tests rods. - [ ] **Pattern ERG (PERG)** specifically assesses retinal ganglion cells → useful in glaucoma. - [ ] **EOG works with eyes closed** because the corneo-retinal potential is a standing DC source.

topics.title: Prosthesis: Heart-Lung Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEETVT615 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Prosthesis: Heart-Lung Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HEART-LUNG MACHINE (HLM) / CARDIOPULMONARY BYPASS (CPB) MACHINE**
> A **Heart-Lung Machine** is a biomechanical prosthetic device that temporarily assumes the dual physiological functions of the **cardiac pump (heart)** and the **gas-exchange unit (lungs)** during open-heart cardiac surgery. It maintains systemic **perfusion** (blood circulation), **oxygenation** (O₂ delivery), and **CO₂ removal** for periods typically ranging from **1 to 6 hours**, while the surgeon operates on a **motionless, bloodless, and relaxed heart**.

The system is formally known as the **Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB) circuit**, and the procedure of using it is termed **extracorporeal circulation (ECC)**. The first successful clinical use was performed by **Dr. John Gibbon in 1953**.

## 1.2 Key Engineering Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Engineering / Clinical Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Cardiopulmonary Bypass (CPB)** | Diverting venous blood away from the heart and lungs to an external circuit |
| **Oxygenator** | Artificial lung; performs gas exchange (O₂ in, CO₂ out) |
| **Perfusion** | Delivery of oxygenated blood to body tissues at adequate pressure |
| **Cannulation** | Surgical insertion of tubes into major vessels (aorta, vena cava, atrium) |
| **Priming Volume** | Volume of crystalloid/colloid solution used to fill the circuit before connection (~1200–1800 mL) |
| **Hypothermia** | Deliberate cooling of the patient (28 °C–32 °C) to reduce metabolic demand |
| **Hemolysis** | Mechanical destruction of red blood cells by shear stress in the pump |
| **Cross-clamp time** | Duration the aorta is clamped (myocardial ischemia time) |
| **Cardioplegia** | Special solution (high K⁺) to arrest and protect the heart |
| **Systemic Perfusion Pressure (MAP)** | Target **Mean Arterial Pressure** of **50–70 mmHg** during CPB |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 3 – Prosthesis):**
> The Heart-Lung Machine falls under **Prosthetic Devices for Cardiovascular Support**. Students are expected to know the block diagram, components, working principle, physiological parameters, and the engineering equations governing flow and gas exchange.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Plumbing Bypass" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine repairing a city's main water pumping station (the heart) and filtration plant (the lungs).** You cannot shut off water supply to the entire city while you fix them — hospitals, homes, and factories would collapse. So, engineers build a **temporary bypass pipe** that:
> 1. **Diverts** the dirty water (deoxygenated venous blood) before it reaches the pump,
> 2. **Filters and aerates** it in a portable plant (oxygenator),
> 3. **Re-pressurizes** it (pump), and
> 4. **Returns** clean water (oxygenated arterial blood) to the city's main distribution line (aorta/arterial system).
>
> The **Heart-Lung Machine is exactly that bypass — a temporary, external, mechanical "plumbing" system** that keeps the body alive while the surgeon rebuilds the original pump and filter.

## 1.4 Clinical Indication Triggers (Why HLM is Needed)

- **Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting (CABG)**
- **Heart Valve Replacement (Aortic / Mitral)**
- **Congenital heart defect repair (ASD, VSD, Tetralogy of Fallot)**
- **Aortic aneurysm repair**
- **Heart transplantation**
- **Pulmonary thromboendarterectomy**

## 1.5 Standard Physiological Targets (Bolded Constants)

- **Systemic Blood Flow:** **2.2 – 2.5 L/min/m²** of Body Surface Area (BSA)
- **Arterial Pressure (MAP):** **50 – 70 mmHg**
- **Patient Temperature:** **28 °C (deep hypothermia)** to **32 °C (moderate)**
- **Pump Flow:** typically **4 – 6 L/min** for an average adult
- **O₂ Partial Pressure (PaO₂):** **150 – 300 mmHg**
- **CO₂ Partial Pressure (PaCO₂):** **35 – 45 mmHg**
- **Activated Clotting Time (ACT):** maintained > **480 seconds** (heparinization)
- **Hemoglobin Target:** **7 – 10 g/dL** (permissive anemia acceptable)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of the HLM

The HLM is a closed-loop (or semi-closed) hydraulic circuit comprising six major subsystems:

1. **Venous Drainage Subsystem** — Gravity siphon or vacuum-assisted drainage from SVC/IVC → reservoir.
2. **Reservoir** — Collects venous blood + cardiotomy suction blood; volume buffer; air trap.
3. **Pumps** — Generate flow (roller or centrifugal).
4. **Oxygenator** — Performs gas exchange (bubble or membrane type).
5. **Heat Exchanger** — Cools/warms the blood.
6. **Arterial Filter & Cannula** — Final bubble/particulate filter before re-entry into aorta.

## 2.2 Why Each Step is Needed — The Logic Chain

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering "WHY-HOW" Breakdown**

- **Step 1 — Venous Drainage:** Heart's right side must be emptied so surgeon can operate on a bloodless field. Gravity drain (siphon from height ~50 cm) or active vacuum (−40 to −80 mmHg).
- **Step 2 — Reservoir Storage:** Provides safety volume (~1000–2000 mL) for sudden volume shifts; allows air bubbles to rise and be vented.
- **Step 3 — Pumping:** Heart's function replaced. Roller pump = positive displacement (occlusive); Centrifugal pump = kinetic (non-occlusive, afterload-sensitive).
- **Step 4 — Oxygenation:** Blood film exposed to O₂ gas; CO₂ diffuses out. Membrane oxygenator mimics alveolar capillary membrane.
- **Step 5 — Heat Exchange:** Hypothermia reduces O₂ demand by ~7% per °C drop (Q₁₀ effect). After surgery, gradual rewarming at ≤ **1 °C per 5–10 min** prevents cerebral hyperthermia.
- **Step 6 — Arterial Filtration:** Removes micro-air, fat globules, platelet aggregates (filter pore size typically **40 μm**).

## 2.3 Types of Oxygenators — Comparative Analysis

| Feature | Bubble Oxygenator | Membrane Oxygenator |
|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Direct O₂ bubbling through blood | Gas diffuses across semi-permeable membrane (e.g., silicone, PP hollow fiber) |
| **Gas Exchange** | High efficiency (direct contact) | More physiological, gas-blood separation |
| **Blood Trauma** | High (hemolysis, foaming) | Low (gentler) |
| **Blood-Air Interface** | Yes (large) | No (miniature) |
| **Duration of Use** | Short (<2 hours) | Long (up to 6+ hours) |
| **Modern Usage** | Largely obsolete | **Gold standard (used in >95% of clinics today)** |
| **Example Material** | Polycarbonate chamber + sparger | Polypropylene (PP) hollow fibers; silicone membrane |

## 2.4 Types of Pumps — Comparative Analysis

| Feature | Roller Pump | Centrifugal Pump |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Rotating rollers compress tubing (peristaltic) | Impeller spins blood (centrifugal force) |
| **Flow Output** | **Constant** (independent of pressure) — positive displacement | **Pressure-dependent** (afterload-sensitive) |
| **Hemolysis Risk** | Higher (occlusion trauma) | Lower (non-occlusive) |
| **Air Pumping Risk** | **YES** (will pump air → embolism danger) | **NO** (air trapped at impeller center) |
| **Pressure Generation** | Up to ~700 mmHg | Up to ~900 mmHg |
| **Use Case** | Suction, cardioplegia delivery | **Main arterial pump in modern HLM** |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $BSA = \sqrt{\dfrac{H \times W}{3600}}$ (Mosteller) | Body Surface Area — for indexed flow | m² |
| 2 | $CI = \dfrac{CO}{BSA}$ | Cardiac Index — normal **2.5 – 4.0 L/min/m²** | L/min/m² |
| 3 | $Q_{pump} = CI \times BSA$ | Target CPB pump flow | L/min |
| 4 | $VO_2 = CO \times (CaO_2 - CvO_2)$ | Fick's principle — oxygen delivery/uptake | mL O₂/min |
| 5 | $CaO_2 = (1.34 \times Hb \times SaO_2) + (0.003 \times PaO_2)$ | Arterial oxygen content | mL O₂/dL blood |
| 6 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P \, r^4}{8 \mu L}$ | **Hagen-Poiseuille** — flow through cannula/tubing | mL/s |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | **Reynolds number** — turbulence check (Re > 2000 → turbulent) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{pump} = \rho g h$ | Gravitational venous drainage head (height × gravity) | Pa (or mmHg) |
| 9 | $\Delta P = \rho \, (v_{out}^2 - v_{in}^2) / 2$ | Pressure–velocity relation (Bernoulli-energy) | Pa |
| 10 | $Q_{10} \approx 2$ (or 7% per °C) | Metabolic rate doubles per 10 °C rise (used for hypothermia planning) | ratio |
| 11 | $Shear\ Stress\ \tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dr}$ | Shear rate in tubing — hemolysis predictor | Pa |
| 12 | $VO_2\,(hypothermia) = VO_2\,(37°C) \times 10^{(T-37)/10} \cdot Q_{10}$ | Reduced O₂ demand during cooling | mL O₂/min |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** In KTU answers, when asked for "pump flow rate for a 70 kg adult of height 170 cm," the examiner expects you to **first calculate BSA → then multiply by target CI (e.g., 2.4)**. Skipping step 1 is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications Beyond Cardiac Surgery

- **Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation (ECMO)** — long-term (days to weeks) HLM for ARDS, COVID-19, neonatal respiratory failure.
- **Liver transplant support** — modified circuits.
- **Rewarming of severe hypothermia victims**.
- **Regional perfusion chemotherapy** — for limb tumors.
- **Research tool** — isolated organ perfusion for transplant preservation (e.g., **Organ Care System / "heart in a box"**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Target Pump Flow Rate from Body Surface Area

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
A 55-year-old male patient (Height H = 170 cm, Weight W = 75 kg) is scheduled for CABG. Calculate his **Body Surface Area (BSA)** and the **required CPB pump flow rate (Q_pump)** if the target Cardiac Index is **2.4 L/min/m²**.

### Step 1: Apply the Mosteller Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
BSA &= \sqrt{\dfrac{H \times W}{3600}} \\[6pt]
BSA &= \sqrt{\dfrac{170 \times 75}{3600}} \\[6pt]
BSA &= \sqrt{\dfrac{12750}{3600}} \\[6pt]
BSA &= \sqrt{3.5417} \\[6pt]
BSA &= 1.882\ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step Explanation:* Mosteller's empirical formula uses H (in cm) × W (in kg), divided by 3600 (a normalization constant derived from regression on actual BSA measurements). The square root yields a value in m².

### Step 2: Apply the Pump Flow Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{pump} &= CI \times BSA \\[6pt]
Q_{pump} &= 2.4\ \dfrac{L/min}{m^2} \times 1.882\ m^2 \\[6pt]
Q_{pump} &= 4.517\ L/min
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step Explanation:* Pump flow in CPB is the mechanical equivalent of the patient's natural cardiac output. The cardiac index is normalized to BSA, so multiplying by the patient's actual BSA gives the absolute volumetric flow.

> **Result:** $Q_{pump} \approx 4.52\ L/min$ (within the standard 4–6 L/min range). ✅

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Oxygen Delivery (DO₂) During CPB

**Problem:** Patient Hb = 10 g/dL, SaO₂ = 100% (post-oxygenator), PaO₂ = 300 mmHg, Q_pump = 4.5 L/min. Calculate **Oxygen Delivery (DO₂)**.

### Step 1: Calculate Arterial Oxygen Content (CaO₂)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CaO_2 &= (1.34 \times Hb \times SaO_2) + (0.003 \times PaO_2) \\[6pt]
CaO_2 &= (1.34 \times 10 \times 1.00) + (0.003 \times 300) \\[6pt]
CaO_2 &= 13.4 + 0.9 \\[6pt]
CaO_2 &= 14.3\ mL\ O_2/dL\ blood
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step Explanation:*
- 1.34 mL O₂/g = Hüfner constant — grams of O₂ bound per gram of fully saturated Hb.
- 0.003 mL O₂/(dL·mmHg) = solubility coefficient of O₂ in plasma.
- SaO₂ is fractional (0–1); PaO₂ is in mmHg.

### Step 2: Convert to L/min Delivery

$$
\begin{aligned}
CaO_2\ \text{(in L)} &= 14.3\ mL/dL \times 10\ dL/L = 143\ mL\ O_2/L\ blood \\[6pt]
DO_2 &= CaO_2 \times Q_{pump} \\[6pt]
DO_2 &= 143\ mL\ O_2/L \times 4.5\ L/min \\[6pt]
DO_2 &= 643.5\ mL\ O_2/min
