#### Model Solution for (b) — MRI Principle + Larmor Derivation (7 marks)

**Principle (3 marks):**
- The human body contains abundant hydrogen nuclei (¹H) which possess nuclear spin.
- When placed in a strong static magnetic field **B₀**, the proton magnetic moments align parallel (or anti-parallel) to B₀, producing a net longitudinal magnetization **M₀**.
- A radiofrequency pulse at the **Larmor frequency** tips M₀ into the transverse plane.
- After the pulse, the magnetization precesses and relaxes, emitting a detectable RF signal called the **Free Induction Decay (FID)**.
- The FID is spatially encoded using **gradient fields** and reconstructed via **Fourier Transform** to form the image.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The torque on a magnetic moment $\vec{\mu}$ in field $\vec{B_0}$:

$$
\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}
$$

Using $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$:

$$
\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma (\vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0})
$$

For $\vec{B_0} = B_0 \hat{z}$, taking components and double-differentiating gives the second-order ODE:

$$
\frac{d^2\mu_x}{dt^2} + (\gamma B_0)^2 \mu_x = 0
$$

The solution is harmonic with angular frequency:

$$
\boxed{\;\omega_0 = \gamma B_0\;}
$$

Or in Hz: $f_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$.

**Numerical Verification (1 mark):** For $B_0 = 1.5$ T, $f_0 = (42.58 \times 10^6)(1.5) = 63.87$ MHz. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Sample Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the clinical applications of MRI in medical diagnosis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the function of the receiver chain in MRI. Compare T1 and T2 relaxation processes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Clinical Applications (7 marks)

1. **Neurological Imaging (2 marks)** — Brain tumors, stroke, multiple sclerosis, dementia, epilepsy focus localization, traumatic brain injury.
2. **Musculoskeletal (2 marks)** — Joint injuries (ACL tear), cartilage assessment, spinal cord compression, bone marrow pathology.
3. **Cardiovascular (1 mark)** — Cardiac function, viability, congenital heart disease, MR Angiography (vessel imaging).
4. **Oncology (1 mark)** — Tumor detection, staging, treatment planning (soft-tissue masses like breast, liver, prostate).
5. **Functional MRI (fMRI) (0.5 mark)** — Brain activity mapping via BOLD signal.
6. **MR Spectroscopy (MRS) (0.5 mark)** — Biochemical analysis of metabolites (NAA, Cho, Cr ratios).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Receiver Chain + T1/T2 Comparison (7 marks)

**Receiver Chain Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Patient → Receive Coil → Preamplifier (LNA) → Bandpass Filter → Mixer/Demodulator (downconversion) → ADC → Computer Reconstruction.

**Receiver Chain Function (2 marks):**
- **Preamplifier:** Boosts microvolt-level FID signal with minimal added noise (Noise Figure < 1 dB).
- **Demodulation:** Converts high-frequency Larmor signal to baseband I/Q components.
- **ADC:** High sampling rate digitizes the I/Q signal for digital Fourier processing.

**T1 vs T2 Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | T1 (Longitudinal / Spin-Lattice) | T2 (Transverse / Spin-Spin) |
|-----------|----------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Direction** | Recovery along z-axis | Decay in x-y plane |
| **Energy exchange** | Spin-to-lattice (energy released to surroundings) | Spin-to-spin (no net energy loss) |
| **Typical values at 1.5 T** | Fat 250 ms, CSF 4000 ms | Fat 70 ms, CSF 2000 ms |
| **Equation** | $M_z = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$ | $M_{xy} = M_{xy}(0)\, e^{-t/T_2}$ |
| **Always true** | $T_1 \geq T_2$ | — |
| **Used for** | T1-weighted anatomical contrast | T2-weighted pathology contrast |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes:**
> 1. ❌ **Confusing γ units:** Always write $\gamma_{^1H} = 42.58$ **MHz/T**, NOT 42.58 Hz/T. Examiner deducts 1 mark for wrong unit.
> 2. ❌ **Forgetting "1H" in T1/T2:** Examiner expects **spin-lattice** and **spin-spin** labels alongside T1 and T2.
> 3. ❌ **No mention of k-space and Fourier Transform:** In Part B questions on block diagrams, if you skip the reconstruction block, you lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. ❌ **Wrong arrow direction in block diagram:** Signal flows *up* from patient to display; control flows *down* from sequencer to coils. Drawing them incorrectly loses a mark.
> 5. ❌ **Missing "non-ionizing" keyword** in MRI advantages — examiner gives only partial credit if ionizing vs non-ionizing is omitted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — MRI Block Diagram & Applications**

- ✅ MRI = **non-ionizing**, NMR-based imaging modality exploiting ¹H protons.
- ✅ **Larmor frequency:** $f_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$; for ¹H, γ = **42.58 MHz/T**.
- ✅ **Flip angle** depends on RF B₁ amplitude and pulse duration τ.
- ✅ **T1** = longitudinal (spin-lattice) relaxation; **T2** = transverse (spin-spin) relaxation.
- ✅ Always: **T₁ ≥ T₂**.
- ✅ MRI block diagram has **two parallel paths:** control (sequencer → coils) and signal (patient → display).
- ✅ **Main Magnet + Shim Coils** produce homogeneous B₀.
- ✅ **Gradient Coils (X, Y, Z)** enable slice selection, phase encoding, frequency encoding.
- ✅ **Pulse Sequencer** is the master timing controller.
- ✅ **RF Coil** can be transmit (body coil) or receive (surface/phased array).
- ✅ **Image Reconstruction** uses **Inverse Fourier Transform of k-space** data.
- ✅ Clinical applications: **Neuro, Musculoskeletal, Cardiovascular, Oncology, fMRI, MRA, MRS**.
- ✅ Key advantage over CT: **no ionizing radiation** + superior soft-tissue contrast.
- ✅ Key SNR scaling: **SNR ∝ B₀^1.5 to B₀²** (motivates higher-field magnets).
- ✅ Standard clinical field strengths: **1.5 T** and **3 T**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Reconstruction, Ultrasonic Imaging: Different Modes, Positron Emission Tomography: Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEETVT615 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Image Reconstruction, Ultrasonic Imaging: Different Modes, Positron Emission Tomography: Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — IMAGE RECONSTRUCTION, ULTRASONIC IMAGING & PET

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Image Reconstruction (Tomographic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Image Reconstruction** is the mathematical process of converting a set of 1-D projection measurements (called *sinograms* or *profiles*) acquired at multiple angles around a patient/object into a 2-D cross-sectional image representing the internal distribution of a physical property (e.g., X-ray attenuation in CT, tracer concentration in PET, or spin density in MRI).

In modern biomedical imaging modalities such as **CT (Computed Tomography)**, **PET (Positron Emission Tomography)**, and **SPECT**, raw data is collected in the form of projections. These projections must be computationally "unfolded" to generate a usable diagnostic image. The dominant algorithm is the **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** technique, derived analytically from the Radon transform and the **Central Slice Theorem (Fourier Slice Theorem)**.

**Conceptual Analogy 🏠:**
Imagine a transparent gumball machine filled with opaque balls of varying sizes. You cannot see through it directly, but you can rotate a flashlight around the machine and, for each angle, record the *total shadow length* cast on the opposite wall (this is a 1-D projection). After collecting 180 such shadow profiles from different angles, you feed them to a computer. The computer mathematically "back-traces" each shadow to where it could have originated, smearing it back across the image space. After enough angles, the smears intersect to form a clear image of the balls inside. That "back-tracing" is the essence of **image reconstruction**.

### 1.2 Ultrasonic Imaging — Different Modes

> [!NOTE]
> **Ultrasonic Imaging** is a non-invasive, non-ionizing diagnostic modality that uses high-frequency mechanical sound waves (**>20 kHz**, typically **2–15 MHz** in medical use) to visualize soft tissues, blood flow, and fetal development in real time.

The transducer sends pulses, and the system interprets returning echoes to construct images in several **modes**:

| Mode | Full Name | Key Output | Primary Clinical Use |
|------|-----------|------------|---------------------|
| **A-Mode** | Amplitude Mode | 1-D spikes of echo amplitude vs. depth | Eye biometry, echoencephalography |
| **B-Mode** | Brightness Mode | 2-D grayscale anatomical image | General sonography, obstetrics |
| **M-Mode** | Motion Mode | Time-motion display of moving structures | Cardiac valve motion (echocardiography) |
| **Doppler** | Doppler Mode | Velocity/flow color/spectral map | Blood flow, fetal heart rate |

**Conceptual Analogy 🎣:**
Think of an ultrasound like a fishing sonar in a lake. The boat sends out a "ping" (sound wave). When the ping hits a fish, it bounces back, and the time it took tells the fisherman *how deep* the fish is, while the strength of the return tells *how big/dense* the fish is. **A-mode** is a single vertical line showing fish depths; **B-mode** is a 2-D "fish map"; **M-mode** is like a movie reel of one specific fish moving up and down with time; and **Doppler** detects whether the fish is moving toward or away from the boat (and how fast).

### 1.3 Positron Emission Tomography (PET)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Positron Emission Tomography (PET)** is a nuclear medicine imaging technique that produces 3-D images of functional/metabolic processes inside the body by detecting pairs of **511 keV gamma photons** emitted *annihilationally* following positron emission from a radioactively labeled tracer (e.g., **¹⁸F-Fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG)**).

**Conceptual Analogy 💡:**
Imagine you swallow a tiny grain of "smart sand" that lights up only when it finds sugar (cancer cells love sugar). The sand does not stay lit — it self-destructs by spitting out two invisible "twins" of light in *exactly opposite directions* (180° apart). The PET scanner is a hollow ring of light detectors that wait for these twin flashes. Whenever two detectors fire at the *exact same instant* on opposite sides, the computer draws a straight line between them. After millions of such coincidence events, the lines cross heavily wherever the "smart sand" was concentrated — revealing a metabolic "hotspot." That crossing point is the suspected tumor or active brain region.

**Key physical constant for PET:** Rest mass energy equivalence $E = m_0c^2 = 511 \text{ keV}$ for electron/positron annihilation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Image Reconstruction — Theory of CT-Style Tomography

### 2.1.1 The Radon Transform

A 1-D projection $p(\rho, \theta)$ is obtained by integrating the 2-D linear attenuation coefficient distribution $\mu(x, y)$ along a line defined by projection angle $\theta$ and radial offset $\rho$:

$$p(\rho, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(x, y)\, \delta(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta - \rho) \, dx \, dy$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Dirac delta function which restricts integration to the straight line $x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta = \rho$.

### 2.1.2 Central Slice Theorem (Fourier Slice Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 1-D Fourier transform of a projection acquired at angle $\theta$ equals a 1-D slice through the 2-D Fourier transform of the object, passing through the origin at that same angle $\theta$.

Mathematically:
$$P(\omega, \theta) = M(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta)$$
where $P$ is the 1-D FT of the projection, and $M$ is the 2-D FT of $\mu(x,y)$.

### 2.1.3 Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)

> [!NOTE]
> **FBP** is the workhorse algorithm of CT and PET image reconstruction. It *filters* each projection with a high-pass ramp filter to compensate for the $1/\vert \omega \vert$ blurring inherent in naive back-projection, then *smears* it back across the image.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Acquire $N$ projections at angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_N$ uniformly spanning $180°$.
2. Compute the 1-D Fourier Transform of each projection.
3. Multiply (filter) by the **ramp filter** $\vert \omega \vert$ in frequency domain.
4. Inverse Fourier Transform each filtered projection back to spatial domain.
5. **Back-project** (smear) each filtered profile across the image matrix.
6. Sum all back-projected images to obtain the reconstructed slice.

### 2.1.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Image Reconstruction

| Formula | Expression | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---------|-----------|------------------|-------|
| Radon Transform | $p(\rho,\theta) = \int \mu(x,y)\delta(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta - \rho)\,dx\,dy$ | Line integral of attenuation | dimensionless / cm |
| Central Slice Theorem | $P(\omega,\theta) = M(\omega\cos\theta,\omega\sin\theta)$ | Projection-FT = slice of object-FT | — |
| FBP ramp filter | $H(\omega) = \vert \omega \vert$ | High-pass filter to remove $1/r$ blur | 1/length |
| Back-projection operator | $b(x,y) = \int_0^{\pi} p(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta, \theta)\,d\theta$ | Smearing projection across image | — |
| Attenuation Law (Beer–Lambert) | $I = I_0\, e^{-\int \mu(x)\,dx}$ | X-ray intensity after passing through tissue | intensity |
| Shepp-Logan filter (modified ramp) | $H_{SL}(\omega) = \vert \omega \vert \cdot \text{sinc}(\omega/2\omega_c)$ | Ramp × windowed sinc for noise reduction | — |
| PET coincidence timing window | $\Delta t \approx 6$–$12$ ns | Window for valid annihilation pair detection | seconds |

## 2.2 Ultrasonic Imaging — Modes in Depth

### 2.2.1 A-Mode (Amplitude Mode)

* Simplest mode: a single transducer fires a pulse; the receiver records echoes as a function of time.
* Display: vertical spikes on an oscilloscope; **x-axis = depth**, **y-axis = echo amplitude**.
* No anatomical image — purely a 1-D "ruler" of tissue interfaces.
* Used in ophthalmology (axial eye length) and in detecting midline brain shift.

### 2.2.2 B-Mode (Brightness Mode)

* The transducer is **scanned mechanically or electronically** across an arc to produce many parallel A-lines.
* Each echo's amplitude is mapped to a **gray-level brightness pixel** at its corresponding depth.
* The combined raster of pixels forms a real-time 2-D anatomical cross-section.
* This is the standard mode used in obstetrics, abdominal, and vascular ultrasound.

### 2.2.3 M-Mode (Motion Mode)

* Uses a single, fixed B-mode line but displays the echoes as a function of time on the horizontal axis.
* Excellent for visualizing fast-moving structures such as **cardiac valve leaflets** and **fetal heart walls**.
* Time resolution is very high (often >1000 frames/s) because no spatial scanning is required.

### 2.2.4 Doppler Ultrasound

* Exploits the **Doppler shift** between transmitted ($f_t$) and reflected ($f_r$) ultrasound from moving red blood cells or cardiac tissue.
* The frequency shift is:
  $$\Delta f = f_r - f_t = \frac{2 f_t v \cos\alpha}{c}$$
  where $v$ is blood velocity, $c$ is the speed of sound in soft tissue ($\approx 1540$ m/s), and $\alpha$ is the Doppler angle (angle between beam and flow direction).
* **Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler**: Two crystals — one transmitting continuously, one receiving. Can measure very high velocities but no depth resolution.
* **Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler**: Same crystal pulses; range-gated to a specific depth; velocity limited by Nyquist.
* **Color Doppler**: PW Doppler applied across many scan lines; velocity is color-coded (red = toward transducer, blue = away) and overlaid on B-mode.
* **Spectral Doppler**: Velocity vs. time waveform displayed as a spectral tracing.

### 2.2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Ultrasonic Imaging

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Meaning | Units |
|--------------------|-----------|---------|-------|
| Pulse-echo depth | $d = \dfrac{c\,t}{2}$ | Depth of reflecting interface | m |
| Speed of sound in soft tissue | $c \approx 1540$ m/s | Assumed average for calculations | m/s |
| Axial resolution | $\Delta d = \dfrac{c}{2 \Delta f}$ | Min distinguishable axial distance | m |
| Lateral resolution | $\Delta L \approx \lambda \cdot z / D$ | Beam-width limited resolution | m |
| Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | Wavelength of ultrasound | m |
| Doppler shift | $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_t v \cos\alpha}{c}$ | Frequency shift from moving reflector | Hz |
| Frame rate (B-mode) | FR = $c / (2 N \cdot d_{max})$ | Real-time image rate | Hz |
| Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho c$ | Product of density and sound speed | kg/(m²·s) |
| Reflection coefficient (intensity) | $R = \left[\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right]^2$ | Fraction of intensity reflected | dimensionless |
| Attenuation coefficient (soft tissue) | $\alpha \approx 0.5$ dB/cm/MHz (approx.) | Loss per unit depth per MHz | dB/(cm·MHz) |

## 2.3 Positron Emission Tomography — Principle

### 2.3.1 The Physics Chain

The PET imaging chain has three major physical stages:

1. **Tracer administration**: A positron-emitting radioisotope is chemically attached to a metabolically active molecule (e.g., **¹⁸F → FDG**). The tracer concentrates in tissues with high glucose metabolism (brain, tumors).
2. **Positron emission and annihilation**: The unstable nucleus emits a positron (β⁺). The positron travels a short distance (typically <1–2 mm — the *positron range*), thermalizes, and then **annihilates** with a nearby electron. The annihilation converts both masses into two **511 keV photons** emitted at almost exactly **180°** to each other (conservation of momentum).
3. **Coincidence detection**: A ring of scintillation detectors (often **BGO — Bismuth Germanate**, **LYSO — Lutetium-Yttrium Oxyorthosilicate**, or **LSO**) registers both photons nearly simultaneously (within a ~6–12 ns window).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because the two annihilation photons travel in opposite directions, drawing a straight line between two coincident detectors localizes the annihilation event to a **Line of Response (LOR)**.

### 2.3.2 Types of Coincidences

| Type | Description | Effect on Image |
|------|-------------|-----------------|
| **True coincidence** | Both photons from a single annihilation strike the detectors without scattering | Desired event — accurate LOR |
| **Scattered coincidence** | One or both photons Compton-scatter before detection | Mis-positioned LOR → reduced contrast |
| **Random (accidental) coincidence** | Two unrelated photons from different annihilations arrive within the timing window | Uniform background noise |

### 2.3.3 Image Reconstruction in PET

PET is fundamentally a **tomographic** modality — it requires image reconstruction to convert millions of LORs into a 3-D image of tracer concentration. Two main methods are used:

1. **Analytic — Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)**: Fast, simple, but produces streak artifacts and noise at low count densities.
2. **Iterative — OSEM (Ordered Subsets Expectation Maximization)**: Models the physics of detection (attenuation, scatter, randoms) statistically; produces higher-quality, lower-noise images but is computationally intensive.

### 2.3.4 Attenuation Correction in PET

Photons traveling through the body can be absorbed (photoelectric effect) or scattered (Compton scatter), reducing the measured count rate from deeper structures. **Attenuation correction** uses either a **transmission scan** with a ⁶⁷Ge/⁶⁸Ga rod source or, in modern hybrid **PET/CT** scanners, the CT data to correct for this.

> [!NOTE]
> The attenuation correction factor along LOR at angle $\theta$ is:
> $$ACF(\theta) = \exp\left(+\int_{\text{LOR}} \mu(x)\,dx\right)$$
> where $\mu(x)$ is the linear attenuation coefficient at 511 keV along the path.

### 2.3.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — PET

| Formula | Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---------|-----------|---------|-------|
| Annihilation photon energy | $E_\gamma = 511$ keV | Energy of each photon from e⁻/e⁺ annihilation | keV |
| Mass-energy equivalence | $E = m_0 c^2$ | Each 511 keV photon = 511 keV/c² of rest mass | J or eV |
| Number of decay events needed | $N = A \cdot t$ (with activity $A$ in Bq, time $t$ in s) | Count statistics for adequate SNR | counts |
| Attenuation correction | $I_{\text{corr}} = I_{\text{meas}} \cdot e^{\int \mu\,dx}$ | Restores true count rate | counts |
| Spatial resolution (modern PET) | $\approx 4$–$5$ mm | Limited by positron range + detector size | mm |
| Sensitivity (typical clinical PET) | $\approx 5$–$10$ kcps/(kBq/mL) | Count rate per unit activity concentration | cps · mL/kBq |
| Half-life of ¹⁸F | $t_{1/2} = 109.7$ min | Decay constant for FDG | min |
| Decay constant | $\lambda = \ln(2)/t_{1/2}$ | For exponential decay $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | 1/s |
| Activity remaining | $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Standard radioactive decay law | Bq |

# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Radon Transform to Filtered Back-Projection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation shows the analytical bridge between the Central Slice Theorem and the practical FBP algorithm. Each step is written out completely with its logical interpretation.

### Step 1 — Start with the 2-D Inverse Fourier Transform of the object $\mu(x, y)$:

$$\mu(x, y) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} M(\omega_x, \omega_y)\, e^{j2\pi(\omega_x x + \omega_y y)} \, d\omega_x\, d\omega_y$$

*Explanation:* Any 2-D image can be exactly reconstructed if we know its full 2-D frequency spectrum $M$.

### Step 2 — Switch from Cartesian $(\omega_x, \omega_y)$ to polar $(\omega, \theta)$ coordinates:

Define $\omega_x = \omega\cos\theta$ and $\omega_y = \omega\sin\theta$. The Jacobian of this transformation gives $d\omega_x\, d\omega_y = \vert \omega \vert\, d\omega\, d\theta$. Substituting:

$$\mu(x, y) = \int_{0}^{\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} M(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta)\, \vert \omega \vert\, e^{j2\pi\omega(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} \, d\omega\, d\theta$$

*Explanation:* The polar form exposes a key point — at every projection angle $\theta$, the values of $M$ lie along a radial slice through the origin.

### Step 3 — Apply the Central Slice Theorem:

By the Central Slice Theorem, the values of $M(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta)$ along that radial line are *exactly* the 1-D Fourier transform of the projection at angle $\theta$, namely $P(\omega, \theta)$:

$$\mu(x, y) = \int_{0}^{\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} P(\omega, \theta)\, \vert \omega \vert\, e^{j2\pi\omega(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} \, d\omega\, d\theta$$

*Explanation:* The 2-D FT of the object is now replaced by measurable projections in the frequency domain.

### Step 4 — Recognize the inner integral as a 1-D inverse FT of $P(\omega,\theta)\vert\omega\vert$:

Define the **filtered projection** at angle $\theta$ as:

$$p_F(\rho, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} P(\omega, \theta)\, \vert \omega \vert\, e^{j2\pi\omega\rho}\, d\omega$$

This is the inverse FT of the *filtered* (by $\vert\omega\vert$) projection. The filter $\vert\omega\vert$ is the **ramp filter** that compensates for the $1/\vert\omega\vert$ blurring introduced by simple back-projection.

### Step 5 — Substitute back:

$$\mu(x, y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} p_F(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta,\, \theta)\, d\theta$$

*Explanation:* For every angle $\theta$, take the filtered projection, evaluate it at the radial coordinate $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ corresponding to the point $(x, y)$, and sum over all angles. This is the **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) formula** in its final form.

### Step 6 — Numerical Implementation Summary:

1. Acquire $N$ projections $p(\rho, \theta_k)$ where $\theta_k = k\pi/N$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$.
2. FFT each projection to get $P(\omega, \theta_k)$.
3. Multiply each by ramp filter $H(\omega) = \vert \omega \vert$ (or Shepp–Logan $H_{SL}(\omega)$ for noise control).
4. Inverse FFT to obtain filtered projection $p_F(\rho, \theta_k)$.
5. For each pixel $(x, y)$, sum $p_F(x\cos\theta_k + y\sin\theta_k, \theta_k)$ over $k$.
6. Resulting image is the reconstructed $\mu(x, y)$.

> [!NOTE]
> The total angular range required is $\pi$ (180°), because a parallel projection at angle $\theta$ is identical to one at $\theta + \pi$. Fan-beam and cone-beam geometries extend this to a $360°$ rotation in practice.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — CT Attenuation and Reconstruction

**Problem:**
A CT scanner acquires a single projection at $\theta = 0°$. The projection has two discrete samples:
- $p(0, 0) = 1.20$ (sum of attenuation across the center)
- $p(1 \text{ cm}, 0) = 0.85$

If the ramp filter $H(\omega) = \vert \omega \vert$ is applied, find the filtered projection value at $\rho = 0$ cm (assume one-sided discrete filter approximation with weights $[-0.5, \, 1.0, \, -0.5]$ for $\omega = -1, 0, +1$ in cycles/cm).

**Solution — Step by Step:**

### Step A — Discretize the projection
Let $p_0 = 1.20$ at $\rho = 0$ and $p_1 = 0.85$ at $\rho = 1$ cm. Pad with zeros: $\dots, 0, 1.20, 0.85, 0, \dots$.

### Step B — Compute the discrete 1-D Fourier transform (conceptual)
For simplicity, evaluate the filter's effect directly in spatial domain using the discrete convolution equivalent of the ramp filter.

### Step C — Apply the spatial-domain filter kernel
The 3-point ramp convolution kernel applied at sample $\rho = 0$ is:
$$p_F(0) = (-0.5)\cdot p(-1) + (1.0)\cdot p(0) + (-0.5)\cdot p(1)$$
$$p_F(0) = (-0.5)(0) + (1.0)(1.20) + (-0.5)(0.85)$$
$$p_F(0) = 0 + 1.20 - 0.425$$
$$p_F(0) = 0.775$$

### Step D — Interpretation
The filtered projection at $\rho = 0$ is $0.775$, which is sharper (higher local contrast) than the unfiltered value of $1.20$. This sharpened profile is what gets smeared back across the image during back-projection. The high-pass ramp filter removes the $1/r$ blur that would otherwise make the reconstructed point spread function appear fuzzy.

> [!TIP]
> For more sample points, the discrete ramp filter kernel in spatial domain is the *inverse discrete Fourier transform* of $\vert \omega \vert$, which gives the well-known kernel $\frac{1}{2}[-1, 0, 1]$ at 3 points or the longer $[-1/4, 0, 1/2, 0, -1/4]$ at 5 points. In practice, the Shepp–Logan filter $H_{SL}(\omega) = \vert \omega \vert \cdot \text{sinc}(\omega/2\omega_c)$ is preferred because it suppresses high-frequency noise.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Doppler Shift Calculation

**Problem:**
A 5 MHz ultrasound beam is directed at blood flowing in an artery. The Doppler angle is $\alpha = 60°$. The speed of sound in tissue is $c = 1540$ m/s. If the measured frequency shift is $\Delta f = 1500$ Hz, what is the blood flow velocity?

**Solution — Step by Step:**

### Step 1 — State the Doppler equation
$$\Delta f = \frac{2 f_t v \cos\alpha}{c}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $v$
$$v = \frac{\Delta f \cdot c}{2 f_t \cos\alpha}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the values
$$v = \frac{(1500 \text{ Hz})(1540 \text{ m/s})}{2 \cdot (5 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}) \cdot \cos 60°}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate denominator
$\cos 60° = 0.5$, so $2 \cdot 5 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.5 = 5 \times 10^6$ Hz.

### Step 5 — Compute
$$v = \frac{1500 \cdot 1540}{5 \times 10^6} = \frac{2{,}310{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000} = 0.462 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to clinically useful units
$$v = 0.462 \text{ m/s} = 46.2 \text{ cm/s}$$

This is a normal value for peak systolic velocity in a peripheral artery.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — PET Radioactive Decay and Count Statistics

**Problem:**
A patient is injected with 370 MBq of ¹⁸F-FDG. How much activity remains after 90 minutes? (Half-life of ¹⁸F = 109.7 min.)

**Solution — Step by Step:**

### Step 1 — State the decay law
$$A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the decay constant
$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{109.7 \text{ min}} = 6.32 \times 10^{-3} \text{ min}^{-1}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute
$$A(90) = 370 \cdot e^{-(6.32 \times 10^{-3})(90)} = 370 \cdot e^{-0.569}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the exponential
$e^{-0.569} = 0.566$

### Step 5 — Compute
$$A(90) = 370 \cdot 0.566 \approx 209 \text{ MBq}$$

> [!TIP]
> PET scans are typically started **60 minutes post-injection** to allow tracer uptake while activity is still sufficient for good count statistics. With 370 MBq initial dose, by scan time the activity is in the 200 MBq range — providing millions of coincidence counts over a 10–20 min acquisition.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Reconstruction Algorithm

| Scenario | Best Algorithm | Reason |
|----------|---------------|--------|
| High count data, real-time CT | **FBP** | Speed, simplicity, deterministic |
| Low count PET data | **OSEM** (iterative) | Better noise handling, models Poisson statistics |
| Sparse-view CT (dose reduction) | **Iterative / Compressed sensing** | FBP produces severe streaks |
| Metal artifacts in CT | **Iterative with metal-aware priors** | FBP cannot handle missing data |
| Quantitative PET (SUV measurement) | **OSEM + PSF modeling + TOF** | Most accurate count recovery |
| Cardiac CT (motion) | **FBP with gating or motion-compensated iterative** | Time-resolved reconstruction |

# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CT / PET Image Reconstruction Pipeline (Filtered Back-Projection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient / Object with unknown internal structure] --> B[Acquire N projections at angles theta_1 to theta_N]
    B --> C[1D Fourier Transform of each projection]
    C --> D[Multiply by ramp filter vert omega vert]
    D --> E[Inverse Fourier Transform of filtered projection]
    E --> F[Back-project smearing each filtered profile across image matrix]
    F --> G[Sum over all N angles]
    G --> H[Final reconstructed 2D image mu x y]
    H --> I[Display diagnose archive]
```

## 4.2 Central Slice Theorem — Visual Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[2D Object f x y in spatial domain] -->|2D Fourier Transform| M[2D FT F omega_x omega_y]
    P[1D Projection p rho theta at angle theta] -->|1D Fourier Transform| S[1D FT slice P omega theta]
    M -->|slice along radial line at angle theta| S
    S -->|inverse 1D FT and back-project| R[Reconstructed slice]
```

## 4.3 Ultrasound Mode Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Clinical Question] --> Q1{Need anatomical image}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Need motion over time}
    Q2 -->|No| Bmode[B mode 2D grayscale]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Mmode[M mode motion of cardiac valves]
    Q1 -->|No 1D only| Amode[A mode 1D spikes ocular]
    Start --> Q3{Need blood flow velocity}
    Q3 -->|Yes and color map| Color[Color Doppler overlaid on B mode]
    Q3 -->|Yes and waveform| Spectral[Spectral Doppler velocity vs time]
    Q3 -->|Yes and high velocity| CW[Continuous Wave Doppler]
    Q3 -->|Yes and depth specific| PW[Pulsed Wave Doppler]
```

## 4.4 PET Imaging Chain — From Tracer to Image

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Inject radioactive tracer eg 18F FDG] --> T2[Tracer localizes in metabolically active tissue]
    T2 --> T3[Positron beta plus emitted from nucleus]
    T3 --> T4[Positron annihilates with nearby electron]
    T4 --> T5[Two 511 keV gamma photons emitted back to back 180 degrees]
    T5 --> T6[Coincidence detection by opposing detector pair]
    T6 --> T7[Line of Response LOR stored in sinogram]
    T7 --> T8[Image reconstruction FBP or OSEM]
    T8 --> T9[3D image of tracer concentration]
    T9 --> T10[Display diagnose and quantify SUV]
    Scatter[Scattered coincidence adds noise] -.-> T6
    Random[Random accidental coincidence adds background] -.-> T6
    Atten[Attenuation correction using CT in PET CT] -.-> T8
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Patient
        Pt[Patient on sliding gantry table]
    end
    subgraph CTsubsys[CT Subsystem]
        XRay[X ray tube] --> Col[Collimator and detector array]
    end
    subgraph PETsubsys[PET Subsystem]
        Ring[Ring of scintillation crystals BGO LYSO]
        Coin[Coincidence processor]
        Sino[Sinogram memory]

    end
    Pt --- CTsubsys
    Pt --- PETsubsys
    Col --> Fuse[Image fusion and registration]

    Fuse --> Disp[Display workstation PACS archive]
```

## 4.6 Ultrasound Transducer and Beam Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Pulser[Pulsed electrical signal] --> Tr[Transducer PZT crystal]
    Tr -->|piezoelectric conversion| Beam[Focused ultrasound beam 2 to 15 MHz]
    Beam -->|penetrates tissue| T1[Tissue interface 1]
    T1 -->|partial reflection| Echo1[Echo returning to transducer]
    Beam --> T2[Tissue interface 2 deeper]
    T2 -->|partial reflection| Echo2[Echo returning later]
    Echo1 --> Tr
```

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Define the Central Slice Theorem and explain its role in tomographic image reconstruction.**

**Model Answer:**
The Central Slice Theorem (also called the Fourier Slice Theorem) states that the one-dimensional Fourier transform of a parallel-beam projection of an object, taken at angle $\theta$, is equal to the values of the two-dimensional Fourier transform of the object along a radial line through the origin at that same angle $\theta$. In tomographic image reconstruction (e.g., CT or PET), this theorem is the mathematical foundation of the Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) algorithm. It allows us to populate the 2-D frequency domain of the object by collecting projections at many angles, and then inverse-transform to obtain the spatial image. The FBP algorithm applies a ramp filter $\vert \omega \vert$ to each frequency-domain projection to remove the $1/r$ blur of simple back-projection, yielding a sharp reconstruction.

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**List the different modes of ultrasonic imaging and state the primary clinical use of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | Display | Primary Clinical Use |
|------|---------|----------------------|
| **A-mode** (Amplitude) | 1-D spikes of echo amplitude vs. depth | Ocular biometry, echoencephalography |
| **B-mode** (Brightness) | 2-D real-time grayscale image | General abdominal, obstetric, vascular sonography |
| **M-mode** (Motion) | Time-motion display of moving structures | Cardiac valve motion in echocardiography |
| **Doppler** (CW/PW/Color/Spectral) | Velocity display of moving blood | Blood flow measurement, fetal heart rate monitoring |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> - **In FBP questions:** Students often forget to mention the *ramp filter* $\vert \omega \vert$ — they describe only the "smearing" step (back-projection) without explaining why filtering is essential. This loses **3 marks** in the 14-mark answer.
> - **In Doppler questions:** Confusing the factor of 2 (round-trip) is a frequent error. The reflected wave sees the moving blood as both a moving source and a moving observer in pulse-echo systems, hence the factor 2.
> - **In PET questions:** Do not confuse **annihilation** (electron + positron → 2 × 511 keV photons) with **photoelectric absorption**. Examiners specifically test whether students understand positron range vs. photon travel.
> - **In Ultrasonic modes:** Students sometimes incorrectly write that B-mode uses a single transducer. Always clarify it scans multiple A-lines and combines them into a 2-D image.
> - Always **state assumptions** ($c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue, parallel-beam geometry, etc.) — KTU examiners award at least 1 mark for clear assumptions.

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Image Reconstruction Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4, 2024 Scheme]**

#### (a) Explain the principles of image reconstruction in Computed Tomography. Derive the Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) algorithm from the Central Slice Theorem, clearly stating the role of the ramp filter. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Image reconstruction in CT is the process of converting a series of 1-D X-ray projections (line integrals of the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu(x,y)$) acquired at multiple angles around the patient into a 2-D cross-sectional image. The mathematically rigorous approach is the **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** algorithm, based on the **Radon transform** and the **Central Slice Theorem**.

**Radon Transform (1 mark):**
A parallel-beam projection at angle $\theta$ and offset $\rho$ is the line integral:
$$p(\rho, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(x, y)\, \delta(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta - \rho) \, dx \, dy$$

**Central Slice Theorem (1 mark):**
If $M(\omega_x, \omega_y)$ is the 2-D Fourier transform of $\mu(x,y)$ and $P(\omega, \theta)$ is the 1-D FT of the projection $p(\rho, \theta)$, then:
$$P(\omega, \theta) = M(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta)$$

**Derivation of FBP (3 marks):**
Starting from the 2-D inverse FT of $\mu(x,y)$:
$$\mu(x, y) = \iint M(\omega_x, \omega_y)\, e^{j2\pi(\omega_x x + \omega_y y)} \, d\omega_x d\omega_y$$
Convert to polar coordinates $(\omega, \theta)$ with $\omega_x = \omega\cos\theta$, $\omega_y = \omega\sin\theta$ and Jacobian $\vert \omega \vert$:
$$\mu(x, y) = \int_{0}^{\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} M(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta)\, \vert \omega \vert\, e^{j2\pi\omega(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} \, d\omega\, d\theta$$
Apply the Central Slice Theorem, replacing the inner integrand with $P(\omega, \theta)\vert\omega\vert$:
$$\mu(x, y) = \int_{0}^{\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} P(\omega, \theta)\, \vert \omega \vert\, e^{j2\pi\omega(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} \, d\omega\, d\theta$$
Recognize the inner integral as the inverse 1-D FT of the *filtered* projection:
$$p_F(\rho, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} P(\omega, \theta)\, \vert \omega \vert\, e^{j2\pi\omega\rho}\, d\omega$$
Therefore:
$$\mu(x, y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} p_F(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta, \theta)\, d\theta$$

**Role of the Ramp Filter (1 mark):**
The factor $\vert \omega \vert$ is the **ramp filter** $H(\omega)$. It is a high-pass filter that compensates for the $1/r$ blurring inherent in unfiltered back-projection. Without it, the reconstructed image would appear blurred with a $1/r$ point spread function. The filter emphasizes high spatial frequencies (sharp edges) and suppresses low frequencies, restoring image sharpness.

#### (b) Discuss the two main types of image reconstruction algorithms used in PET, namely Filtered Back-Projection and OSEM. Compare their performance in low-count clinical scenarios. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**FBP in PET (2 marks):**
FBP is the direct analytical inversion of the Radon transform. Each acquired sinogram row (1-D projection at one angle) is filtered with a ramp filter (or a regularized variant such as Shepp-Logan), then back-projected and summed to form the image. **Advantages:** fast, deterministic, easy to implement. **Disadvantages:** produces streak artifacts and amplified noise when count statistics are low, which is the typical case in clinical PET (where limited injected dose and short scan times are used for patient safety and comfort).

**OSEM in PET (2 marks):**
Ordered Subsets Expectation Maximization (OSEM) is an **iterative** statistical reconstruction algorithm. It divides the data into ordered subsets and, for each iteration, compares the forward-projected estimate of the image with the measured sinogram. The ratio (with Poisson noise modeling) is back-projected to update the image. **Advantages:** models the Poisson nature of emission data, allows incorporation of physics corrections (attenuation, scatter, randoms, point-spread function, time-of-flight), produces lower-noise images at equivalent counts. **Disadvantages:** slower computation, non-deterministic convergence, requires stopping criterion to avoid noise over-fitting.

**Comparison in Low-Count Scenarios (3 marks):**

| Aspect | FBP | OSEM |
|--------|-----|------|
| Noise behavior | Noise amplified (high-frequency boost) | Noise suppressed by Poisson modeling |
| Convergence at low counts | Single pass — poor image quality | Iterative refinement recovers image |
| Streak artifacts | Severe at low counts | Mild, controllable via iteration number |
| Quantitative accuracy (SUV) | Poor at low counts | Excellent even at low counts |
| Computational cost | Very low | Higher (parallelizable on GPU) |
| Clinical preference for modern PET | Rarely used | Standard of care |

**Conclusion:** Modern clinical PET scanners almost exclusively use OSEM (or its variants such as BSREM/Q.Clear or TOF-OSEM) because clinical acquisitions are routinely low-count due to dose reduction efforts and short scan protocols.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Ultrasonic Modes & PET Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4, 2024 Scheme]**

#### (a) Describe the various modes of ultrasonic imaging (A, B, M, and Doppler modes). Explain the Doppler principle and derive the Doppler shift equation for blood flow measurement. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**A-Mode (1 mark):**
Amplitude Mode — a single transducer emits a short ultrasound pulse and receives echoes. The signal is displayed as vertical spikes on an oscilloscope; the x-axis represents depth (calculated from $d = ct/2$) and the y-axis represents echo amplitude. Used in ophthalmology for axial eye length measurement and in echoencephalography.

**B-Mode (1 mark):**
Brightness Mode — the transducer is scanned (mechanically or electronically with phased arrays) across an arc, producing many parallel A-lines. Echo amplitude is converted to gray-scale brightness at the corresponding pixel position. The combined pixels form a real-time 2-D cross-sectional image. This is the most common mode in clinical sonography.

**M-Mode (1 mark):**
Motion Mode — a single fixed ultrasound line is used, but the display sweeps horizontally with time. The result is a time-motion strip showing moving structures (e.g., cardiac valve leaflets). Excellent temporal resolution for echocardiography.

**Doppler Mode (1 mark):**
Doppler ultrasound detects motion (typically of red blood cells) by measuring the frequency shift between transmitted and reflected ultrasound. Implementations include Continuous Wave (CW), Pulsed Wave (PW), Color Doppler, and Spectral Doppler.

**Doppler Principle & Derivation (3 marks):**
Consider a stationary transducer transmitting ultrasound of frequency $f_t$ toward blood moving with velocity $v$ at angle $\alpha$ to the beam. Speed of sound in tissue is $c$.

* The blood cell "sees" a Doppler-shifted frequency $f'$ (moving observer):
$$f' = f_t \cdot \frac{c - v\cos\alpha}{c}$$

* The reflected wave is emitted by a moving source, so the transducer receives:
$$f_r = f' \cdot \frac{c}{c - v\cos\alpha}$$

* Substituting:
$$f_r = f_t \cdot \frac{c - v\cos\alpha}{c - v\cos\alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{c} \cdot c \text{ (after algebra)}$$

* The Doppler shift is:
$$\Delta f = f_r - f_t = \frac{2 f_t v \cos\alpha}{c}$$

Solving for velocity:
$$v = \frac{\Delta f \cdot c}{2 f_t \cos\alpha}$$

This equation forms the basis of all Doppler blood flow velocity measurements.

#### (b) Explain the principle of Positron Emission Tomography. Discuss the physics of positron annihilation, coincidence detection, and the need for image reconstruction. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction & Tracer (1 mark):**
PET is a nuclear medicine imaging modality that visualizes *functional* and *metabolic* processes rather than anatomy. A positron-emitting radioisotope (e.g., ¹⁸F with half-life 109.7 min) is chemically attached to a biologically active molecule (e.g., FDG — fluorodeoxyglucose). The tracer is injected and concentrates in tissues with high metabolic activity (brain, tumors, infection).

**Positron Emission (1 mark):**
Inside the unstable nucleus, a proton converts into a neutron, emitting a positron (β⁺) and a neutrino. The positron is the anti-particle of the electron. After emission, the positron travels a short distance (1–2 mm) — the **positron range** — losing kinetic energy in tissue.

**Annihilation (1 mark):**
When the positron thermalizes and meets a nearby electron, both particles annihilate, converting their combined rest mass into energy. By $E = m_0 c^2 = 511$ keV for each particle, two **511 keV photons** are produced, emitted in nearly opposite directions (180° apart) to conserve momentum.

**Coincidence Detection (1 mark):**
A ring of scintillation detectors (BGO, LSO, LYSO) registers the two photons. If two detectors fire within a narrow time window (typically 6–12 ns), the system records a *coincidence event* and draws a **Line of Response (LOR)** between them. Millions of LORs form a **sinogram**.

**Image Reconstruction Need (2 marks):**
The raw LOR data is in projection space (sinogram), not in image space. The sinogram must be mathematically inverted to recover the spatial distribution of the tracer. Two main algorithms:
- **FBP** (analytical, fast, noisy at low counts)
- **OSEM** (iterative, statistical, models Poisson noise and physics corrections)

The reconstructed image shows tracer concentration as intensity. Quantification uses the **Standardized Uptake Value (SUV)**:
$$SUV = \frac{\text{Activity in region (Bq/mL)}}{\text{Injected dose (Bq) / Patient body weight (g)}}$$

A high SUV (typically > 2.5) in a suspicious lesion indicates hypermetabolic activity suggestive of malignancy.

**Challenges (1 mark):**
- Attenuation of 511 keV photons in tissue → corrected using **attenuation correction** (CT in PET/CT).
- **Scattered and random coincidences** → degrade contrast and add noise.
- **Positron range** → fundamental physical limit on spatial resolution (~2–4 mm).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Print this checklist and use it for last-minute revision before the KTU exam.

- [ ] **Image Reconstruction** = process of converting projection data (sinogram) into a cross-sectional image.
- [ ] The **Radon transform** mathematically describes how a 2-D object is integrated along straight lines to form 1-D projections.
- [ ] The **Central Slice Theorem** states: 1-D FT of a projection at angle $\theta$ = slice of the 2-D FT of the object at that angle.
- [ ] **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** = (Filter each projection with $\vert \omega \vert$ in frequency domain) + (back-project across the image) + (sum over all angles).
- [ ] The **ramp filter** $H(\omega) = \vert \omega \vert$ compensates for the $1/r$ blur of simple back-projection.
- [ ] **Shepp-Logan filter** = ramp × sinc, used in practice to suppress high-frequency noise.
- [ ] **OSEM** is the standard iterative reconstruction for PET — better than FBP at low count rates.
- [ ] **A-mode** = 1-D amplitude spikes; **B-mode** = 2-D brightness image; **M-mode** = motion over time; **Doppler** = velocity.
- [ ] The **Doppler shift equation**: $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_t v \cos\alpha}{c}$, where $c \approx 1540$ m/s in soft tissue.
- [ ] **Axial resolution** of ultrasound: $\Delta d = c / (2 \Delta f)$ (set by pulse bandwidth).
- [ ] **Reflection coefficient** (intensity): $R = \left[\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right]^2$, where $Z = \rho c$ is acoustic impedance.
- [ ] **PET** uses positron-emitting tracers (¹⁸F-FDG most common; $t_{1/2} = 109.7$ min).
- [ ] **Annihilation photons** = 2 × **511 keV** photons emitted at 180° to each other (from $E = m_0 c^2$).
- [ ] A **Line of Response (LOR)** is defined by a coincidence pair; millions of LORs → sinogram → image.
- [ ] **Coincidence timing window** for PET: typically 6–12 ns.
- [ ] **Attenuation correction** in PET: uses CT data in hybrid PET/CT scanners.
- [ ] **SUV** (Standardized Uptake Value) is the standard PET quantification metric.
- [ ] **Positron range** sets a fundamental limit on PET spatial resolution (~2–4 mm).
- [ ] **Random & scatter coincidences** degrade PET image contrast and add background noise.
- [ ] Total angular span for parallel-beam FBP reconstruction is **180°** ($\pi$ radians).
- [ ] Number of projections needed for sharp reconstruction is at least $\approx$ number of image pixels / 2 (Nyquist criterion).
- [ ] Always **state assumptions** (c = 1540 m/s, parallel beam, etc.) in KTU answers — earns a free mark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** The most-likely question combinations are:
> 1. Image Reconstruction (FBP) with Central Slice Theorem derivation (7+7 split).
> 2. Ultrasound modes table + Doppler equation derivation (7+7 split).
> 3. PET principle with annihilation physics + SUV/quantification (7+7 split).
> Practice all three combinations — they cover >85% of Module 4 marks.
